questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Four diatomic species are listed below in different sequences. Which of these represents the correct order of their increasing bond order?
questions.solution:
According to molecular orbital theory, the energy level of the given molecules are
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_2^{2-} \rightarrow \sigma 1 s^2 \sigma^{\star} 1 s^2 \sigma 2 s^2 \sigma^{\star} 2 s^2 \pi 2 p_x^2 \pi 2 p_y^2 \sigma 2 p_z^2 \\ & \text { B.O. }=1 / 2[10-4]=3 \\ & \mathrm{He}_2^{+} \rightarrow \sigma 1 s^2 \sigma^{\star} 1 s^1 \\ & \text { B.O. }=1 / 2[2-1]=1 / 2=0.5 \\ & \mathrm{NO} \rightarrow \sigma 1 s^2 \sigma^{\star} 1 s^2 \sigma 2 s^2 \sigma^{\star} 2 s^2 \sigma 2 p_z^2 \pi 2 p_x^2 \pi 2 p_y^2 \\ & \quad \pi^{\star} 2 p_x^1\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { B.O. }=1 / 2[10-5]=2.5 \\ & \mathrm{O}_2^{-} \rightarrow \sigma 1 s^2 \sigma^{\star} 1 s^2 \sigma 2 s^2 \sigma^{\star} 2 s^2 \sigma 2 p_z^2 \pi 2 p_x^2 \pi 2 p_y^2 \pi^{\star} 2 p_x^2 \\ & \text { B.O. }=1 / 2[10-7]=1.5 \\ & \pi^{\star} 2 p_y^1 \\ & \end{aligned}$
So, the correct order of their increasing bond order is $\mathrm{He}_2^{+} < \mathrm{O}_2^{-} < \mathrm{NO} < \mathrm{C}_2^{2-}$

If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Assertion is true because usually, for acrylic systems trans-isomers are more stable than cisisomers. This is due to increased unfavourable steric interaction of the substituents in cis-isomer. Reason is also true because generally the dipole moment of trans-form is zero (or less) depending whether the substituents on both sides of double bond are same or not while cis-forms are polar in nature with certain value of dipole moment.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Inert gases are mixed in iodine vapours. Then there are ____ between them.
questions.solution: There are several types of van der Waals attraction : dipole/dipole, dipole/induced-dipole and spontaneous-dipole/induced-dipole.The spontaneousdipole/induced-dipole attractions are also known as London dispersion forces. LDF are surprisingly strong but are only short range forces e.g. surface of neutral molecules or inert gases. All molecules have LDF and strength increases with the size/surface area of the molecule. In case of halogens, \(\mathrm{LDF}\) increases as \(\mathrm{F}_2  < \mathrm{Cl}_2 < \mathrm{Br}_2 < \mathrm{I}_2\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The $\mathrm{ONO}$ angle is maximum in
questions.solution:
The nitrate ion has three resonance structure, with the double bond on a different oxygen for each. In all resonance structures the nitrogen has three bonds, therefore, its hybridisation is $s p^2$. Each of the oxygen atoms also has $s p^2$ hybridisation, since the hybridisation is determined by the resonance structure with the double bond, where the oxygen has a double bond and two lone pairs. The actual geometry of the polyatomic ion is trigonal planar with bond angles of $120^{\circ}$.
$\mathrm{NO}_2^{-}: 18$ electrons; ideal geometry trigonal planar, $s p^2$ with bond angle of $116^{\circ}$.
$\mathrm{NO}_2: 17$ electrons, ideal geometry trigonal planar; $s p^2$ with bond angle of $134^{\circ}$
$\mathrm{NO}_2^{+}: 16$ electrons; ideal geometry linear ; sp with bond angle of $180^{\circ}$.

The highest occupied molecular orbital is of $\pi$-type.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following molecule has highest bond energy?
questions.solution: The bond energies of $\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{F}, \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{N}-\mathrm{N}$ and $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}$ bonds are $33,80,39$ and $34.2 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ respectively. Therefore molecule of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ has the highest bond energy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of $\sigma$ - and $\pi$-bonds present in pent-4-ene-1-yne is
questions.solution:
Pent-4-ene-1-yne
$$
\stackrel{1}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H} \equiv \stackrel{2}{\mathrm{C}}-\stackrel{3}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_2-\stackrel{4}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}=\stackrel{5}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_2
$$
No. of $\sigma$-bonds $-4(\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C})+6(\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H})=10$
No. of $\pi$-bonds $-1(\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C})+2(\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C})=3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The formal oxidation no. of sulphur in $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_4 \mathrm{O}_6$ is 2.5 .
Reason : Two S-atoms are not directly linked with $\mathrm{O}$-atoms.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Formal oxidation number of sulphur $$ =\frac{2 \times 5+2 \times 0}{4}=2.5 $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The length of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bond in benzene is
questions.solution: The bond length $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ (single bond as in ethane) is $1.54 \mathrm{~A}$ and $\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}$ (double bond as in ethene is $1.33 Å$. The carbon carbon bond length in benzene has been found to be $1.39 Å$ which lies between single and double bond length value.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following has the highest dipole moment?
questions.solution: The electronegative oxygen atom is a strong electron withdrawing group. So there is polarity in the carbonyl compounds. This polarity is even greater than in the asymmetric alkene.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of $\sigma$ - and $\pi$-bonds present in pent-4-ene, 1-yne is
questions.solution:
Pent - 4 - en - 1 - yne
No. of $(\sigma)$ bonds $-4(\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C})+6(\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H})=10$
No. of $\pi$ - bonds $-1(\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C})+2(\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{C})=3$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In solvents like DMSO, acetonitrile, $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ion of dissolved $\mathrm{NaF}$ is more reactive than in methyl alcohol becasue
options: $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}$ is more polar than DMSO and $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CN}$
$\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}$ is less polar than DMSO and $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CN}$
unsolvated $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ion is $\mathrm{DMSO}$ or $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CN}$ acts more efficiently as nucleophile
$-\mathrm{OH}$ group is a better leaving group than $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ ion.
questions.solution: In methyl alcohol, $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ion is solvated but in DMSO or $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CN}$ (aprotic solvent) $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ion is not solvated. Unsolvated $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ion acts more efficiently as a nucleophile.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is most stable?
questions.solution: 2-Butene is more stable due to more number of hyperconjugative structures.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The $M-\mathrm{O}-M$ bond angles in $\mathrm{M}_2 \mathrm{O}$ (where $M$ is halogen) is in the order
questions.solution: In $\mathrm{OF}_2$ repulsion between lone pairs is greater than that between bond pair since electrons are away from $\mathrm{O}$ and nearer to $\mathrm{F}$. In $\mathrm{Cl}_2 \mathrm{O}$, bonding electrons are nearer to $\mathrm{O}$ than to $\mathrm{Cl}$, so the bond angle is greater than $109^{\circ} 28^{\prime}$. $\operatorname{In} \mathrm{Br}_2 \mathrm{O}$, the bonding electrons are more closer to oxygen than in $\mathrm{Cl}_2 \mathrm{O}$, so the bond angle is largest $\left(116^{\circ}\right)$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Boiling and melting points of amides are higher than corresponding acids.
Reason : It is due to strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding in their molecules.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Amides are $R-\mathrm{CONH}_2$ Acids are $R-\mathrm{COOH}$ Due to the presence of $-\mathrm{NH}_2$ group in amides there is stronger hydrogen bonding between the amide molecules than between the acid molecules. Some acids are rather present in dimers form due to the presence of intra molecular hydrogen bonding.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Ethers behave sas bases in the presence of mineral acids.
Reason : It is due to the presence of lone pair of electrons on the oxygen.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Due to the presence of lone pair of electrons on oxygen atom, ethers behave as base and form stable oxonium salts with mineral acids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Molecular nitrogen is less reactive than molecular oxygen.
Reason : The bond length of $\mathrm{N}_2$ is shorter than that of oxygen.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true, but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution: Bond order of $\mathrm{N}_2=3$, bond order of $\mathrm{O}_2=2$. Higher the bond order, higher is the bond dissociation energy. i.e. higher stability or less reactivity. Thus $\mathrm{N}_2$ is less reactive than $\mathrm{O}_2$. Higher the bond order, shorter is the bond length. Shorter bond length of $\mathrm{N}_2$ shows its higher bond order.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In $\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]^{-}$, the number of $\pi$ bonds is
questions.solution:
Cyanide is considered, in a broad sense, to be the most potent ligand for many transition metals. The very high affinities of metals for cyanide can be attributed to its negative charge, compactness and ability to engage in $\pi$-bonding. The dicyanides $\left[M(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]^{-}(M=\mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Au})$ are linear in geometry.
$-\mathrm{C} \underset{2 \pi}{\stackrel{1 \sigma}{\equiv}} \mathrm{N}$
Thus, two cyanide ions have $4 \pi$-bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following order is correct?
questions.solution:
(a) Polarising power of cations increases with the increasing charge. $\begin{aligned} & (+1) \quad(+2) \quad(+3) \\ & \mathrm{NaCl} < \mathrm{MgCl}_2 < \mathrm{AlCl}_3 \\ & \end{aligned}$ (b) Bond order $\propto \frac{1}{\text { Bond length }}$ Bond order $=\frac{\text { Bond order of each } \mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O} \text { bond }}{\text { Total no. of resonating structures }}$
Hence, the decreasing order of bond length is $\mathrm{CO}_3^{2-}>\mathrm{HCO}_2^{-}>\mathrm{CO}_2>\mathrm{CO}$. (c) $\mathrm{Cl} \longleftarrow \mathrm{Be} \rightleftarrows \mathrm{Cl}$,
Correct order of dipole moment : $\mathrm{BeCl}_2 < \mathrm{NF}_3 < \mathrm{NH}_3$ (d) Correct order of bond angle : $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{BF}_3>\mathrm{SiH}_4>\mathrm{NH}_3>\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S} \\ & \begin{array}{llll}120^{\circ} & 109^{\circ} 28^{\prime} & 107^{\circ} & 93.3^{\circ}\end{array} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following option w.r.t. increasing bond order is correct ?
questions.solution:
Bond order of the molecules :
$$
\mathrm{O}_2^{-}: K K(\sigma 2 s)^2\left(\sigma_2 *\right)^2\left(\sigma 2 p_{\mathrm{z}}\right)^2\left(\pi 2 p_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^2\left(\pi 2 p_{\mathrm{y}}\right)^2\left(\pi^* 2 p_x\right)^2\left(\pi^* 2 p_y\right)^1
$$


Bond order of $\mathrm{O}_2^{-}=\frac{1}{2}(8-5)=1.5$
$\mathrm{He}_2^{+}:(\sigma 1 s)^2\left(\sigma^* 1 s\right)^1$
Bond order of $\mathrm{He}_2^{+}=\frac{1}{2}(2-1)=0.5$
$$
\mathrm{C}_2: K K(\sigma 2 s)^2\left(\sigma^* 2 s\right)^2\left(\pi 2 p_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^2\left(\pi 2 p_{\mathrm{y}}\right)^2
$$
Bond order $=\frac{1}{2}(6-2)=2.0$
$$
\text { NO : } K K(\sigma 2 s)^2\left(\sigma^* 2 s\right)^2\left(\sigma 2 p_{\mathrm{z}}\right)^2\left(\pi 2^2 p_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^2\left(\pi 2^2 p_{\mathrm{y}}\right)^2\left(\pi * 2 p_{\mathrm{x}}\right)^1
$$
Bond order $=\frac{1}{2}(8-3)=2.5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Hybridisation in $\mathrm{CH}_3, \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_3$ and $\overline{\mathrm{C}}_3$ are respectively
questions.solution:
$\dot{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}_3$ (methyl free radical) has planar structure with $s p^2$ hybridisation of ' $\mathrm{C}$ ' atom. The odd electron is present in unhybridised $2 p_z$ orbital.
$\oplus$
$\mathrm{CH}_3$ (methyl carbonium ion) has also trigonal planar structure $\left(s p^2\right)$.
$\stackrel{\ominus}{\mathrm{CH}_3}$ (methyl carbanion) has tetrahedral structure $\left(s p^3\right)$ and one of the hybrid orbitals contains the lone pair of electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In which of the following $p \pi-d \pi$ bonding is possible?
questions.solution:
Carbon, oxygen and nitrogen atoms do not have any $d$-orbital available. Therefore, in $\mathrm{CO}_3^{2-}, \mathrm{NO}_2^{-}$ and $\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}$, there is no possibility of $p \pi-d \pi$ type of bonding.
In $\mathrm{PO}_4{ }^{3-}$ phosphorus atom has vacant $d$-orbital available which is used to form $p \pi-d \pi$ type of bonding with $p$-orbital of oxygen atom.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The $\mathrm{BCl}_3$ is a planar molecule where as $\mathrm{NCl}_3$ is pyramidal, because
options: $\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond is more polar than $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond
$\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond is more covalent than $\mathrm{B}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond
nitrogen atom is smaller than boron atoms
$\mathrm{BCl}_3$ has no lone pair but $\mathrm{NCl}_3$ has a lone pair of electrons.
questions.solution:
No lone pair of electron is available in $\mathrm{BCl}_3$.
One lone pair of electron is available on N-atoms, it occupies a corner in the tetrahedral arrangement. Therefore, $\mathrm{NCl}_3$ appears pyramidal in shape.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statements about $\mathrm{CO}_3^{2-}$ ion is correct?
options: The formal charge on each oxygen atom is $-0.67$
It has two $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}$ single bonds and one $\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$ double bond.
The hybridization of central atom is $s p^3$.
questions.solution: Bond order $=4 / 3=1.33$ Formal charge $=-2 / 3=-0.67$ All bonds are equivalent due to resonance and hybridisation of central atom is $s p^2$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : $o$-Nitrophenol is more volatile than $p$-nitrophenol.
Reason : Intramolecular hydrogen bonding is present in $o$-nitrophenol while intermolecular $\mathrm{H}$ - bonding is in $p$-nitrophenol.
options: If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
If Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.solution: p-Nitrophenol has higher boiling point than o-nitrophenol, because intermolecular hydrogen bonding present in $p$-nitrophenol is responsible for it. But intramolecular hydrogen bonding in $o$-nitrophenol does not effect boiling point.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : $\mathrm{CHCl}_3$ and $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}$ are miscible.
Reason : One of them is polar.
options: If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
If Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.solution: $\mathrm{CHCl}_3$ and $\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{OH}$ are miscible due to intermolecular van der Waals force of attraction. However, $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}$ and $\mathrm{CHCl}_3$ are polar molecules.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OCH}_3$ and $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH}$ has comparable molecular weight but boiling point of $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH}$ is more than dimethyl ether.
Reason : $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH}$ forms intermolecular Hbonding while $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OCH}_3$ forms intramolecular $\mathrm{H}$-bonding.
options: If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
If Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.solution: Due to the presence of hydroxyl group $(-\mathrm{OH})$, there is extensive hydrogen bonding between the ethanol molecules $\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{OH}\right)$. But there is no such hydrogen bonding in dimethyl ether (due to absence of -OH group). So boiling point of dimethyl ether is much lower than ethanol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ is soluble in water while $\mathrm{BaSO}_4$ is not.
Reason : Lattice energy of $\mathrm{BaSO}_4$ exceeds its hydration energy.
options: If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
If Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.solution: The large sized sulphate anion $\left(\mathrm{SO}_4{ }^{2-}\right)$ is stabilised better by a large sized cation. So the stability of sulphates down the group increases so the lattice energy is more in $\mathrm{BaSO}_4$ than in $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Sigma ( $\sigma$ is a strong bond, while pi $(\pi)$ is a weak bond.
Reason : Atoms rotate freely about pi ( $\pi$ ) bond.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Sigma bonding involves end to end overlapping of the two atomic orbitals e.g. between $s$ and $s, s$ and $p$ and $p$ and $p$ orbitals. Pi bonding $(\pi)$ involves sidewise overlapping of the two atomic orbitals e.g. between $p$ and $p$ orbitals. Overlapping of orbitals is more effective in $\sigma$ bonding than in $\pi$-bonding. Due to the geometry of overlapping or orbitals, rotation of one atom is not
possible around $\pi$-bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following has highest second ionization energy?
questions.solution:
Electronic configuration for chromium is :
$$
{ }_{24} \mathrm{Cr} \rightarrow 1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^5 4 s^1
$$
After ionisation of $4 s$ electron the next electron which will be ionised is from $3 d$ orbital. As $3 d^5$ electronic configuration, is stable, hence, the second ionisation of $\mathrm{Cr}$ will require much greater ionisation energy than the expected one.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Smallest intermolecular distance is found in
questions.solution:
In $\mathrm{O}_2$, the bond order $=2$
In $\mathrm{O}_2^{+}$, the bond order $=2.5$
In $\mathrm{O}_2^{-}$, the bond order $=1.5$
In $\mathrm{O}_2^{-2}$, the bond order $=1$
As the bond order in $\mathrm{O}_2^{+}$is highest, so its internuclear distance is smallest.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : $\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6, \mathrm{Si}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$ are said to have similar structure.
Reason : They have same number of $\sigma$ and $\pi$ bonds .
options: If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
If Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.solution: $\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$ is an electron deficient compound. $\mathrm{B}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$ contain some unusual bonds which are called as 2 -electron 3 -centre bonds.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
One mole of $\mathrm{SO}_3$ was placed in a vessel of $1 \mathrm{~L}$ capacity at a certain temperature and the following equilibrium was established.
$$
2 \mathrm{SO}_{3(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(g)}
$$
If 0.6 moles of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ were formed at equilibrium, $K_c$ for the reaction will be
questions.solution: $$ K_c=\frac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_2\right]^2\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{\left[\mathrm{SO}_3\right]^2}=\frac{(0.6)^2(0.3)}{(0.4)^2}=0.675 $$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A vessel of one litre capacity containing 1 mole of $\mathrm{SO}_3$ is heated till a state of equilibrium is attained.
$2 \mathrm{SO}_{3(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(g)}$
At equilibrium, 0.6 moles of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ had formed. The value of equilibrium constant is
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{SO}_{3(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(g)}$
$\begin{array}{lcl}\text { Inital conc. } 1.0 \text { mole } & 0.0 \text { mole } & 0.0 \text { mole } \\ \text { Equilibrium } 0.4 \text { mole } & 0.6 \text { mole } & 0.3 \text { mole } \\ \Rightarrow \text { Equilibrium constant is given by } \\ K=\frac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_2\right]^2\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{\left[\mathrm{SO}_3\right]^2}=\frac{(0.6)^2(0.3)}{(0.4)^2}=0.675 \approx 0.68\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Solubility of gases increases with increase in pressure.
Reason : Dissolution of gas in liquid is exothermic.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The equilibrium constant for the reaction, $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2(g)}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HI}_{(g)}$ is $K_c$.
Equilibrium constant for the reaction, $2 \mathrm{HI}_{(g)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{I}_{2(g)}$ will be
questions.solution:
The given reaction is, $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2(g)}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HI}_{(g)}$
Hence, $K_c=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]}{\left[\mathrm{H}_2\right]^{1 / 2}\left[\mathrm{I}_2\right]^{1 / 2}}$
Now, reverse the eqn. (i) and multiply by 2 , we get $2 \mathrm{HI} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{I}_{2(g)}$ Hence, $K_c^{\prime}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_2\right]}{[\mathrm{HI}]^2}$
Equating equations (ii) and (iii), we get $K_c^{\prime}=\frac{1}{\left(K_c\right)^2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When pressure is increased at constant temperature
options: solubility of gas in liquid increases
solubility of solid in liquid increases
$2 \mathrm{C}_{(s)}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(g)} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{(g)}$ reaction moves in the forward direction.
questions.solution: Solubility of gas in liquid increases on increasing pressure according to Henry's law.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At $60^{\circ}$ and $1 \mathrm{~atm}, \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ is $50 \%$ dissociated into $\mathrm{NO}_2$ then $K_p$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2$
$\begin{array}{lcc}\text { Initially } & 1 & 0 \\ \text { At equilibrium } & 1-\alpha & 2 \alpha\end{array}$
$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ is $50 \%$ dissociated, so $\alpha=\frac{1}{2}$
$K_p=\frac{p_{\mathrm{NO}_2}^2}{p_{\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4}}=\frac{\left(2 \times \frac{1}{2}\right)^2}{\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)}=2 \mathrm{~atm}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In which of the following reactions $K_p>K_c$ ?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  When } \Delta n_g>0, K_p>K_c \\ & 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \\ & \Delta n_g=(2+1)-2=1 \\ & \text { i.e., } \Delta n_g>0\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The equilibrium constant is fixed and a characteristic for any given chemical reaction at a specified temperature.
Reason: The composition of the final equilibrium mixture at a particular temperature depends upon the starting amount of reactants.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The equilibrium constant is always fixed and is characteristic of a reaction at specified temperature. However final composition of a reaction mixture at equilibrium at constant temperature depends on the initial concentration of reactants and products.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A vessel of one litre capacity containing 1 mole of $\mathrm{SO}_3$ is heated till a state of equilibrium is attained.
$2 \mathrm{SO}_{3(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(g)}$.
At equilibrium, 0.6 moles of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ had formed. The value of equilibrium constant is
questions.solution:
$\quad 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(g)}$
Initial conc. 1.0 mole $\quad 0.0$ mole 0.0 mole
Equilibrium 0.4 mole $\quad 0.6$ mole $\quad 0.3$ mole
$\Rightarrow$ Equilibrium constant is given by :
$K=\frac{\left[\mathrm{SO}_2\right]^2\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{\left[\mathrm{SO}_3\right]^2}=\frac{(0.6)^2(0.3)}{(0.4)^2}=0.675 \approx 0.68$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The equilibrium constant for mutarotation $\alpha$-D Glucose $\rightleftharpoons \beta$-D Glucose is 1.8 . What percentage of $\alpha$ form remains at equilibrium?
questions.solution:
$\alpha$-D glucose $\rightleftharpoons \beta$-D glucose
$\begin{array}{lcc}\text { Initial } & 1 & 0 \\ \text { At equilibrium } & 1-\alpha & \alpha\end{array}$
$K=\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}=1.8$
Solving $\alpha=0.642$
$1-\alpha=0.358$
Percent of $\alpha$-D glucose remaining at equilibrium $=35.8 \%$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The dissociation equilibrium of a gas \(A B_2\) can be represented as
\[
2 A B_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 A B_{(g)}+B_{2(g)}
\]
The degree of dissociation is \(x\) and is small compared to 1 . The expression relating the degree of dissociation \((x)\) with equilibrium constant \(K_p\) and total pressure \(p\) is
questions.solution:
$2 A B_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 A B_{(g)}+B_{2(g)}$
$\begin{array}{lccc}\text { Initial } & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ \text { Equilibrium } & 2(1-x) & 2 x & x\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Moles at equilibrium }=2(1-x)+2 x+x \\ & =2-2 x+2 x+x=x+2 \\ & \begin{aligned} K_p & =\frac{\left[P_{A B}\right]^2\left[P_{B_2}\right]}{\left[P_{A B_2}\right]}=\frac{\left(\frac{2 x}{x+2} \times p\right)^2\left(\frac{x}{2+x} \times p\right)}{\left[\frac{2(1-x)}{x+2} \times p\right]^2} \\ & =\frac{\frac{4 x^3}{x+2} \times p}{4(1-x)^2}=\frac{4 x^2 \times p}{2} \times \frac{1}{4}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$x=\left(\frac{2 K_p}{p}\right)^{1 / 3}$
(as $1-x \approx 1,2+x \approx 2$ )

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Ice $\rightleftharpoons$ water, if pressure is applied water will evaporate.
Reason : Increases of pressure pushes the equilibrium towards the side in which number of gaseous molecule increases.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Increase in pressure favours melting of ice into water because at higher pressure melting point of ice is lowered.
 According to Le-Chatelier's principle, if pressure is increased the equilibrium will shift in direction which produces smaller number of moles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The equilibrium constant is fixed and a characteristic for any given chemical reaction at a specified temperature.
Reason : The composition of the final equilibrium mixture at a particular temperature depends upon the starting amount of reactants.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The equilibrium constant is always fixed and is a characteristic of a reaction at specified temperature. It defines the composition of the final equilibrium mixture of that reaction, regardless of the starting amount of reactants and products.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For reaction, $2 \mathrm{NOCl}_{(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{(g)}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2(g)}$, $K_c$ at $427^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $3 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~mol}{ }^{-1}$. The value of $K_p$ is nearly

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Of the following which change will shift the reaction towards the product?
$\mathrm{I}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{I}_{(\mathrm{g})}, \Delta H_r^{\circ}(298 \mathrm{~K})=+150 \mathrm{~kJ}$
questions.solution: The given reaction is endothermic, so on increasing the temperature, it will shift in forward direction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In which of the following reaction $K_p>K_c$ ?
questions.solution:
$$
\text { } K_p=K_c(R T)^{\Delta n}
$$
$\Delta n=$ change in number of moles
If $\Delta n=$ positive then $K_p$ will be greater than $K_c$.
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{PCl}_3+\mathrm{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{PCl}_5, & \Delta n=1-2=-1 ; K_p < K_c \\
\mathrm{H}_2+\mathrm{I}_2 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HI}, & \Delta n=0 ; \quad K_p=K_c \\
2 \mathrm{SO}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_2+2 \mathrm{SO}_2, & \Delta n=1 ; \quad K_p>K_c \\
\mathrm{~N}_2+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_3 ; & \Delta n=-2 ; K_p < K_c
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction, $\mathrm{H}_2+\mathrm{I}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}, \mathrm{K}=47.6$. If the initial number of moles of each reactant and product is 1 mole then at equilibrium
questions.solution:
For the given reaction, $K=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]^2}{\left[\mathrm{H}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_2\right]}$
As 1 mole of $\mathrm{H}_2$ reacts with 1 mole of $\mathrm{I}_2$, even at equilibrium, $\left[\mathrm{H}_2\right]=\left[\mathrm{I}_2\right]$
Hence, $K=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]^2}{\left[\mathrm{I}_2\right]^2} \quad$ or $\quad \sqrt{K}=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]}{\left[\mathrm{I}_2\right]}=\sqrt{47.6}$ i.e., $[\mathrm{HI}]>\left[\mathrm{I}_2\right]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If for the reaction, $2 \mathrm{ICl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{I}_2+\mathrm{Cl}_2 ; K_c=0.14$ and initial concentration of $\mathrm{ICl}$ is $0.6 \mathrm{M}$ then equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{I}_2$ is
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{ICl} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{I}_2+\mathrm{Cl}_2$
Initial $0.6 \quad 0 \quad 0$
At eqm : $0.6-2 x \quad x \quad x$
$\begin{aligned} & K_c=0.14=\frac{\left[\mathrm{I}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{Cl}_2\right]}{[\mathrm{ICl}]^2}=\frac{x^2}{(0.6-2 x)^2} \\ & 0.37=\frac{x}{0.6-2 x} \Rightarrow 0.222-0.74 x=x \\ & \Rightarrow 1.74 x=0.222 \therefore x=0.128 \mathrm{M}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\frac{K_p}{K_c}$for following reaction will be
questions.solution:
$\Delta_{n_g}=n_p-n_r=1-\frac{3}{2}$
$\Delta_{n_g}=\frac{-1}{2}$. Hence $K_p=K_c(R T)^{-1 / 2}$
$\frac{K_p}{K_c}=\frac{1}{(R T)^{1 / 2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{R T}}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the given reaction : $A+2 B \rightleftharpoons 2 C$ 2 moles each of $A$ and $B$ present in $10 \mathrm{~L}$ of solution combine to form 1 mole of $C$. Calculate $K_c$ for the reaction.
questions.solution:
$A+2 B \rightleftharpoons 2 C$
As 1 mole of $C$ is formed at equilibrium, $2 x=1 \Rightarrow x=0.5$ $\Rightarrow$ Number of moles of $A$ at equilibrium $=1.5, B=1$ and $C=1$ $\therefore$ Equilibrium concentration of $A=1.5 / 10$ $\begin{gathered}B=1 / 10 \text { and } C=1 / 10 \\ K_c=\frac{[C]^2}{[A][B]^2}=\frac{[1 / 10]^2}{[1.5 / 10][1 / 10]^2}=6.67\end{gathered}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not a characteristic of equilibrium?
options: Rate is equal in both directions.
Measurable quantities are constant at equilibrium
Equilibrium occurs only in open vessel at constant temperature.
questions.solution: Equilibrium state can only be achieved if a reversible reaction is carried out in a closed space.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : According to Le-Chatelier's principle addition of heat to an equilibrium solid \(\rightleftharpoons\) liquid results in decrease in the amount of solid.
Reason : Reaction is endothermic, so on heating forward reaction is favoured.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Solid + heat $\rightleftharpoons$ liquid. So on heating forward reaction is favoured and amount of solid will decrease.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the chemical equilibrium,
$$
\mathrm{CaCO}_{3(s)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CaO}_{(s)}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(g)} \text {, }
$$
$\Delta H_r^{\circ}$ can be determined from which one of the following plots?
questions.solution:
For the reaction,
$$
K_p=p_{\mathrm{CO}_2} \text { and } K_c=\left[\mathrm{CO}_2\right]
$$
$\left(\because\left[\mathrm{CaCO}_3\right]=1\right.$ and $[\mathrm{CaO}]=1$ for solids $]$
According to Arrhenius equation we have
$$
K=A e^{-\Delta H_r^{\circ} / R T}
$$
Taking logarithm, we have
$$
\log K_p=\log A-\frac{\Delta H_r^{\circ}}{R T(2.303)}
$$

This is an equation of straight line. When $\log K_p$ is plotted against $1 / T$. we get a straight line.
The intercept of this line $=\log A$, slope $=-\Delta H_r^{\circ} / 2.303 R$
Knowing the value of slope from the plot and universal gas constant $R, \Delta H_r^{\circ}$ can be calculated. (Equation of straight line : $Y=m x+C$. Here,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \log K_p=-\frac{\Delta H_r^{\circ}}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right)+\log A \\
& \begin{array}{llll}
Y & m & x & C
\end{array} \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is a characteristic of a reversible reaction?
options: It can be influenced by a catalyst
It proceeds only in forward direction
Number of moles of reactants and products are equal.
questions.solution: Reversible reactions are those reaction in which there is equilibrium established at every infinitesimal step. But the rate of forward and rate of backward reaction can be influenced (or speed up) by the use of catalyst.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If energy of activation of the reaction is $53.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and the temperature changes from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ then the value of $\left(\frac{k_{37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}{k_{27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}}\right)$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { (c) : Given : } T_1=27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=27+273=300 \mathrm{~K} \\ & T_2=37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=37+273=310 \mathrm{~K} \\ & \text { and } E_a=53.6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \begin{aligned} \log \left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right)=\frac{E_a}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{T_2-T_1}{T_1 T_2}\right) \\ \log \left(\frac{k_{310 \mathrm{~K}}}{k_{300 \mathrm{~K}}}\right)=\frac{53.6 \times 10^3}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left(\frac{310-300}{300 \times 310}\right) \\ =0.3010\end{aligned} \\ & \therefore \frac{k_{310 \mathrm{~K}}}{k_{300 \mathrm{~K}}}=2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Half-life period of a reaction of first order is independent of initial concentration.
Reason : Half-life period for a first order reaction, $t_{1 / 2}=\frac{2.303}{k} \log 2$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The decomposition of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ on platinum surface is zero order reaction. The rates of production of $\mathrm{N}_2$ and $\mathrm{H}_2$ are respectively $\left(k=2.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
For the reaction, $2 \mathrm{NH}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_2+3 \mathrm{H}_2$

From equation (i) and (ii)
$\therefore$ Rate of production of $\mathrm{N}_2, \frac{d\left[\mathrm{~N}_2\right]}{d t}=k$ $=2.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ From equation (iii) Rate of production of $\mathrm{H}_2, \frac{d\left[\mathrm{H}_2\right]}{d t}=3 k$ $\begin{aligned} & =3 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\ & =7.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For a first order gas phase reaction,
$A_{(g)} \longrightarrow 2 B_{(g)}+C_{(g)}$
$P_0$ be initial pressure of $A$ and $P_t$ the total pressure at time ' $t$ '. Integrated rate equation is
questions.solution: Total pressure at time $t=P_0-P+2 P+P=P_t$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow P_t=P_0+2 P \\ & P_t-P_0=2 P \Rightarrow P=\frac{P_t-P_0}{2} \\ & k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \left[\frac{P_0}{P_0-P}\right] \\ & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \left[\frac{P_0}{P_0-\left(\frac{P_t-P_0}{2}\right)}\right] \\ & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \left[\frac{2 P_0}{2 P_0-P_t+P_0}\right]=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \left[\frac{2 P_0}{3 P_0-P_t}\right]\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The chemical reaction, $2 \mathrm{O}_3 \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{O}_2$ proceeds as follows :
Step 1: $\mathrm{O}_3 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{O}_2+\mathrm{O}$ .(fast)
Step $2: \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_3 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{O}_2$ .(slow)
The rate law expression should be
questions.solution:
As the slowest step is the rate determining step, hence from step 2 ,
From step 1, $K_{e q}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right][\mathrm{O}]}{\left[\mathrm{O}_3\right]}$
From eq. (i) and (ii), $r=k K_{e q} \frac{\left[\mathrm{O}_3\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}=k^{\prime}\left[\mathrm{O}_3\right]^2\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]^{-1} \quad\left[\because k^{\prime}=k K_{e q}\right]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the activation energy $(\mathrm{kJ} / \mathrm{mol})$ for a reaction if its rate constant doubles when the temperature is raised from $300 \mathrm{~K}$ to $400 \mathrm{~K}$ ?
$\left(R=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } \log \frac{k_2}{k_1}=\frac{E_a}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right) \\ & \log \frac{2}{1}=\frac{E_a}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left(\frac{1}{300}-\frac{1}{400}\right) \\ & 0.3010=\frac{E_a}{2.303 \times 8.314}\left(\frac{100}{300 \times 400}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow E_a=0.3010 \times 2.303 \times 8.314 \times 3 \times 400 \\ & =6.916 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: During the decomposition of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ to give oxygen, $48 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{O}_2$ is formed per minute at a certain point of time. The rate of formation of water at this point is
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_2$
Rate $=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2\right]}{d t}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right]}{d t}=\frac{d\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{d t}$
Rate of formation of oxygen $=48 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{48}{32} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~min}^{-1}=1.5 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~min}^{-1} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Rate of formation of } \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right]}{d t}=\frac{d\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{d t} \\ & \frac{d\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right]}{d t}=2 \times \frac{d\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{d t}=2 \times 1.5=3 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~min}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A first order reaction, which is $30 \%$ complete in 30 minutes has a half-life period of
questions.solution:
For first order reaction,
$\begin{aligned} & t=\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{a}{a-x} \Rightarrow \frac{t_{30 \%}}{t_{50 \%}}=\frac{\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{100-30}}{\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{100}{100-50}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{30}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{\log \frac{10}{7}}{\log 2} \Rightarrow \frac{30}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{0.1549}{0.3010} \\ & \Rightarrow t_{1 / 2}=\frac{0.3010 \times 30}{0.1549}=58.29 \mathrm{~min}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $75 \%$ of a zero order reaction complete in $4 \mathrm{~h}$ $87.5 \%$ of the same reaction completes in
questions.solution:
Using the relation,
$t_{87.5 \%}=\frac{3}{2} t_{75 \%}$
$t_{87.5 \%}=\frac{3}{2} \times \frac{4}{1}=6 \mathrm{~h}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a decay process ${ }_Z^A X$ changes into ${ }_{Z-1}^A Y$. Which process is this?
questions.solution: ${ }_Z^A X \longrightarrow{ }_{Z-1}^A Y+{ }_{+1}^0 e\left(\right.$ or $\left.\beta^{+}\right)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction $A_{(g)} \longrightarrow B_{(g)}+C_{(g)}$, the rate constant is given as $\left(P_i\right.$ is initial pressure and $P_t$ is pressure at time $t$ )
questions.solution:
For the reaction, $A_{(g)} \longrightarrow B_{(g)}+C_{(g)}$
$\begin{array}{llll}
t=0 & P_i & 0 & 0 \\
t & \left(P_i-x\right) & x & x
\end{array}$
Given: $P_i$ is initial pressure at time, $t=0$ and $P_t$ is pressure at time ' $t$ '.
$P_t=\left(P_i-x\right)+x+x=P_i+x$
$x=P_t-P_i$
Here, $P_A=P_i-x \Rightarrow P_i-\left(P_t-P_i\right)$
$P_A=2 P_i-P_t$
$k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{P_i}{P_A}$
$k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{P_i}{\left(2 P_i-P_t\right)}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For a first order gas phase reaction-
$$
A_{(g)} \rightarrow 2 B_{(g)}+C_{(g)}
$$
$P_0$ be initial pressure of $A$ and $P_t$ the total pressure at time ' $t$ '. Integrated rate equation is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{lccc}
\text {  } & \text { } & A_{(g)} & \longrightarrow 2 B_{(g)}+C_{(g)} \\
\text { Initial }: & P_0 & 0 & 0 \\
& P_0-P & 2 P & P
\end{array}
$$


Total pressure at time $(t)=P_0-P+2 P+P=P_t$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow & P_t=P_0+2 P \\
& P_t-P_0=2 P \Rightarrow P=\frac{P_t-P_0}{2} \\
k= & \frac{2.303}{t} \log \left[\frac{P_0}{P_0-P}\right]=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \left[\frac{P_0}{P_0-\left(\frac{P_t-P_0}{2}\right)}\right] \\
= & \frac{2.303}{t} \log \left(\frac{2 P_0}{2 P_0-P_t+P_0}\right)=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \left(\frac{2 P_0}{3 P_0-P_t}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The plot of a concentration of the reactant versus time for a reaction is a straight line with a negative slope. The reaction follows a
questions.solution: A straight line plot of $[A] v s t$ with negative slope is for a reaction of zero-order.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $t_{1 / 2} v \frac{1}{a^2}$ is a straight line graph then determine the order of reaction.
questions.solution:
For a $n^{\text {th }}$ order reaction,
$t_{1 / 2} \propto \frac{1}{[a]^{n-1}}$
$t_{1 / 2} \propto \frac{1}{[a]^{3-1}} \quad[\therefore n=3]$
$t_{1 / 2} \propto \frac{1}{[a]^2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Two different reactions can never have same rate of reaction.
Reason : Rate of reaction always depends only on frequency of collision and Arrhenius factor.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Two different reactions can have same rate of reaction. Rate of reaction depends upon many factors like nature and concentration of reactants, temperature, catalyst, energy factors, orientation factor, etc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a $1^{\text {st }}$ order reaction if concentration is doubled then rate of reaction becomes
questions.solution: For a first order reaction, rate $=k[A]$, when concentration of $A$ is doubled, the rate becomes double.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a reaction $X \longrightarrow Y$, the graph of the product concentration $(x)$ versus time $(t)$ came out to be a straight line passing through the origin. Hence the graph of $\frac{-d[X]}{d t}$ and time would be
options: straight line with a negative slope and an intercept on $y$-axis
straight line with a positive slope and an intercept on $y$-axis
questions.solution:
If product concentration is $x$.
For a zero order reaction $\frac{x}{t}=k$
Thus graph would be a straight line passing through origin. So the given information is for zero order reaction. For a zero order reaction, rate of the reaction is constant. Thus, plot of rate $v s$ time, i.e., $-\frac{d[X]}{d t} v s$ time will be a straight line parallel to $x$-axis.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The position of an element in periodic table after emission of one $\alpha$ - and two $\beta$-particles remains unchanged.
Reason : Emission of one $\alpha$ - and two $\beta$-particles give isotope of the element which acquires same position in periodic table.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The loss of one $\alpha$-particle will reduce the mass number by four and atomic number by two. Subsequent two $\beta$-emissions will increase the atomic number by two without affecting the mass number. Hence, the new element will be only an isotope of the parent nucleide and hence its position in the periodic table remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a homogenous reaction $A \longrightarrow B+C+D$ the initial pressure was $P_0$ and after time $t$ it was $P$. Expression for rate constant $k$ in terms of $P_0$, $P$ and $t$ will be
questions.solution:
$A \longrightarrow B+C+D$ $\begin{array}{lllll}\text { Initial } & a & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \text { After time } t a-x & x & x & x\end{array}$
From (i), $P_0+2 x=P \quad$ or $\quad x=\frac{P-P_0}{2}$ From rate equation $k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}$ $=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{P_0}{P_0-\left(\frac{P-P_0}{2}\right)}=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{2 P_0}{3 P_0-P}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : In rate law, unlike in the expression for equilibrium constants, the exponents for concentrations do not necessarily match the stoichiometric coefficients.
Reason : It is the mechanism and not the balanced chemical equation for the overall change that governs the reaction rate.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which curve corresponds to the temperature dependence of the rate $R$ of a simple one step reaction?
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution: Slowest step of mechanism decides the rate expression. Thus rate $=k\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{F}_2\right]$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Decay constant of a radioactive substance is \(69.3 \mathrm{~sec}^{-1}\), find \(t_{1 / 16}\) of the same substance.
questions.solution:
\(1 \stackrel{t_{1 / 2}}{\longrightarrow} \frac{1}{2} \stackrel{t_{1 / 2}}{\longrightarrow} \frac{1}{4} \stackrel{t_{1 / 2}}{\longrightarrow} \frac{1}{8} \stackrel{t_{1 / 2}}{\longrightarrow} \frac{1}{16}\)
So, \(t_{1 / 16}=4 t_{1 / 2}\)
\(=4 \times \frac{0.693}{\lambda}=4 \times \frac{0.693}{69.3}=4 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{sec} .\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Find out the time required for $2 / 3^{\text {rd }}$ completion of a first order reaction, if the value of rate constant is $4.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
For a first order reaction,
$\begin{aligned} & k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x} \\ & \therefore \quad t=\frac{2.303}{4.3 \times 10^{-4}} \log \frac{a}{a / 3}=2.55 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The temperature dependence of a reaction is represented by the Arrhenius equation : $\ln k=-\frac{E_a}{R T}+\ln A$ Which among the following is wrong conclusion about the given plot?
options: Reaction with high activation energy is more temperature sensitive than that of low activation energy $\left(E_a\right)$.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which option is valid for a zero order reaction?
questions.solution:
For a zero order reaction, $A=[A]_0-k t$
$t_{1 / 2}=\frac{[A]_0}{2 k} ; t_{3 / 4}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{[A]_0}{2 k} \therefore \frac{t_{3 / 4}}{t_{1 / 2}}=\frac{3}{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The kinetics of the reaction
\(m A+n B+p C \rightarrow m^{\prime} X+n^{\prime} Y+p^{\prime} Z\)
obeys the rate expression as -
\(\frac{d x}{d t}=k[A]^m[B]^n\)
Reason : The rate of reaction does not depend upon the concentration of \(C\).
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Rate expression \(\frac{d x}{d t}=k[A]^m[B]^n\) shows that the total order of reactions is \(m+n+0=m+n\) as the rate of reaction is independent of concentration of \(C\), i.e, the order with respect to \(C\) is zero. This is the reason that \(C\) does not figure in the rate expression.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: X-rays are emitted during
questions.solution: In certain nucleus, the nucleus captures an electron from the \(K\)-shell (being nearest to the nucleus). The vacancy created is filled up with the electron from the higher shells thereby emitting X-rays. As a result of \(K\)-electron capture, a proton in the nucleus is converted into a neutron \(\left(P^{+}+e^{-} \rightarrow n\right)\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The position of an element in periodic table after emission of one \(\alpha\) and two \(\beta\)-particles remains unchanged.
Reason : Emission of one \(\alpha\) and two \(\beta\)-particles give isotope of the element which acquires same position in periodic table.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The loss of one \(\alpha\)-particle will reduce the mass number by four and atomic number by two. Subsequent two \(\beta\)-emissions will increase the atomic number by two without affecting the mass number. Hence, the new element will be only an isotope of the parent nucleide and hence its position in the periodic table remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction, $2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2$ rate of reaction is
questions.solution:
In general for a reaction,
$$
x_1 A+x_2 B \rightarrow y_1 C+y_2 D
$$
The rate expression may be expressed as
$$
-\frac{1}{x_1} \frac{d[A]}{d t}=-\frac{1}{x_2} \frac{d[B]}{d t}=\frac{1}{y_1} \frac{d[C]}{d t}=\frac{1}{y_2} \frac{d[D]}{d t} \text {. }
$$
For $2 \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{O}_2$
$$
-\frac{1}{2} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{d t}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{d t}=\frac{d\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{d t}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An endothermic reaction with high activation energy for the forward reaction is given by the diagram
questions.solution: Endothermic reactions are those which involve absorption of heat. High activation energy means potential energy of product must be much greater than reactants.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For reaction $a A \rightarrow x P$, when $[A]=2.2 \mathrm{mM}$, the rate was found to be $2.4 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{s}$. On reducing concentration of $A$ to half, the rate changes to $0.6 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$. The order of reaction with respect to $A$ is
questions.solution:
$a A \rightarrow x P$
Rate of reaction $=[A]^a$
Order of reaction $=a$.
$[A]_1=2.2 \mathrm{mM}, r_1=2.4 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{M} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$
$[A]_2=\frac{2.2}{2} \mathrm{mM}, r_2=0.6 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ or, $\frac{2.4}{4}$
On reducing the concentration of $A$ to half, the rate of reaction is decreased by four times.
Rate of reaction $=[A]^2$
Order of reaction $=2$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Hydrogen nuclei combines to form helium nuclei then energy is released.
Reason : Binding energy/nucleon of He is greater than hydrogen.
options: If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
If Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.solution:
Anuclear reaction in which two lighter nuclei are fused together to form a heavier nuclei is called nuclear fusion. In such a process, more stable nuclei come into existence i.e., binding energy per nucleon increases. Fusionreactionsarehighlyexothermicbecause of loss of mass during fusion. e.g.
$$
{ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}+{ }_1^2 \mathrm{H} \rightarrow{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}+24.9 \mathrm{MeV}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Assertion : Rate of reaction doubles when concentration of reactant is doubled if it is a first order reaction.Reason : Rate constant also doubles.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
For first order reaction, $\operatorname{Rate}_1=k\left[A_1\right]$
According to question, $\left[A_2\right]=\left[2 A_1\right]$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \text { Rate }_2=k\left[2 A_1\right] \\
& \Rightarrow \text { Rate }_2=2 \text { Rate }_1
\end{aligned}$
For a given reaction, rate constant is constant and independent of the concentration of reactant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Acid hydrolysis of sucrose is a
questions.solution:
It is an example of pseudo unimolecular reaction where molecularity $\geq 2$ but order of reaction is one.
Rate $=k[$ Sugar $]\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right]$
In the reaction, water is present in excess and its concentration is taken to be constant, thus the reaction becomes independent of $\left[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right]$ and is pseudo first order.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : According to transition state theory, for the formation of an activated complex, one of the vibrational degrees of freedom is converted into a translational degree of freedom.
Reason : Energy of the activated complex is higher than the energy of reactant molecules.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true, but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution:
$: A+B \rightleftharpoons(A B)^{\#} \rightarrow$ products.
The activated complex can also be treated as a distinct chemical species in equilibrium with the reactants which then decompose into products. It is, however, a special molecule in which one vibrational degree of freedom has been converted to a translational degree of freedom along the reaction coordinate. It must be remembered that the activated complex is not merely an intermediate in process of breaking or forming of chemical bonds. It is unstable because it is situated at the maximum of the potential energy barrier separating the produts from the reactants. The difference between the energy of the activated complex $\left(E_1\right)$ and the energy of the reactants $\left(E_2\right)$ is the activation energy, $E_a$.
$$
E_1-E_2=E_a, E_1>>E_2 .
$$

$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For reaction $a A \rightarrow x P$, when $[A]=2.2 \mathrm{mM}$, the rate was found to be $2.4 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$. On reducing concentration of $A$ to half, the rate changes to $0.6 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$. The order of reaction with respect to $A$ is
questions.solution:
$a A \rightarrow x P$
Rate of reaction $=[A]^a$
Order of reaction $=a$
$[A]_1=2.2 \mathrm{mM}, r_1=2.4 \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$
$[A]_2=\frac{2.2}{2} \mathrm{mM}, r_2=0.6$ or $\frac{2.4}{4} \mathrm{mM} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$
On reducing the concentration of $A$ to half, the rate of reaction is decreased by four times.
$\therefore$ Rate of reaction $\propto[A]^2$
$\Rightarrow$ Order of reaction $=2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q:
Assertion : $E^{\circ}$ for $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+} / \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ is more positive than $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+} / \mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$.
Reason : The third ionization energy of $\mathrm{Mn}$ is larger than that of $\mathrm{Cr}$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true, but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution:
It is known that the value of $E^{\circ}$ becomes more negative down the series. As the chromium is present below in electrochemical series than $\mathrm{Mn}$, so $\mathrm{Mn}$ has more positive $E^{\circ}$ value than $\mathrm{Cr}$.
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\mathrm{Mn} \rightarrow 3 d^5 4 s^2, & \mathrm{Mn}^{2+} \rightarrow 3 d^5 4 s^0 \\
\mathrm{Cr} \rightarrow 3 d^5 4 s^1, & \mathrm{Cr}^{2+} \rightarrow 3 d^4 4 s^0
\end{array}
$$
For elimination of one electron from $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$, more energy is required due to its stable configuration. That is why third ionization energy of $\mathrm{Mn}$ is larger than $\mathrm{Cr}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q:
Assertion : First ionization energy for nitrogen is lower than oxygen.
Reason : Across a period effective nuclear charge decreases.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
First ionization energy for nitrogen is greater than oxygen.
$$
\begin{array}{ccc} 
& \mathrm{N} & \mathrm{O} \\
\mathrm{eV} & 14.5 & 13.6
\end{array}
$$
This is due to stable configuration of nitrogen (halffilled $2 p$-orbital).
Due to screening effect the valency electron experiences less attraction towards the nucleus. This brings decrease in the nuclear charge $(Z)$ actually present on the nucleus. The reduced nuclear charge is termed effective nuclear charge and its magnitude increases in a period when we move from left to right.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: An element $(X)$ belongs to fourth period and fifteenth group of the periodic table. Which one of the following is true regarding the outer electronic configuration of $(X)$ ? It has
options: partially filled $d$ orbitals and completely filled $s$ orbital
completely filled $s$ orbital and completely filled $p$ orbitals
completely filled $s$ orbital and half-filled $p$ orbitals
half-filled $d$ orbitals and completely filled $s$ orbital.
questions.solution:
The electronic configuration of $(X)$ can be written as
$X=1 s^2 2 s^2 2 p^6 3 s^2 3 p^6 4 s^2 3 d^{10} 4 p^3$
So, element $(X)$ has completely filled $s$ and $d$ orbitals and half filled $p$ orbitals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Correct increasing order for the wavelength of absorption in the visible region for the complexes of $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ is
questions.solution:
Strength of ligands attached with $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ ion is in the order of $\mathrm{CN}^{-}>\mathrm{NH}_3>\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$. So, order of crystal field splitting energy (values of $\Delta_o$ ) will be in the same order.
$\therefore \quad$ Wavelength of absorbing light $\propto \frac{1}{\Delta_o}$

Due to high electronegativity and electron withdrawing nature of $\mathrm{F}$-atom, the lone pair of $\mathrm{N}$-atom in $\mathrm{NF}_3$ molecule is not easily available for donation. Whereas in $\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_3,-\mathrm{CH}_3$ group is an electron releasing group, thus lone pair of $\mathrm{N}$-atom in $\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_3$ molecule can be ligated easily.

questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{CN}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{CN})_2 \downarrow$
$\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{CN})_2 \downarrow+2 \mathrm{CN}^{-} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$
$\left[\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$ is colourless compound and not too stable. When hydrogen sulphide gas is added, cadmium sulphide is precipitated.
$\left[\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CdS} \downarrow+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{CN}^{-}$
But in case of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$,
$\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{CN}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{CN})_2 \downarrow$
$2 \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{CN})_2 \downarrow \frac{\text { Quickly }}{\text { decomposes }} 2 \mathrm{CuCN} \downarrow+(\mathrm{CN})_2 \uparrow$
$\mathrm{CuCN} \downarrow+3 \mathrm{CN}^{-} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{3-}$
This complex is so stable (i.e., $\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{+}\right]$is too low) that $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ cannot precipitate $\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{I})$ sulphide $\left(\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{~S}\right)$

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{Fe}^{+}$has 3 unpaired electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following can be reduced easily?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{CO})_6$ can be easily reduced to $\left[\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{CO})_6\right]^{-}$. $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{CO})_6$ has a total of 17 bonding electrons, hence it is very reactive and unstable. $\left[\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{CO})_6\right]$ on the other hand has complete set of 18 bonding electrons as an electron is added into the bonding orbital when $\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{CO})_6$ gets reduced to $\left[\mathrm{V}(\mathrm{CO})_6\right]^{-}$.
All others have 18 bonding electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Wavelength of red light is absorbed by the complex
questions.solution:
When \(\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}\) is added to the solution containing \(\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\) ions, deep blue solution is obtained.
\(\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH} \rightarrow\underset{\text{deep blue solution}}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right]^{2+}}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\)
Hydrated cupric compounds absorb radiations corresponding to red light and the transmitted colour is greenish blue (which is complementary to red colour). Thus, cupric compounds give greenish-blue colour.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The pair in which both species have same magnetic moment (spin only value) is
questions.solution:
Spin only magnetic moment, $\mu_s=\sqrt{n(n+2)}$ where $n=$ number of unpaired electrons. Number of unpaired electrons in $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}\left([\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^4\right)$ is 4 , in $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}\left([\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^7\right)$ is 3 , in $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\left([\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^6\right)$ is 4 , in $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\left([\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^5\right)$ is 5 .
As the number of unpaired electrons in $\mathrm{Cr}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ are same, hence $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$ will have same magnetic moment.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : \(\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}\) bond in metal carbonyl is long.
Reason : There is delocalisation of electrons from filled \(d\) orbitals into the empty orbitals on the \(\mathrm{CO}\) ligands.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: \(\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{O}\) bond in metal carbonyl is long as ligated \(\mathrm{CO}\) has lower bond order. The lower B.O. is due to the transfer of metal \(d \pi\) electrons into the \(\pi^*\) orbitals of ligated \(\mathrm{CO}\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Among the following, the species having square planar geometry for central atom are (i) $\mathrm{XeF}_4$, (ii) $\mathrm{SF}_4$, (iii) $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$, (iv) $\left[\mathrm{PdCl}_4\right]^{2-}$
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{XeF}_4: s p^3 d^2$ hybridisation, shape is square planar instead of octahedral due to presence of two lone pair of electrons on $\mathrm{Xe}$ atom. $\mathrm{SF}_4: \mathrm{SF}_4$ molecule shows $s p^3 d$ hybridisation but its expected trigonal bipyramidal geometry gets distorted due to presence of a lone pair of electrons and it becomes distorted tetrahedral or see-saw with the bond angles equal to $89^{\circ}$ and $177^{\circ}$ instead of the expected angles of $90^{\circ}$ and $180^{\circ}$ respectively.

Experiments have, however, shown that 4coordinated complexes of $\mathrm{Pd}$ (II) are diamagnetic $(n=0)$. Thus it is $d s p^2$ hybridisation which is involved in the formation of $\left[\mathrm{PdCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ ion i.e. 4coordinated complexes of Pd(II) have square planar geometry with $n=0$ (diamagnetic).

Reason : Crystal field splitting in ferrocyanide ion is greater than that of ferricyanide ion.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Ferrocyanide ion :
In ferrocyanide ion, the oxidation state of $\mathrm{Fe}$ is +2 however in ferricyanide ion, the oxidation number of $\mathrm{Fe}$ is +3 . Generally, the higher the oxidation state of the metal, the greater the crystal field splitting. It means crystal field splitting in ferrocyanide ion is lower than that of ferricyanide ion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following does not have optical isomer?
questions.solution:
Optical isomerism is very common in octahedral complexes of general formulae, $\left[M a_2 b_2 c_2\right]^{n \pm},[M a b c d e f],\left[M(A A)_3\right]^{n \pm},\left[M(A A)_2 a_2\right]^{n \pm}$, $\left[M(A A) a_2 b_2\right]^{n \pm},\left[M(A A)_2 a b\right]^{n \pm}$ and $\left[M(A B)_3\right]^{n \pm}$, where $A A$ is symmetrical bidentate ligand and $A B$ is unsymmetrical bidentate ligand.
Octahedral complex of the type $M a_4 b_2$ and $M a_3 b_3$ exhibit geometrical isomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Assertion : The $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\text { en })_3\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$ (en = ethylene diamine) has lower stability than $\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$. Reason : In $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{en})_3\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$ the geometry of $\mathrm{Ni}$ is trigonal bipyramidal.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{en})_3\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2$ is a chelating compound and chelated complexes are more stable than similar complexes with unidentate ligands as dissociation of the complex involves breaking of two bonds rather than one. In $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{en})_3\right] \mathrm{Cl}_2, \mathrm{Ni}$ with $d^8$ configuration shows octahedral geometry. Six electrons will occupy the $t_{2 g}$ orbitals and two electrons will occupy the $e_g$ orbitals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The complex used as an anti-cancer agent is
questions.solution: Cis-[ $\left[\mathrm{PtCl}_2\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_2\right]$ is used as an anti-cancer agent, called as cisplatin and is highly toxic. On injecting, the more reactive $\mathrm{Cl}$ groups are lost so the Pt atom bonds to a $\mathrm{N}$ atom in guanosine (part of the DNA molecule). Those cells which are undergoing cell division are attacked by cisplatin.

options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^5$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Mn}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^4 \\
& \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^5
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^6 \text {, }
$$
Thus, $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ has more stable configuration than $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+}$ while $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ has more stable configuration than $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$. Hence, reduction potential for $\mathrm{Mn}^{3+} / \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$ couple is more positive than $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+} / \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry

Assertion : $\mathrm{Yb}^{2+}$ is more stable as compare to $\mathrm{Gd}^{2+}$.
Reason : The electronic configuration of Gd is [ $\mathrm{Xe}] 4 f^7 5 d^2 6 s^2$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text {  } \mathrm{Yb}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Xe}] 4 f^{14} 5 d^0 6 s^0 \\ & \mathrm{Gd}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Xe}] 4 f^7 5 d^1 6 s^0\end{aligned}$
Because of fully filled $4 f$ orbital, $\mathrm{Yb}^{2+}$ is more stable than $\mathrm{Gd}^{2+}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Transition metals are poor reducing agents .
Reason : Transition metals form numerous alloys with other metals.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: In actual practice transition metals react with acid very slowly and act as poor reducing agents. This is due to the protection of metal as a result of formation of thin oxide protective film. Further, their poor tendency as reducing agent is due to high ionisation energy, high heat of vapourization and low heat of hydration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The degree of complex formation in actinides decreases in the order $M^{4+}>\mathrm{MO}_2{ }^{2+}>M^{3+}>\mathrm{MO}_2{ }^{+}$.
Reason : Actinides form complexes with p-bonding ligands such as alkyl phosphines and thioethers.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
The higher the charge on the metal ion, smaller is the ionic size and more is the complex forming ability. Thus, the degree of complex formation decreases in the order
$$
M^{4+}>M_2^{2+}>M^{3+}>M \mathrm{O}_2^{+}
$$
The higher tendency of complex formation of $\mathrm{MO}_2^{2+}$ as compared to $M^{3+}$ is due to high concentration of charge on metal atom $M$ in $\mathrm{MO}_2^{2+}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The colour imparted by Co(II) compounds to glass is
questions.solution: Glass is an amorphous and transparent or translucent solid obtained by solidification of a mixture of silicates of differentmetals, one of which is always an alkali metal. Addition of small amounts of transition metal salts to the glass mixture imparts colour to the glass. For example, addition of small amounts of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}, \mathrm{Co}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Mn}^{4+}$ salts imparts yellow-brown, light blue, deep blue, green or violet colour respectively.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Cuprous ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{+}\right)$is colourless whereas cupric ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{++}\right)$is blue in the aqueous solution.
Reason : Cuprous ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{+}\right.$) has unpaired electrons while cupric ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{++}\right.$) does not.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Cuprous ions $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{+}\right)$is colourless because it has no unpaired electrons in the $3 d$ sub-shell as it has $3 d^{10}$ configuration. The cupric ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{++}\right)$has one unpaired electron as it has configuration $3 d^9$. By absorbing the visible light, the unpaired electron can migrate from one set of $d$-orbitals to another under the applied field of water molecules. Hence, cupric ion $\left(\mathrm{Cu}^{++}\right)$in aqueous solution shows blue colour.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The compound insoluble in water is
questions.solution:
According to "solubility rule" following compounds are soluble in water:
(i) All common group IA and $\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}$compounds.
(ii) $\mathrm{All}$ common $\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}$(nitrate), $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COO}^{-}$(acetate), $\mathrm{ClO}_4^{-}$(perchlorate) compounds
(iii) $\mathrm{All}$ common $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{Br}^{-}, \mathrm{I}^{-}$compounds, except those of $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{Pb}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cu}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Hg}_2^{2+}$ (mercurous)
(iv) All common $\mathrm{SO}_4{ }^{2-}$ (sulphate) compounds, except those of $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}, \mathrm{Sr}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Solution of $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4$ in water is coloured.
Reason: Oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cr}$ in $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4$ is +6 .
options: If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4$ solution in water is yellow in colour, when acidified changes to orange colour (due to the formation of $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$ ions).
$$
\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \rightarrow \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7+\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
$$
Oxidation state : $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4$
Let oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cr}$ be $x$.
$$
\Rightarrow 2(+1)+x+4(-2)=0 \Rightarrow x=+6 \text {. }
$$

How many Faradays of electricity are required for the given reaction to occur?
$\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$
questions.solution:
$\stackrel{+7}{\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$
The reaction can be written as :

Hence, 5 moles of electrons or $5 \mathrm{~F}$ of electricity are required for the given reaction.
