appropriate molar conductances of the electrolytes listed in the given table
at infinite dilution in $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \Lambda_{\mathrm{HOAc}}^{\infty} & =\Lambda_{\mathrm{NaOAc}}^{\infty}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{HCl}}^{\infty}-\Lambda_{\mathrm{NaCl}}^{\infty} \\ & =(91.0+426.2-126.5) \mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & =390.7 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solution is present in a conductivity cell with electrode of $100 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ area placed $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ apart and resistance observed is $5 \times 10^3 \mathrm{ohm}$. What is the molar conductivity of this solution?
questions.solution:
Molar conductivity, $\Lambda_m=\frac{\kappa \times 1000}{M}$
$M=$ molarity and $\kappa=$ specific conductivity
$\begin{aligned} \kappa & =\frac{1}{R} \times \text { cell constant } \\ & =\frac{1}{R} \times \frac{\ell}{A}=\frac{1}{5 \times 10^3 \Omega} \times \frac{1 \mathrm{~cm}}{100 \mathrm{~cm}^2} \\ & =2 \times 10^{-6} \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \\ \therefore \quad \Lambda_m & =\frac{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1000}{0.1}=0.02 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Calculate emf of the following cell at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ if value of $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ is $4 \mathrm{~V}$ (Given $R T / F \ln 10=0.06$ ) :
$M\left|M_{(0.01 \mathrm{M})}^{2+}\right|\left|M_{(0.0001 \mathrm{M})}^{2+}\right| M$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } E_{\text {cell }}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-\frac{R T}{2 F} \ln \frac{0.01}{0.0001} \\ & =4-\frac{R T}{2 F} \ln \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-4}}=4-\frac{R T}{2 F} \ln 10^2 \\ & =4-\frac{R T}{F} \ln 10=4-0.06=3.94 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Time taken to completely decompose $36 \mathrm{~g}$ of water by passing $3 \mathrm{~A}$ current is
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2+\mathrm{O}_2$
$36 \mathrm{~g}$
2 moles of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \equiv 4$ moles of $e^{-} \mathrm{s}$
$4 \mathrm{~F}$ of charge is required for complete decomposition.
Now, $Q=I \times t$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad t=\frac{Q}{I}=\frac{4 \times 96500}{3} & =128666 \mathrm{sec} \\ & =\frac{128666}{3600}=35.8 \mathrm{hr}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Electrode potential of hydrogen electrode is $18 \mathrm{mV}$, then $\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$is
questions.solution:
Given : $E_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_2}=18 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V},\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=?$
Applying Nernst equation,
$E_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_2}=E_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_2}^{\circ}-\frac{0.0591}{1} \log \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}$
$18 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V}=0+0.0591 \log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$
$18 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V}=0.0591 \log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$
$\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=0.3046$
$\therefore \quad\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\operatorname{antilog}(0.3046)=2.02=2.0$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : A reaction is spontaneous if $E_{\text {cell }}=+$ ve.
Reason : For $E_{\text {cell }}=+\mathrm{ve}, \Delta G$ is always -ve.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
For a redox reaction to be spontaneous, the EMF of the cell must be positive.
$$
\text { As, }-\Delta G=n F E_{\text {cell }}
$$
Therefore, for $E_{\text {cell }}=+$ ve, $\Delta G$ is always negative.

questions.q: For the electrochemical cell, $M\left|M^{+} \| X^{-}\right| X$, $E^{\circ}\left(M^{+} \mid M\right)=0.44 \mathrm{~V}$ and $E^{\circ}\left(X \mid X^{-}\right)=0.33 \mathrm{~V}$. From this data one can deduce that

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The specific conductance of a N/10 KCl at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $0.0112 \mathrm{ohm}^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. The resistance of cell containing solution at the same temperature was found to be $55 \mathrm{ohm}$. The cell constant will be
questions.solution: We know that specific conductance $=$ Cell constant $\times$ conductance $\Rightarrow$ Cell constant $=$ Resistance $\times$ sp. conductance $=55 \times 0.0112=0.616 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Hydrofluoric acid is a weak acid. At $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the molar conductivity of $0.002 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HF}$ is $176.2 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. If its $\Lambda_m^{\circ}=405.2 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. Equilibrium constant at the given concentration is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \alpha=\frac{\Lambda_c}{\Lambda_m^{\circ}}=\frac{176.2 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{405.2 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}=0.435 \\ K & =\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{F}^{-1}\right]}{[\mathrm{HF}]}=\frac{C \alpha^2}{1-\alpha}=\frac{(0.002 \mathrm{M})(0.435)^2}{1-0.435}=6.7 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M} \\ & =6.70 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The time period to coat a metal surface of \(80 \mathrm{~cm}^2\) with \(5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~cm}\) thick layer of silver (density \(1.05 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}\) ) with the passage of \(3 \mathrm{~A}\) current through a silver nitrate solution is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  Weight of Ag required }=V \times d \\ & =80 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.05=0.42 \\ & \because \quad W=\frac{\text { Eit }}{96500} \\ & \therefore \quad 0.42=\frac{108 \times 3 \times t}{96500} \Rightarrow t=125 \mathrm{sec} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the following concentration cell, to be spontaneous \(\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right) P_1\) atm. \(|\mathrm{HCl}| \mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right) P_2\) atm. Which of the following is correct?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right) \mathrm{P}_1$ atm. $|\mathrm{HCl}| \mathrm{Pt}\left(\mathrm{H}_2\right) \mathrm{P}_2$ atm.
For spontaneous reaction, $E_{\text {cell }}$ should be positive so $P_1>P_2$
$E_{\text {cell }}=\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{P_1}{P_2}$
Also, if $P_1>P_2$ oxidation occurs at L.H.S. electrode and reduction occurs at R.H.S. electrode.

questions.subject: Chemistry
Chemistry
questions.q: To observe the effect of concentration on the conductivity of electrolytes of different nature were taken in two vessels \(A\) and \(B . A\) contains weak electrolyte \(\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}\) and \(B\) contains strong electrolyte \(\mathrm{NaCl}\). In both containers, concentration of respective electrolyte was increased and conductivity observed
options: in \(A\) conductivity increases, in \(B\) conductivity decreases
in \(A\) conductivity decreases, while in \(B\) conductivity increases
questions.solution: Solution which contains large number of ions compared to another solution of same concentration at the same temperature has more conductance and is said to be stronger electrolyte. The one which has relatively small number of ions is called weak electrolyte. If concentration of respective electrolyte was increased, the equivalent conductance decreased in each case, but rapid decrease in equivalent conductance is seen in weak electrolyte.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: On the basis of standard electrode potential of redox couples given below, find out which of the following is the strongest oxidising agent. $\left(E^{\circ}\right.$ values $: \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\left|\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}=+0.77 \mathrm{~V} ; \mathrm{I}_{2(s)}\right| \mathrm{I}^{-}=$ $\left.+0.54 \mathrm{~V} ; \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\left|\mathrm{Cu}=+0.34 \mathrm{~V} ; \mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right| \mathrm{Ag}=+0.80 \mathrm{~V}\right)$
questions.solution:
Higher the electrode potential, better is the oxidising agent. Since, the electrode
 potential decreases in the order :
$\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\left|\mathrm{Ag}(+0.80 \mathrm{~V})>\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}\right| \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(+0.77 \mathrm{~V})>$
$\mathrm{I}_{2(s)}\left|\mathrm{I}^{-}(+0.54 \mathrm{~V})>\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right| \mathrm{Cu}(+0.34 \mathrm{~V})$
Hence, $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$is the strongest oxidising agent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A conductivity cell has a cell constant of $0.5 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. This cell when filled with $0.01 \mathrm{M}$ $\mathrm{NaCl}$ solution has a resistance of $384 \mathrm{ohms}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Calculate the equivalent conductance of the given solution.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  Equivalent conductance, } \Lambda_{e q}=\frac{\kappa \times 1000}{\text { Normality }} \\ & \text { where, } \kappa \text { (Specific conductance) }=C \times \frac{l}{a} \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{R} \times \frac{l}{a}=\frac{1}{384} \times 0.5 \\ & \quad=1.302 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{ohm}^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1} \\ & \therefore \quad \Lambda_{e q}=\frac{1.302 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000}{0.01} \\ & \quad=130.2 \mathrm{ohm}^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2(\mathrm{~g} \mathrm{eq})^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Copper metal gets readily corroded in acidic aqueous solution.
Reason : Free energy change for this process is negative.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Non-oxidising acids $\left(\mathrm{HCl}\right.$ and dil. $\left.\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4\right)$ do not have any effect on copper. However they dissolve the metal in presence of air. As it is a nonspontaneous process so, $\Delta G$ can not be $-\mathrm{ve}$.

If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Electropositive elements like zinc precipitate copper from a solution of copper sulphate and finally forms a complex.
\(\mathrm{CuSO}_4+\mathrm{Zn} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{ZnSO}_{4(a q)} \rightarrow\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_4\right] \mathrm{SO}_4\)

questions.solution: $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+3 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{MnO}_2+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$ Charge required for the reduction of $1 \mathrm{~mol}_{\text {of } \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}}$ to $\mathrm{MnO}_2=3 \mathrm{~F}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : For the Daniel cell, $\mathrm{Zn}\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} \| \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right| \mathrm{Cu}$ with $E_{\text {cell }}=1.1 \mathrm{~V}$, the application of opposite potential greater than $1.1 \mathrm{~V}$ results into flow of electron from cathode to anode.
Reason : $\mathrm{Zn}$ is deposited at anode and $\mathrm{Cu}$ is dissolved at cathode.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true, but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution:
If the opposing potential becomes slightly larger than that of the cell, the direction of current flow is reversed, and so is the cell reaction. Now zine ions are converted to zine at one electrode and $\mathrm{Cu}$ is converted into copper ion and the overall cell reaction becomes
$$
\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+\mathrm{Cu} \rightarrow \mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following statements is true for the electrochemical Daniell cell?
options: Electrons flow from copper electrode to zinc electrode.
Current flows from zinc electrode to copper electrode.
questions.solution: In Daniell cell, during the passage of electric current through external circuit, electrons flow from the zinc electrode to the copper electrode. At the zinc electrode, the zinc metal is oxidised to $\mathrm{Zn}^{++}$which go into the solution. The electrons released at the electrode travel through the external circuit to the copper electrode where they are used in the reduction of $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ ions to metallic copper which is deposited on the electrode.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The chemical reaction,
$$
2 \mathrm{AgCl}_{(s)}+\mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}_{(a q)}+2 \mathrm{Ag}_{(s)}
$$
taking place in a galvanic cell is represented by the notation
questions.solution:
$2 \mathrm{AgCl}_{(s)}+\mathrm{H}_{2(g)} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HCl}_{(a q)}+2 \mathrm{Ag}_{(s)}$ The activities of solids and liquids are taken as unity and at low concentrations, the activity of a solute is approximated to its molarity.
The cell reaction will be
$$
\mathrm{Pt}_{(s)} \mid \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}, 1 \text { bar }\left|\mathrm{H}_{(a q)}^{+} 1 \mathrm{M} \| \mathrm{AgCl}_{(a q)} 1 \mathrm{M}\right| \mathrm{Ag}_{(s)}
$$

If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Ag}^{+}+e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}$
For deposition of $\mathrm{Ag}, 1$ mole of electrons is required while for $\mathrm{Cu}, 2$ moles of electrons are required. So quantity of electricity needed for depositing is different.

questions.q: Time required to deposit one millimole of aluminium metal by the passage of 9.65 amperes through aqueous solution of aluminium ion is
questions.solution:
$ \mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Al}$
For deposition of $1 \mathrm{~g}$ equivalent $\equiv 96500 \mathrm{C}$
For deposition of 1 millimole $\equiv 96.500 \mathrm{C}$
As $Q=I t \Rightarrow t=\frac{Q}{I}=\frac{96.5}{9.65}=10 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many electrons are delivered at the cathode during electrolysis by a current of $1 \mathrm{~A}$ in 60 seconds?
questions.solution:
Charge $=$ current $\times$ time
Charge, $Q=1 \mathrm{~A} \times 60 \mathrm{sec}$
$=60$ coulumb
Now,
96500 C charge is carried by $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ electron
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow 60 \mathrm{C} \text { charge } & =\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23}}{96500} \times 60 \text { electrons } \\
& =3.74 \times 10^{20} \text { electrons }
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A factory produces $40 \mathrm{~kg}$ of calcium in two hours by electrolysis. How much aluminium can be produced by same current in 2 hours if current efficiency is $50 \%$ ?
questions.solution:
$\frac{W_{\mathrm{Ca}}}{E_{\mathrm{Ca}}}=\frac{W_{\mathrm{Al}}}{E_{\mathrm{Al}}} \Rightarrow \frac{40}{20}=\frac{W_{\mathrm{Al}}}{9} \Rightarrow W_{\mathrm{Al}}=18 \mathrm{~kg}$
As current efficiency is $50 \% \mathrm{so}, W_{\mathrm{Al}}=9 \mathrm{~kg}$

questions.solution: Copper corrodes at negligible rate in unpolluted air, water and deaerated non-oxidising acids. questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The products formed when an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{NaBr}$ is electrolysed in a cell having inert electrodes are
questions.solution:
$: \mathrm{NaBr} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{Br}^{-}$
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$
At cathode : $2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+2 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_2$
At anode : $2 \mathrm{Br}^{-}-2 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Br}_2$
In solution : $\mathrm{Na}^{+}+\mathrm{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaOH}$
As discharge potential of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions is lower than that of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions and discharge potential of $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$ ions is lower than that of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions, $\mathrm{H}_2$ is liberated at cathode and $\mathrm{Br}_2$ is liberated at anode.

options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Zinc metal which has a more negative electrode potential than iron will provide electrons in preference to the iron, and therefore corrode first. Only when all the zinc has been oxidised does the iron start to rust.

Therefore, number of moles of electrons required to deposite $1 \mathrm{gm}$ equivelent of aluminium is three.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Cell equation : $A+2 B^{+} \longrightarrow A^{2+}+2 B$ $A^{2+}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow A ; E^{\circ}=+0.34 \mathrm{~V}$ and $\log _{10} K=15.6$ at $300 \mathrm{~K}$ for cell reactions.
Find $E^{\circ}$ for $B^{+}+e^{-} \longrightarrow B$.
$\left[\right.$ Given $: \frac{2.303 R T}{n F}=0.059$ at $\left.300 \mathrm{~K}\right]$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text {  } E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\frac{0.059}{2} \log K \\ & E_{B^{+} / B}^{\circ}-E_{A^{2+} / A}^{\circ}=\frac{0.059}{2} \log K\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & E_{B^{+} / B}^{\circ}-0.34 \mathrm{~V}=\frac{0.059}{2} \times 15.6 \\ & E_{B^{+} / B}^{\circ}=0.46+0.34=0.80 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

van-Arkel process

questions.q:
Assertion : Extraction of iron metal from iron oxide ore is carried out by heating with coke. 
Reason : The reaction $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_{3(s)} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_{(s)}+3 / 2 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ is a spontaneous process.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Extraction of iron metal from iron oxide ore is carried out by heating with coke and flux (calcium carbonate). Flux is a slag forming substance. It converts infusible impurities into fusible slag.
The reaction : $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_{3(s)} \rightarrow \mathrm{Fe}_{(s)}+3 / 2 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ is not a spontaneous process. $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ is converted to $\mathrm{FeO}$ at about $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C} . \mathrm{FeO}$ is converted to $\mathrm{Fe}$ at about $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}-1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate the overall complex dissociation equilibrium constant for the $\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right]^{2+}$ ions, given that stability constant $\left(\beta_4\right)$ for this complex is $2.1 \times 10^{13}$.
questions.solution:
Dissociation constant is the reciprocal of the stability constant $(\beta=1 / K)$.
Overall complex dissociation equilibrium
constant, $\begin{aligned} K & =\frac{1}{\beta_4} \\ & =\frac{1}{2.1 \times 10^{13}}=4.76 \times 10^{-14}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{ZnO}$ is white when cold and yellow when heated. It is due to the development of
questions.solution:

$\mathrm{NaHCO}_3+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate ionisation constant for pyridinium hydrogen chloride. (Given that $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion concentration is $3.6 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{M}$ and its concentration is $0.02 \mathrm{M}$.)
questions.solution:
Pyridinium hydrochloride is a salt of weak base and strong acid.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=-\log \left(3.6 \times 10^{-4}\right)=3.44 \\ & \text { Now, } \mathrm{pH}=-\frac{1}{2}\left[\log K_w-\log K_a+\log C\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 3.44=-\frac{1}{2}\left[-14-\log K_a+\log \left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)\right] \\ & \Rightarrow 6.88=14+\log K_a+1.70 \\ & \Rightarrow \log K_a=-8.82 \\ & \Rightarrow K_a=\operatorname{antilog}(-8.82)=1.5 \times 10^{-9}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $K_a$ for $\mathrm{HCN}$ is $5 \times 10^{-10}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. For maintaining a constant $\mathrm{pH}=9$, the volume of $5 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{KCN}$ solution required to be added to $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $2 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCN}$ solution is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{pH}=\mathrm{p} K_a+\log \frac{[\text { Salt }]}{[\text { Acid }]}$
Let the volume of $\mathrm{KCN}$ solution required be $V \mathrm{~mL}$ $\therefore \quad[\mathrm{KCN}]=\frac{5 \times V}{V+10}$ and $[\mathrm{HCN}]=\frac{10 \times 2}{V+10}$ Now from eqn. (i), $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left(5 \times 10^{-10}\right)+\log \left[\frac{5 \times V}{V+10} / \frac{10 \times 2}{V+10}\right] \\ & 9=-\log \left(5 \times 10^{-10}\right)+\log \frac{V}{4}\end{aligned}$ On solving, $V=1.99 \approx 2 \mathrm{~mL}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ is stronger acid than $\mathrm{PH}_3$.
Reason : $S$ is more electronegative than $\mathrm{P}$, conjugate base $\mathrm{HS}^{-}$is more stable than $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{P}^{-}$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}_{(g)}+a q \longrightarrow \mathrm{HS}_{(a q)}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{(a q)}^{+} \\ & \mathrm{PH}_{3(g)}+a q \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{P}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{(a q)}^{+}\end{aligned}$
As $\mathrm{S}$ is more electronegative than $\mathrm{P}$, conjugate base $\mathrm{HS}^{-}$is more stable than $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{P}^{-}$. Hence, $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ is stronger acid than $\mathrm{PH}_3$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When $50 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NH}_3$ is mixed with $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}$ then what is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of resultant solution? $\left(\mathrm{p} K_b=4.75\right)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } \quad \mathrm{NH}_3+\mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl} \\ & \text { Initial } 50 \times 0.1 \quad 10 \times 0.1 \\ & =5 \mathrm{mmol} \quad=1 \mathrm{mmol} \\ & \text { After } \quad 4 \mathrm{mmol} \quad 0 \mathrm{mmol} \quad 1 \mathrm{mmol} \\ & \text { reaction } \\ & \mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{p} K_b+\log \frac{[\text { salt }]}{[\text { base }]} \\ & =4.75+\log \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)=4.15 \\ & \mathrm{pH}=14-\mathrm{pOH}=14-4.15=9.85 \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $K_{s p}$ of $\mathrm{CaSO}_4 \cdot 5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is $9 \times 10^{-6}$, find the volume for $1 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CaSO}_4$ (M.wt. = 136).
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { } \mathrm{CaSO}_{4(s)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}{ }_{(a q)}+\mathrm{SO}_4{ }^{2-}{ }_{(a q)} \\
& S \\
& K_{s p}=S^2=9 \times 10^{-6} \\
& S=3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$


Solubility in $\mathrm{g}$ litre $^{-1}=$ molecular mass $\times S$
$$
=136 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}=408 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}
$$
$408 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CaSO}_4$ present in 1 litre
$1 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CaSO}_4$ present is $\frac{1}{408 \times 10^{-3}}=2.45$ litre

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which has the highest $\mathrm{pH}$ ?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}$ solution is acidic, its $\mathrm{pH} < 7 . \mathrm{NaNO}_3$ solution is neutral, its $\mathrm{pH}=7 . \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOK}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$ solutions are basic their $\mathrm{pH}>7$. But $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3$ solution is more basic, its $\mathrm{pH}>\mathrm{pH}$ of $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOK}$ solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of $0.01 \mathrm{M}$ glycine solution? For glycine $K_{a_1}=4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ and $K_{a_2}=1.7 \times 10^{-10}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$.
questions.solution:
Glycine $\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{~N}^{+} \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{COO}^{-}$is more acidic than basic. Instead of $K_b$ value, the second given $K_a$ value is corresponding to the $K_b$ value $\left(\because K_a \times K_b=10^{-14}\right)$. Hence, overall ionization constant,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& K=K_{a_1} \times K_{a_2}=4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.7 \times 10^{-10} \\
& =7.65 \times 10^{-13} \\
& \mathrm{H}^{+}=\sqrt{K C}=\sqrt{7.65 \times 10^{-13} \times 0.01} \\
& =\sqrt{0.765 \times 10^{-14}}=0.87 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{M} \\
& \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left(0.87 \times 10^{-7}\right)=7.06 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{pH}$ of a $0.01 \mathrm{M}$ solution $\left(K_a=6.6 \times 10^{-4}\right)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  } \alpha=\sqrt{\frac{K_a}{C}}=\sqrt{\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-4}}{0.01}}=\sqrt{6.6 \times 10^{-2}}=0.257 \\ & {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\mathrm{C} \alpha=0.01 \times 0.257=2.57 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & \mathrm{pH}=3-\log 2.5=2.60\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $25 \mathrm{~mL}, 0.2 \mathrm{MCa}(\mathrm{OH})_2$ is neutralised by $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $1 \mathrm{MHCl}$. Then $\mathrm{pH}$ of resulting solution is
questions.solution:
Number of millimoles of base (i.e., $\left.\mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_2\right)$ $=N_1 V_1=2 \times M_1 \times V_1=2 \times 0.2 \times 25=10$
Number of millimoles of acid (i.e., $\mathrm{HCl})=\mathrm{N}_2 V_2$
$$
=10 \times 1=10
$$
As, no. of millimoles of acid $=$ no. of millimoles of base
$\therefore$ Acid is completely neutralised by base forming a neutral solution.
$\therefore \mathrm{pH}$ of the resulting solution $=7$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $40 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ ammonia solution is mixed with $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{MHCl}$. What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of the mixture? ( $\mathrm{p} K_b$ of ammonia solution is 4.74)
questions.solution:
$40 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ ammonia solution $=40 \times 0.1$ $=4$ milliequivalent ammonia solution $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}=20 \times 0.1$ $=2$ milli equivalent of $\mathrm{HCl}$

$$
\begin{array}{lccc} 
& \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}+ & \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
\text { Initial milli-eqv. } & 4 & 2 & 0 \\
\text { Milli-eqv. after } & 4-2 & 0 & 2 \\
\text { reaction } & =2 & &
\end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{gathered}
\therefore \mathrm{pOH}=\mathrm{pK} K_b+\log \frac{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}\right]}=4.74+\log \frac{2}{2} \\
=4.74+\log 1=4.74 \\
\therefore \mathrm{pH}=14-4.74=9.26
\end{gathered}
$$

options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true, but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false statements.

$\mathrm{Sb}_2 \mathrm{~S}_3$ is soluble in yellow ammonium sulphide.
$$
\mathrm{Sb}_2 \mathrm{~S}_3+3\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_2 \mathrm{~S} \rightarrow 2\left(\mathrm{NH}_4\right)_3 \mathrm{SbS}_3
$$
The stability of sulphides increases with increase in atomic number of the element.

questions.q: When $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ acetic acid $\left(\mathrm{p} K_a=5.0\right)$ is titrated against $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ ammonia solution ( $\mathrm{p} K_b=5.0$ ), the equivalence point occurs at $\mathrm{pH}$
questions.solution:
$: \mathrm{p} K_a=-\log K_a, \mathrm{p} K_b=-\log K_b$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{pH} & =-\frac{1}{2}\left[\log K_a+\log K_w-\log K_b\right] \\
& =-\frac{1}{2}\left[5 \log \left(1 \times 10^{-14}\right)-(5)\right] \\
& =-\frac{1}{2}[5-14-5]=-\frac{1}{2}(-14)=7
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Dimethyl glyoxime gives a red precipitate with $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$, which is used for its detection. To get this precipitate readily the best $\mathrm{pH}$ range is
questions.solution:
Nickel(II) forms a precipitate with the organic compound dimethylglyoxime, $\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_6(\mathrm{NOH})_2$. The formation of the red chelate occurs quantitatively in a solution in which the $\mathrm{pH}$ is buffered in the range of 5 to 9 . The chelation reaction that occurs is illustrated below.
The reaction is performed in a solution buffered by either an ammonia or citrate buffer to prevent the $\mathrm{pH}$ of the solution from falling below 5 . If the $\mathrm{pH}$ does become too low the equilibrium of the above reaction favors the formation of nickel(II) ion, causing the dissolution of $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{DMG})_2$ back into the mother liquor.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When the temperature of reactions will increase then the effect on $\mathrm{pH}$ value will
questions.solution: As the temperature increases, the dissociation of water in solution increases. Hence the $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ion conc. increases and $\mathrm{pH}$ of the solution decreases with increase in temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is strongest Bronsted base?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{HClO}_4$ is the strongest acid among $\mathrm{HClO}$, $\mathrm{HClO}_2, \mathrm{HClO}_3$ and $\mathrm{HClO}_4$ as more the number of oxygen atoms attached, more is the acidity of the molecule order of acidity is :
$$
\mathrm{HClO} < \mathrm{HClO}_2 < \mathrm{HClO}_3 < \mathrm{HClO}_4
$$
So the conjugate base order will be just the reverse:
$$
\mathrm{ClO}^{-}>\mathrm{ClO}_2^{-}>\mathrm{ClO}_3^{-}>\mathrm{ClO}_4^{-}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The standard half-cell reduction potential for $\mathrm{Ag}^{+} \mid \mathrm{Ag}$ is $0.7991 \mathrm{~V}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Given the experimental value $K_{s p}=1.56 \times 10^{-10}$ for $\mathrm{AgCl}$, calculate the standard half-cell reduction potential for the $\mathrm{Ag} \mid \mathrm{AgCl}$ electrode.
questions.solution:
For the desired reaction,
$$
\mathrm{AgCl}+1 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \quad E^{\circ}=\frac{-\Delta_r G^{\circ}}{(1) F}
$$
The needed $\Delta_r G^{\circ}$ can be obtained by adding the values of $\Delta_r G^{\circ}$ for the reactions
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+1 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag} ; \Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ} \\
& \mathrm{AgCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{Ag}^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} ; \Delta G^{\circ}=-R T \ln K_{s p}, \text { giving } \\
& \Delta_r G^{\circ}=-(1 \mathrm{~mol})\left(9.648 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}{ }^{-1} \mathrm{~V}^{-1}\right)(0.7991 \mathrm{~V}) \\
& \quad-\left(8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)(298 \mathrm{~K}) \ln \left(1.56 \times 10^{-10}\right) \\
& =-77.10 \mathrm{~kJ}+55.95 \mathrm{~kJ}=-21.15 \mathrm{~kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$
The potential is $E^{\circ}=\frac{-(-21.15)}{(1)(96.485)}=+0.2192 \mathrm{~V}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Assertion : Relative strength of acids can be known by knowing the value of dissociation constant. Reason : It gives the value of $\mathrm{H}^{+}$dissolved in solution.
options: If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
If Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.solution:
Dissociation consant for :
$$
\mathrm{H} A \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+A^{-}
$$
Can be given as :
$$
K_s=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{H} A]}
$$
Therefore, we can say that dissociation constant is a measure of its ability to furnish protons in the solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: During titration of acetic acid with aq. \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) solution, the neutralisation graph has a vertical line. This line indicates
questions.solution:
In any type of acid-base titration, there is a sudden change in the \(\mathrm{pH}\) value at the end point. During the titration of a weak acid with a strong base, beyond the equivalent point, the solution will contain the salt and excess of free base.
\(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \underset{\text{Sodium acetate}}{\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COONa}}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\)
Due to the presence of free base, solution becomes alkaline in nature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the $\mathrm{pH}$ of $0.01 \mathrm{M}$ glycine solution? For glycine, $K_{a_1}=4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ and $K_{a_2}=1.7 \times 10^{-10}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ ?
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text {  } K=K a_1 \times K a_2=4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.7 \times 10^{-10} \\
& =7.65 \times 10^{-13} \\
& {\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\sqrt{K C}=\sqrt{7.65 \times 10^{-13} \times 0.01}} \\
& =\sqrt{7.65 \times 10^{-15}}=0.87 \times 10^{-7} \\
& \mathrm{pH}=-\log 0.87 \times 10^{-7}=7-0.93=6.07 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following is not a buffer solution?
questions.solution:
Buffer solutions can be obtained by mixing a weak acid with its salt formed with a strong base or by mixing a weak base with its salt formed with a strong acid.
As $\mathrm{HClO}_4$ is a strong acid, therefore equimolar mixture of $\mathrm{HClO}_4$ and its salt $\mathrm{KClO}_4$ is not a buffer solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solution with $\mathrm{pH}=2$ is more acidic than one with a $\mathrm{pH}=6$, by a factor of
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text {  } \mathrm{pH}=-\log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{pH}=2 \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \text {for } \mathrm{pH}=2 \text { is } 10^{-2} \\
& \mathrm{pH}=6 \Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right] \text {for } \mathrm{pH}=6 \text { is } 10^{-6} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-6}}=10^4=10,000 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.q: The solubility of $\mathrm{CuBr}$ is $2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The $K_{s p}$ value for $\mathrm{CuBr}$ is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{CuBr} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{+}+\mathrm{Br}^{-}$
Solubility of $\mathrm{CuBr}$ is $2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
Therefore, solubility of $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$
$\Rightarrow$ Solubility product $=2 \times 10^{-4} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}$
$=4 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~mol}^2 \mathrm{~L}^{-2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Hydrides of group-16 elements show volatility in the order:
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}>\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Se}>\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Te}$
Reason : Electronegativity of group-16 elements increases down the group from oxygen to tellurium.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Hydrides of group-16 elements show volatility in the order :
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}>\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Se}>\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Te}>\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Oxygen is most electronegative than other elements of group-16, it undergoes hydrogen bonding. Due to strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding in $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$, it shows lowest volatility.
As the size of the atom increases regularly from sulphur to tellurium, van der Waals' forces of attraction increase and hence volatility decreases from $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$ to $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Te}$.

questions.solution: A mixture of $2 \% \mathrm{O}_2$ and $98 \%$ He is used in diving apparatus which gives the same partial pressure of $\mathrm{O}_2$ as in normal air at $1 \mathrm{~atm}$. Due to high partial pressure and greater solubility, $\mathrm{N}_2$ gets dissolved in blood and form bubbles (bends or decompression sickness). Hence, $\mathrm{N}_2$ is replaced by helium which is much less soluble in biological fluids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The true statement for the acids of phosphorus, $\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_2, \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_3$ and $\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_4$ is
options: the order of their acidity is $\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_4>\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_3>\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_2$
all of them are reducing in nature
all of them are tribasic acids
the geometry of phosphorus is tetrahedra in all the three.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_2, \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_3$ and $\mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_4$ contain one, two and three ionisable hydrogen atoms respectively. $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_2^{-} \\ & \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_3^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HPO}_3^{2-} \\ & \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{PO}_4 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{PO}_4^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HPO}_4^{2-} \\ & \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{PO}_4^{3-} \\ & \end{aligned}$ But there is very little difference in acidity.
As $\mathrm{P}$ is $s p^3$ hybridised in all therefore all are tetrahedral.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the correct relationship between the \(\mathrm{pH}\) of isomolar solutions of sodium oxide \(\left(\mathrm{pH}_1\right)\), sodium sulphide \(\left(\mathrm{pH}_2\right)\), sodium selenide \(\left(\mathrm{pH}_3\right)\) and sodium telluride \(\left(\mathrm{pH}_4\right)\) ?
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { I } & \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH} \\ \text { II } & \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S} \\ \text { III } & \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Se}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Se} \\ \text { IV } & \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{Te}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NaOH}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Te}\end{array}$
I will have the highest $\mathrm{pH}$. Among $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}, \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Se}$, $\mathrm{H}_2$ Te acidity goes on increasing on going down the group as bond length increases on increasing size of central atom. So, $\mathrm{pH}_4 < \mathrm{pH}_3 < \mathrm{pH}_2$ Overall order is $\mathrm{pH}_1>\mathrm{pH}_2>\mathrm{pH}_3>\mathrm{pH}_4$

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.solution: The bond strength $\mathrm{H}-X$ decreases from $\mathrm{HF}$ to HI. Thus HF is the most stable and hence weakest acid while is least stable and most acidic. In aqueous solutions, $\mathrm{HF}$ is only slightly ionised but $\mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{HBr}$ and HI are almost completely ionised. As basicity is the reverse of acidity. Therefore, $\mathrm{HF}$ is the most basic of all $\mathrm{H}-X$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : $\mathrm{HClO}_4$ is a stronger acid than $\mathrm{HClO}_3$.
Reason : Oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cl}$ in $\mathrm{HClO}_4$ is +8 and in $\mathrm{HClO}_3+5$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Greater the number of negative atoms present in the oxy-acid make the acid stronger.
In general, the strengths of acids that have general formula, $(\mathrm{HO})_m \mathrm{ZO}_n$ can be related to the value of $n$. As the value of $n$ increases, acidic character also increases. The negative atoms draw electrons away from the Z-atom and make it more positive. The Zatom, therefore, becomes more effective in withdrawing electron density away from the oxygen atom that bonded to hydrogen. In turn, the electrons of $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{O}$ bond are drawn more strongly away from the $H$-atom. The net effect makes it easier from the proton release and increases the acid strength.

questions.solution: Nylon 6, 6 and dacron are the examples of condensation polymers. Condensation polymers are formed by condensation of two or more bifunctional monomers with the elimination of simple molecules like $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{NH}_3$, alcohol, etc. Dacron is formed from ethylene glycol and terephthalic acid with the removal of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$. questions.solution:
$\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution is taken in a conical flask with phenolphthalein indicator (pink colour) and $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOCH}_3+\mathrm{HCl}$ solution is added from burette slowly.
First, $\mathrm{HCl}$ left after hydrolysis of ester (being a strong acid) gets consumed then $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}$ being a weak acid will bring the titration to the neutralisation point.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOCH}_3 \frac{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}{\mathrm{H}^{+}} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH} \\ & \mathrm{HCl}+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ & \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}+\mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COONa}\end{aligned}$

questions.solution:
With $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7, \mathrm{SO}_2$ works as reducing agent.
$\begin{aligned} \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+3 \mathrm{SO}_2 \longrightarrow & \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+ \\ & \mathrm{Cr}_2\left(\mathrm{SO}_4\right)_3+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
With water the reaction is not a redox reaction,
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{SO}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_3 \\ & 2 \mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{SO}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{MgO}+\mathrm{S} \\ & 3 \mathrm{Mg}+\mathrm{SO}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{MgO}+\mathrm{MgS}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion: The correct order of oxidising power is : $\mathrm{VO}_2^{+} < \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-} < \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}$
Reason: The oxidation state of $\mathrm{Mn}$ is +7 .
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
The oxidation states of the given compounds are
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{VO}_2^{+}: x+2(-2)=+1 \\ & \Rightarrow x=+5 \\ & \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}: 2 x+7(-2)=-2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 x=+12 \\ & \Rightarrow x=+6 \\ & \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}: x+4(-2)=-1 \\ & \quad x=-1+8=+7\end{aligned}$
The correct order of oxidising power is $\mathrm{VO}_2^{+} < \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-} < \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}$
due to the increasing stability of the lower species to which they are reduced.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $2.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of the carbonate of a metal was treated with $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$. After the completion of the reaction, the solution was boiled off to expel $\mathrm{CO}_2$ and was then titrated against $1 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{NaOH}$ solution. The volume of alkali that would be consumed, if the equivalent weight of the metal is 20
questions.solution:
Equivalent weight of metal carbonate $=20+30=50$
$2.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of metal carbonate $=\frac{2.5}{50}=0.05 \mathrm{eq}$.
Number of equivalent of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ would have reacted $=0.05$
Number of equivalent of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ taken
$=\frac{100 \times 1}{1000}=0.1$
Number of equivalent of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ remains unreacted
$=0.1-0.05=0.05 \mathrm{eq}$.
$\therefore$ Number of equivalent of alkali consumed $=0.05 \mathrm{eq}$.
milli eq. $=$ Normality $\times$ Volume in $\mathrm{mL}$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 1.0 \times V=0.05 \times 1000 \\ & V=\frac{0.05 \times 1000}{1.0}=50 \mathrm{~mL}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In the balanced chemical reaction
$$
\mathrm{IO}_3^{-}+a \mathrm{I}^{-}+b \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow c \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+d \mathrm{I}_2
$$
$a, b, c$ and $d$ respectively corresponds to
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text {  }: \mathrm{IO}_3^{-}+a \mathrm{I}^{-}+b \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow c \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+d \mathrm{I}_2 \\
& \text { Step } 1: \mathrm{I}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_2 \text { (oxidation) } \\
& \quad \mathrm{IO}_3^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_2 \text { (reduction) } \\
& \text { Step } 2: 2 \mathrm{IO}_3^{-}+12 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_2+6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
& \text { Step } 3: 2 \mathrm{IO}_3^{-}+12 \mathrm{H}^{+}+10 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_2+6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
& \quad 2 \mathrm{I}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_2+2 e^{-} \\
& \text {Step } 4: 2 \mathrm{IO}_3^{-}+12 \mathrm{H}^{+}+10 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_2+6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
& \quad\left[2 \mathrm{I}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_2+2 e\right] \times 5
\end{aligned}
$$


$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Step } 5: 2 \mathrm{IO}_3^{-}+10 \mathrm{I}^{-}+12 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{I}_2+6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
& \mathrm{IO}_3^{-}+5 \mathrm{I}^{-}+6 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{I}_2+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}
\end{aligned}
$$
On comparing, $a=5, b=6, c=3, d=3$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : In the iodometric titration starch is used as an indicator.
Reason : Starch is a polysaccharide.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true, but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution: Starch $\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{O}_5\right)_n$ is a polysaccharide. It consists of two fractions - one is known as $\alpha$ amylose and the other is $\beta$-amylose or amylopectin. In iodimetric and iodometric titrations, starch solution is used as an indicator. It gives blue or violet colour with free iodine. At the end point the blue or violet colour disappears when iodine is completely changed to iodide.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : In some cases oxygen shows positive oxidation number though it is an electronegative element.
Reason : Fluorine is more electronegative than oxygen.
options: If both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
If Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.solution:
Oxygen is the most electronegative element after fluorine. Therefore, in the compounds between oxygen and fluorine, oxygen is found to show positive oxidation state.
e.g. $\mathrm{OF}_2$ : Oxygen difluoride.
Oxidation state of oxygen here is +2 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For which of the following elements it is difficult to disproportionate in +3 oxidation state?
questions.solution: In nitrogen family, due to inert pair effect as the tendency to show +3 oxidation state increases down the group from As to $\mathrm{Bi}$, tendency to disproportionate decreases.

questions.q: $\mathrm{ZnO}$ is white when cold and yellow when heated. It is due to the development of
questions.solution: $\mathrm{ZnO}$ turns yellow on heating as $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ ions move in interstitial sites and electrons also get entrapped in nearby interstitial sites to maintain electrical neutrality. As extra $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ ions are present in interstitial sites thus, it is metal excess defect.

questions.q: The number of tetrahedral voids present in 0.5 mole of $h c p$ crystal structure are
questions.solution:

Tetrahedral voids present in $0.5 \mathrm{~mol}=12 \times 0.5 \mathrm{~mol}$ $=6 N_A=6 \times 6.02 \times 10^{23}=3.6 \times 10^{24}$

no. of $B$ atoms $=\frac{1}{3} \times 12=4$
Hence, the formula of the compound is $A_6 B_4$ or $A_3 B_2$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An element germanium crystallises in $b c c$ type crystal structure with edge of unit cell equal to $288 \mathrm{pm}$ and the density of the element equal to $7.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. The number of atoms present in $52 \mathrm{~g}$ of the crystalline element and the atomic mass of the element are respectively
questions.solution:
$d=7.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}, a=288 \mathrm{pm}$ and mass of element $=52 \mathrm{~g}$
Hence, $d=\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { Volume }} \Rightarrow 7.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}=\frac{52 \mathrm{~g}}{V}$
$V=\frac{52 \mathrm{~g}}{7.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}}=7.222 \mathrm{~cm}^3$
Edge of unit cell, $a=288 \mathrm{pm}=288 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}$ $=288 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~cm}=2.88 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}$
Volume of unit cell, $a^3=\left(2.88 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~cm}\right)^3$ $=23.888 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~cm}^3$
The number of unit cells in $52 \mathrm{~g}$ of metal
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\text { Total volume }}{\text { Volume of one unit cell }} \\ & =\frac{7.222 \mathrm{~cm}^3}{23.888 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~cm}^3}=0.3023 \times 10^{24}\end{aligned}$
Germanium has body centred cubic structure. Hence, its unit cell contains 2 atoms.
$\therefore$ Total atoms in $3.023 \times 10^{23}$ unit cells are $2 \times 3.023 \times 10^{23}=6.046 \times 10^{23}$ atoms
Molar mass $=\frac{52 \mathrm{~g} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text { atom } \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{6.046 \times 10^{23} \text { atom }}$
Hence, atomic mass $=51.8$

Reason : Doping means introduction of small amount of impurities like $\mathrm{P}$, As or Bi into the pure crystal.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Ifboth assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: When a silicon crystal is doped with a group-15 element like P, As, Sb or Bi, the silicon atoms at some lattice sites are substituted by atoms of group-15 element. Asthese atoms have 5 electrons in the valence shell, after forming normal four covalent bonds with silicon atoms, the extra fifth electron is free and gets delocalized and hence, increases the conductivity of silicon.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $A$ forms $h c p$ lattice and $B$ are occupying $1 / 3^{\text {rd }}$ of tetrahedral voids, then the formula of compound is
questions.solution:
Let no. of atoms of $A$ used in close packing$=n$
Number of tetrahedral voids $=2 n$
Number of atoms of $B=\frac{1}{3} \times 2 n=\frac{2}{3} n$
$A: B=n: \frac{2}{3} n=3: 2$
Formula of the compound $=A_3 B_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a solid, atom $M$ occupies ccp lattice and $1 / 3^{\text {rd }}$ of tetrahedral voids are occupied by atom $N$. Find the formula of solid formed by $M$ and $N$.
questions.solution: Suppose number of $M$ atoms $=n$ Then number of tetrahedral sites $=2 n$ Number of $N$ atoms $=\frac{1}{3}(2 n)$ Ratio $M: N=n: \frac{2}{3} n=3: 2$, i.e., formula is $M_3 N_2$

Assertion: The presence of a large number of Schottky defects in $\mathrm{NaCl}$ lowers its density.
Reason : In $\mathrm{NaCl}$, there are approximately $10^6$ Schottky pairs per $\mathrm{cm}^3$ at room temperature.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: When an atom or an ion is missing from its normal lattice site, a lattice vacancy or defect is created, which is called Schottky defect. Due to missing density of crystal will be lowered.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Schottky defect is
questions.solution: In Schottky defect, some of the lattice points are unoccupied (vacancies or holes). The number of missing cations and anions is the same, thus crystal remains neutral.

questions.solution: In a Frenkel defect an ion leaves its position in the lattice and occupies normally vacant interstitial position.

questions.solution: This defect arises when a compound has excess metal ion. If a negative ion is absent from its lattice site leaving a hole occupied by electron. The holes occupied by electrons are called F- centres. Greater the number of F-centres greater is the intensity of colour.

questions.solution: The number of nearest neighbour with which a given sphere is in contact is called coordination number. The C.N. in \(h c p\) and \(c c p\) arrangement is 12 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Formula of metal oxide with metal deficiency defect in its crystal is $A_{0.8} \mathrm{O}$. The crystal contains $A^{2+}$ and $A^{3+}$ ions. The fraction of metal existing as $A^{2+}$ ions in the crystal is
questions.solution:
$A: \mathrm{O}=0.8: 1=80: 100$
Thus, if there are $100 \mathrm{O}$ atoms, then number of $A$ atoms $=80$
Charge on $100 \mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions $=100 \times(-2)=-200$
Suppose number of $A$ atoms present as $A^{2+}=x$
$\therefore \quad$ Number of $\mathrm{A}$ atoms present as $A^{3+}=80-x$
Total charge on $A^{2+}$ and $A^{3+}=x \times(+2)$
$+(80-x) \times(+3)$
$=2 x+240-3 x=240-x$
As metal oxide is neutral,
Total charge on cations $=$ total charge on anions
$\therefore \quad 240-x=200$ or $x=40$
$\therefore \quad$ Fraction of metal existing as $A^{2+}$ ions in the crystal $=40 / 80=0.5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The energy gaps $\left(E_g\right)$ between valence band and conduction band for diamond, silicon and germanium are in the order
questions.solution:
Semiconductors are solids where there is only a small difference in energy, called a 'band gap', between the filled valence band of electrons and a conduction band.
$\begin{array}{lc}\text { Compound } & \left.\text { Energy gap (kJ } \mathbf{J} \mathbf{~ m o l}^{-\mathbf{1}}\right) \\ \text { Diamond } & 579 \\ \text { Silicon } & 106 \\ \text { Germanium } & 68\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Graphite is an example of tetragonal crystal system.
Reason : For a tetragonal system, $a=b \neq c$, $\alpha=\beta=90^{\circ}, \gamma=120^{\circ}$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true, but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution:
Graphite has a two dimensional sheet structure. Each $\mathrm{C}$-atom is in $s p^2$ hybridised state and is linked to three other carbon atoms in a hexagonal planar structure.
For a tetragonal system, $a=b \neq c, \alpha=\beta=\gamma=90^{\circ}$. For a hexagonal system, $a=b \neq c, \alpha=\beta=90^{\circ}$, $\gamma=120^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.solution:
When $a=b \neq c, \alpha=\beta=90^{\circ}, \gamma=120^{\circ}$.
The given condition is characteristic of hexagonal system.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the co-ordination number of sodium in $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}$ ?
questions.solution: $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}$ has antifluorite structure. $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions constitute a ccp lattice and $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions occupy the tetrahedral voids, each $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ion is in contact with $8 \mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ions and each $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ion is in contact with $4 \mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions. Thus C.N. of sodium in $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{O}=4$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Schottky defect defines imperfection in the lattice structure of a
questions.solution: Schottky defect - If some of the lattice points are unoccupied, called vacancies or holes, the lattice becomes defective. Equal number of cations and anions usually absent so that the crystal remains neutral.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An element (atomic mass $100 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$ ) having BCC structure has unit cell edge $400 \mathrm{pm}$. The density of element is (No. of atom in BCC $(Z)=2$ ).
questions.solution:
Atomic mass of element $=100 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$
Cell edge $=400 \mathrm{pm}=400 \times 10^{-12}=4 \times 10^{-10}$
and number of atoms in $\mathrm{BCC}(Z)=2$.
As the atomic mass of the metal is $100 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$, therefore
mass of each atom $(\mathrm{m})=\frac{100}{6.023 \times 10^{23}}=16.6 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~g}$
The volume of unit cell $=\left(4 \times 10^{-8}\right)^3=64 \times 10^{-24} \mathrm{~cm}^3$.
And mass of unit cell $=Z \times m$
$=2 \times\left(16.6 \times 10^{-23}\right)=33.2 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~g}$.
Therefore density of element $=\frac{\text { Mass of unit cell }}{\text { Volume of unit cell }}$
$$
=\frac{33.2 \times 10^{-23}}{64 \times 10^{-24}} \approx 5.188 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3 \text {. }
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A solid $A B$ has $\mathrm{NaCl}$ type structure. The radius of $A^{+}$is $100 \mathrm{pm}$. What is the radius of $B^{-}$?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{NaCl}$ type structure is also called face centred cubic lattice. Here the radius ratio $\left(r_c / r_a\right)$ ranges from 0.414 to 0.732 .
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { Radius ratio }=\frac{r_c}{r_a}= & \frac{\text { radius of cation }}{\text { radius of anion }} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \text { Radius of anion }\left(B^{-}\right) & =\frac{\text { radius of } A^{+}}{\text {radius ratio }} \\
& =\frac{100}{0.414} \text { to } \frac{100}{0.732} \\
& =241.5 \text { to } 136.6 .
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An $A B_2$ type structure is found in
questions.solution:
For an $A B_2$ type structure, the equivalent equation at equilibrium is
$$
A B_2 \rightleftharpoons A^{2+}+2 B^{-}
$$
For $\mathrm{CaF}_2$, the equivalent equation at equilibrium is
$$
\mathrm{CaF}_2 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{~F}^{-} \text {. }
$$
Therefore $\mathrm{CaF}_2$ has an $A B_2$ type structure.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Crystalline solids have
questions.solution: Crystalline solids are anisotropic therefore their physical properties e.g. electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, refractive index are the different in different directions. But amorphous solids, liquids and gases are isotropic (i.e. same physical properties in all directions).

In $b c c$, particles are present at corners and one particle is present at the centre within the body of the unit cell. In hcp, the packing gives a hexagonal pattern.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When $45 \mathrm{~g}$ of solute is dissolved in $600 \mathrm{~g}$ water, freezing point gets lowered by $2.2 \mathrm{~K}$, calculate molar mass of solute. $\left(K_f=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  } w_1=600 \mathrm{~g}, \quad \Delta T_f=2.2 \mathrm{~K} \\ & w_2=45 \mathrm{~g}, \quad K_f=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & M=\frac{K_f \times w_2}{\Delta T_f \times w_1}=\frac{1.86 \times 45 \times 1000}{2.2 \times 600}=63.4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

Reason : The relative lowering of vapour pressure of a solution containing a nonvolatile solute is equal to the mole fraction of the solute in the solution.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: On adding a non volatile solute in a liquid the vapour pressure decreases because some molecules of the solvent on the surface are replaced by the molecules of the solute.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The depression in freezing point of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ aqueous solutions of $\mathrm{HCl}, \mathrm{CuSO}_4$ and $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ are in the ratio
questions.solution:
$\Delta T_f=i K_f m$
Thus, $\Delta T_f \propto i$ (as concentration is same for all solutions) $i$ for $\mathrm{HCl}=2, i$ for $\mathrm{CuSO}_4=2, i$ for $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4=3$ Thus, ratio of depression in freezing point $=2: 2: 3=1: 1: 1.5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The freezing point of a solution containing $0.2 \mathrm{~g}$ of acetic acid in $20.0 \mathrm{~g}$ benzene is lowered by $0.45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The degree of association of acetic acid in benzene is (Assume acetic acid dimerises in benzene and $K_f$ for benzene $=5.12 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) $M_{\text {observed }}$ of acetic acid $=113.78$
questions.solution:
Given : $w_2=0.2 \mathrm{~g}, w_1=20 \mathrm{~g}, \Delta T_f=0.45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
$\Delta T_f=\frac{1000 \times K_f \times w_2}{w_1 \times M} \Rightarrow 0.45=\frac{1000 \times 5.12 \times 0.2}{20 \times M}$
$\therefore \quad M_{\text {(observed) }}=113.78$ (acetic acid)
$2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH} \rightleftharpoons\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}\right)_2$
Before association
After association
$1-\alpha$
$\alpha / 2$
(where $\alpha$ is degree of association)
Molecular weight of acetic acid $=60$
$\begin{aligned} & \quad i=\frac{\text { Normal molecular mass }}{\text { Observed molecular mass }} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{M_{(\text {normal })}}{M_{(\text {observed) }}}=1-\alpha+\frac{\alpha}{2} \\ & \text { or, } \frac{60}{113.78}=1-\alpha+\frac{\alpha}{2} \therefore \alpha=0.945 \text { or } 94.5 \%\end{aligned}$
