Assertion: An ideal solution obeys Raoult's law.
Reason: In an ideal solution, solute-solute as well as solvent-solvent interactions are similar to solute-solvent interactions.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : A non volatile solute is mixed in a solution then elevation in boiling point and depression in freezing point both are $2 \mathrm{~K}$.
Reason : Elevation in boiling point and depression in freezing point both depend on melting point of non-volatile solute.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Elevation in boiling point $\left(\Delta T_b\right)=K_b \times m$ Depression in freezing point $\left(\Delta T_f\right)=K_f \times m$. Elevation in boiling point and depression in freezing point are colligative properties i.e., they depend only on the number of particles of the solute. Value of $K_b$ and $K_f$ are different, so $\Delta T_b$ and $\Delta T_f$ are also different.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Boiling point of benzene is $353.23 \mathrm{~K}$. When $1.8 \mathrm{~g}$ of non-volatile solute is dissolved in $90 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene. Then boiling point is raised to $354.11 \mathrm{~K}$. Given $K_b$ (benzene) $=2.53 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The molecular mass of non-volatile substance is
questions.solution:
$T_b^{\circ}=353.23 \mathrm{~K}, W_B=1.8 \mathrm{~g}$,
$W_A=90 \mathrm{~g}_{\prime} T_b=354.11 \mathrm{~K}$,
$K_b=2.53 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{\textrm {mol } ^ { - 1 }}$
$\Delta T_b=T_b-T_b{ }^{\circ}=354.11-353.23=0.88 \mathrm{~K}$
$M_B=\frac{W_B \times K_b \times 1000}{\Delta T_b \times W_A}=\frac{1.8 \times 2.53 \times 1000}{0.88 \times 90}$
$=57.5 \approx 58 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a dilute solution, Raoult's law states that
options: the relative lowering of vapour pressure is proportional to the amount of solute in solution
the relative lowering of vapour pressure is equal to the mole fraction of solute
the lowering of vapour pressure is equal to the mole fraction of the solute
the vapour pressure of the solution is equal to the mole fraction of the solvent.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A 0.1 molal solution of an acid is $4.5 \%$ ionized. Calculate freezing point. (molecular weight of the acid is 300$) . K_f=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~kg}$.
questions.solution:
If acid is $4.5 \%$ ionized then $\alpha=0.045$.
$\begin{aligned} & \Delta T_f=\text { molality } \times K_f=0.1 \times 1.86=0.186 \\ & \Delta T_{\exp }=\Delta T_f(1+\alpha)=0.186(1+0.045)=0.194^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A mixture of two miscible liquids $A$ and $B$ is distilled under equilibrium conditions at $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ pressure. The mole fraction of $A$ in solution and vapour phase are 0.30 and 0.60 respectively. Assuming ideal behaviour of the solution and the vapour, calculate the ratio of the vapour pressure of pure $A$ to that of pure $B$
questions.solution:
In solution, $x_A=0.30 ; x_B=0.70$
In vapour phase, $x_A^{\prime}=0.60 ; x_B^{\prime}=0.40$
Using Dalton's law and Raoult's law
$\begin{aligned} & x_A^{\prime}=0.60=\frac{p_A}{P}=\frac{p_A}{p_A+p_B}=\frac{0.30 p_A^{\circ}}{0.30 p_A^{\circ}+0.70 p_B^{\circ}} \\ & x_B^{\prime}=0.40=\frac{p_B}{P}=\frac{p_B}{p_A+p_B}=\frac{0.70 p_B^{\circ}}{0.30 p_A^{\circ}+0.70 p_B^{\circ}} \\ & \frac{x_A^{\prime}}{x_B^{\prime}}=\frac{0.60}{0.40}=\frac{0.30 p_A^{\circ}}{0.70 p_B^{\circ}} \\ & \frac{p_A^{\circ}}{p_B^{\circ}}=\frac{0.60 \times 0.70}{0.40 \times 0.30}=\frac{7}{2}=3.5\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: \(1 \mathrm{~mol}\) each of the following compounds is dissolved in \(1 \mathrm{~L}\) of solution. Which will have the largest \(\Delta T_b\) value?
questions.solution:
Bond dissociation energy increases in the order.
\(\mathrm{HI}  < \mathrm{HBr} < \mathrm{HCl} < \mathrm{HF}\)
\(\mathrm{HF}\) is most stable halogen acid and \(\mathrm{HI}\) is the least. Higher the bond dissociation energy, lower is the degree of ionisation.
As we know \(\Delta T_b=i K_b m\).
Hence \(\Delta T_b\) value is largest in HI. All colligative properties depend on number of particles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A $5 \%$ solution (by mass) of cane sugar in water has freezing point of $271 \mathrm{~K}$ and freezing point of pure water is $273.15 \mathrm{~K}$. The freezing point of a $5 \%$ solution (by mass) of glucose in water is
questions.solution:
$: K_f$ for water $=\frac{\Delta T_f \times W \times m}{1000 \times w}$
(where $W=$ wt. of water, $w=$ wt. of cane sugar,
$m=$ molecular wt. of cane sugar)
$$
=\frac{2.15 \times 100 \times 342}{1000 \times 5}=14.7
$$
Now, for $5 \%$ glucose,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Delta T_f=\frac{K_f \times 1000 \times w^{\prime}}{W \times m^{\prime}}\left(\text { where } w^{\prime}=\right.\text { wt. of glucose, } \\
&\left.m^{\prime}=\text { molecular wt. of glucose }\right) \\
&=\frac{14.7 \times 1000 \times 5}{100 \times 180}=4.08
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ Freezing point of glucose solution $=273.15-4.08=269.07 \mathrm{~K}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The vapour pressure of pure benzene at a certain temperature is 0.850 bar. A non-volatile, nonelectrolyte solid weighing \(0.5 \mathrm{~g}\) is added to 39.0 \(\mathrm{g}\) of benzene (molar mass \(78 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}\) ). The vapour pressure of the solution then is 0.845 bar. What is the molecular mass of the solid substance?
questions.solution:
\(P_A^{\circ}=0.850\) bar, \(P_S=0.845\) bar
\(w=0.5 \mathrm{~g}, \quad m=\text { ? }\)
weight of solvent (benzene) \(=39.0 \mathrm{~g}\)
and molecular weight of benzene \(=78 \mathrm{~g}\)
As we know, \(\frac{P_A^{\circ}-P_S}{P_A^{\circ}}=x_B=\frac{n_B}{n_A}\)
\(\frac{0.850-0.845}{0.850}=\frac{\frac{w_B}{M_B}}{\frac{w_A}{M_A}}=\frac{\frac{0.5}{m}}{\frac{39}{78}}\)
On solving, we get molecular mass of solid structure \((\mathrm{m})=170 \mathrm{~g}\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : If red blood cells were removed from the body and placed in pure water, pressure inside the cells increases.
Reason : The concentration of salt content in the cells increases.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true, but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution: If the red blood cells are placed in pure water, pressure inside the cells increases as the water is drawn in and the cell swells.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The vapour pressure of pure $\mathrm{CHCl}_3$ and $\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2$ are 200 and 41.5 atm respectively. The weight of $\mathrm{CHCl}_3$ and $\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2$ are $11.9 \mathrm{~g}$ and $17 \mathrm{~g}$ respectively in a solution. The vapour pressure of solution (in atm) will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { (c) }: p_{\mathrm{CHCl}_3}^{\mathrm{o}}=200 \mathrm{~atm} ; p_{\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2}^{\mathrm{o}}=41.5 \mathrm{~atm} \\ & n_{\mathrm{CHCl}}=11.9 / 119.5=0.1, n_{\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Cl}_2}=17 / 85=0.2 \\ & P_T=p_A^{\mathrm{o}} x_A+p_B^{\mathrm{o}} x_B \\ & =200 \times(0.1 / 0.3)+41.5 \times(0.2 / 0.3)=94.3 \mathrm{~atm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The water pouch of instant cold pack for treating athletic injuries breaks when squeezed and $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3$ dissolves lowering the temperature.
Reason : Addition of non-volatile solute into solvent results into depression of freezing point of solvent.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true, but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution: Freezing point of a substance is defined as the temperature at which the vapour pressure of its liquid is equal to the vapour pressure of the corresponding solid. Since the addition of a non-volatile solute always lowers the vapour pressure of solvent, therefore it will be in equilibrium with solid phase at a lower pressure and hence at a lower temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The relationship between osmotic pressure at $273 \mathrm{~K}$ when $10 \mathrm{~g}$ glucose $\left(P_1\right), 10 \mathrm{~g}$ urea $\left(P_2\right)$ and $10 \mathrm{~g}$ sucrose $\left(P_3\right)$ are dissolved in $250 \mathrm{~mL}$ of water is
questions.solution: $P=\frac{w}{M V} R T$; since $w, V$ and $T$ are same, thus $P \propto(1 / M)$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $1 \mathrm{~g}$ of polymer having molar mass $1,60,000 \mathrm{~g}$ is dissolved in $800 \mathrm{~mL}$ water. Calculate the osmotic pressure in pascal at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. $\left(R=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mole}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\pi=C R T$
$\pi=\frac{1}{1.6 \times 10^5} \times \frac{8.314 \times 300}{800 \times 10^{-6}}=19.4 \mathrm{~Pa}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ethylene glycol is used as antifreeze to reduce freezing point of water to $-2.4^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What mass of antifreeze is required for $2 \mathrm{~L}$ water? $\left(K_f\right.$ water $\left.=1.86 \mathrm{~K} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{mole}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} w_{\text {solute }} & =\frac{\Delta T_f \times w_{\text {water }} \times M_{\text {solute }}}{K_f} \\ & =\frac{2.4 \times 2 \times 0.062}{1.86}=160 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$
[Mass of $2 \mathrm{~L}$ of water is $2 \mathrm{~kg}$.]

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Two elements $A$ and $B$ form compounds of formula $A B_2$ and $A B_4$. When dissolved in $20.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene $1.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of $A B_2$ lowers f. pt. by $2.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ whereas $1.0 \mathrm{~g}$ of $A B_4$ lowers $\mathrm{f}$. pt. by $1.3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The $K_f$ for benzene is 5.1. The atomic masses of $A$ and $B$ are
questions.solution:
Let the masses of $A$ and $B$ be $a$ and $b$. The mass of $A B_2$ will be $(a+2 b) \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ and $A B_4$ will be $(a+4 b) \mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$. $\Delta T_f=\frac{K_f \times W_B \times 1000}{M_B \times W_A}$
On solving (i) and (ii), we get $a=25.59$ and $b=42.64$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The average osmotic pressure of human blood is 7.8 bar at $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the concentration of an aqueous $\mathrm{NaCl}$ solution that could be used in the blood stream?
questions.solution:
$: \pi=C R T$
$$
C=\frac{\pi}{R T}=\frac{7.8}{0.082 \times 310}=0.31 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L} .
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The molecular weight of acetic acid determined by depression in freezing point method in benzene and water was found to be different. 
Reason : Water is polar and benzene is non-polar.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Depression in freezing point is a colligative property which depends upon the number of particles. The number of particles are different in case of benzene and water that is why molecular weight of acetic acid determined by depression in freezing point method is also different.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the statements given below concerning properties of solutions, describes a colligative effect?
options: Boiling point of pure water decreases by the addition of ethanol.
Vapour pressure of pure water decreases by the addition of nitric acid.
Vapour pressure of pure benzene decreases by the addition of naphthalene.
Boiling point of pure benzene increases by the addition of toluene.
questions.solution: Boiling point of a pure solvent increases by increase in number of soluble particles present in it. So by addition of toluene, boiling point of pure benzene increases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Vapour pressure of benzene at $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $121.8 \mathrm{~mm}$. When $15 \mathrm{~g}$ of a non-volatile solute is dissolved in $250 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzene, its vapour pressure is decreased to $120.2 \mathrm{~mm}$. The molecular weight of the solute is
questions.solution:
Relative lowering of vapour pressure is given by :
$$
\frac{P^0-P}{P^0}=\frac{w / m}{w / m+W / M}
$$
Where, $P^0=$ Vapour pressure of pure solvent
$P=$ Vapour pressure of solution
$w$ mass of solute
$m=$ molecular mass of solute
$W$ = mass of solvent
$M=$ molecular mass of solvent.
For dilute solution
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{121.8-120.2}{121.8}=\frac{15 / m}{250 / 78} \Rightarrow m=356.265
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Assertion : The solubility of a gas in a liquid increases with increase of pressure.Reason : The solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the pressure of the gas.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: This is according to Henry's law which states that the solubility of a gas in given volume of a liquid at a particular temperature is directly proportional to the pressure of gas above the liquid. $m \propto p, m=k p$ where $k=$ Henry's constant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Increasing pressure on pure water decreases its freezing point.
Reason : Density of water is minimum at $273 \mathrm{~K}$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
According to Clapeyron-Clausius equation, $\frac{d T}{d P}=\frac{T\left(V_l-V_s\right)}{\Delta H_f}$
Since density of ice is less than that of water, $V_s$ is greater than $V_l$. In other words, the expression on the right hand side of the above equation is negative. This means that the increase of pressure must lower and decrease of pressure must raise the freezing point.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Azeotropic mixtures are formed only by non-ideal solutions and they may have boiling points either greater than both the components or lesser than both the components.
Reason: The composition of the vapour phase is same as that of the liquid phase of an azeotropic mixture.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Non-ideal solutions with positive deviation i.e., having more vapour pressure than expected, boil at lower temperature while those with negative deviation boil at higher temperature than those of the components.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : One mole of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ formula units of sodium chloride.
Reason : $58.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ also contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ formula units of $\mathrm{NaCl}$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The normality of $10 \% \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ solution having density $1.1 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}$ is
questions.solution:
Weight of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4=10 \mathrm{~g}$
Weight of solution $=100 \mathrm{~g}$
$\begin{aligned} \text { Volume of solution } & =\frac{\text { Mass of solution }}{\text { Density of solution }} \\ & =\frac{100}{1.1}=90.91 \mathrm{~mL}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Normality }=\frac{n_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4} \times 1000}{\text { Volume of solution (in mL) }} \\ & =\frac{w_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4} \times 1000}{\mathrm{GEM}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4} \times \text { Volume of solution (in mL) }}\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{10 \times 1000}{\left(\frac{98}{2}\right) \times 90.91}=2.24 \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of atoms in $52 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{He}$ is
questions.solution:
Number of atoms $=\frac{N_A \times \text { mass }}{\text { At. mass }}$
$=\frac{6.023 \times 10^{23} \times 52}{4}=7.829 \times 10^{24}$ atoms

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Find empirical formula of the compound containing a metal, $M=68 \%$ (atomic mass =34) and remaining $32 \%$ oxygen.
questions.solution: $\therefore \quad$ Empirical formula $=M O$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many significant figures are present in 0.0000135 ?
questions.solution: Zeros to the left of the first non-zero digit in a number are not significant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In a chemical reaction, $A+B \longrightarrow A B$, if $B$ is acting as a limiting reagent, then choose the correct option.
questions.solution: As $B$ is a limiting reagent so, it should be present in limited amount and its amount should be less than the amount of $A$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of liquid carbon disulphide (specific gravity 2.63) is burnt in oxygen. Find the volume of the resulting gases measured at STP.
questions.solution:
$1 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{CS}_2$ weighs $2.63 \mathrm{~g}$
$10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{CS}_2$ weighs $26.3 \mathrm{~g}$
$\because \quad 76 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{CS}_2$ will yield $67.2 \mathrm{~L}$ of a mixture of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ and $\mathrm{SO}_2$ at STP
$\therefore \quad 26.3$ gof $\mathrm{CS}_2$ would yield $\frac{67.2}{76} \times 26.3=23.25 \mathrm{~L}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The amount of $\mathrm{BaSO}_4$ precipitated on mixing $\mathrm{BaCl}_2(0.5 \mathrm{M})$ with $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4(1 \mathrm{M})$ will correspond to

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $100 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of a given sample of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ gives $1000 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of $\mathrm{O}_2$ at S.T.P. The given sample is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When $25 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ is dissolved in $10^3 \mathrm{~kg}$ of solution, its concentration will be
questions.solution:
When a solute is present in very minute amounts (trace quantities), the concentration is expressed in ppm.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { ppm of } A=\frac{\text { mass of component } A}{\text { total mass of solution }} \times 10^6 \\
& =\frac{0.025}{10^3} \times 10^6=25 \mathrm{ppm} \text {. } \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the molarity of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ solution that has a density of $1.84 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cc}$ at $35^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and contains $98 \%$ by weight?
questions.solution:
Molarity
$=\frac{\text { wt. of solute }}{\text { mol. wt. }} \times \frac{1000}{\text { vol. of solution (in mL) }}$
(Volume of solution $=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { density }}=\frac{100}{1.84}=54.34 \mathrm{~mL}$ )
Now, molarity $=\frac{98}{98} \times \frac{1000}{54.34}=18.4 \mathrm{M}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : When a concentrated solution is diluted by adding more water, molarity of the solution remains unchanged.
Reason : Product of moles of a solute and volume is equal to the molarity.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Molarity equation is written as $M_1 V_1=M_2 V_2$ thus if the $V_2$ changes $M_2$ also changes.
Molarity $=\frac{\text { moles of solute }}{\text { volume of solution in litre }}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Stomach acid, a dilute solution of $\mathrm{HCl}$ in water, can be neutralized by reaction with sodium hydrogen carbonate,
$$
\mathrm{NaHCO}_{3(a q)}+\mathrm{HCl}_{(a q)} \rightarrow \mathrm{NaCl}_{(a q)}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(l)}+\mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}
$$
How many milliliters of $0.125 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{NaHCO}_3$ solution are needed to neutralize $18.0 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.100 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{HCl}$ ?

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The molality of the solution does not change with change in tempe-rature.
Reason : The molality is expressed in units of moles per $1000 \mathrm{~g}$ of solvent.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Molality does not depend upon volume thus it does not depend on temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The weight of one molecule of a compound $\mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}_{122}$ is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Molecular weight of } \mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}_{122}=60 \times 12+1 \times 122 \\
& =842 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \therefore \text { Weight of } 1 \text { mole of } \mathrm{C}_{60} \mathrm{H}_{122} \text { molecules }=842 \mathrm{~g} \\
& \Rightarrow \text { Weight of } 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { molecules } \quad=842 \mathrm{~g} \\
& \Rightarrow \text { Weight of } 1 \text { molecule }=\frac{842}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} \\
& =1.4 \times 10^{-21} \mathrm{~g} \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Atoms can neither be created nor destroyed.
Reason : Under similar condition of temperature and pressure, equal volume of gases does not contain equal number of atoms.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Atoms can neither be created nor destroyed. This is the statement of law of conservation of mass. Under similar conditions of temperature and pressure, equal volume of gases contain equal number of atoms. As, $P V=n R T$ (Ideal gas equation)
If, for Ist case, for IInd case,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P_1 V_1=n_1 R T_1 \\
& P_2 V_2=n_2 R T_2
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{P_1 V_1}{P_2 V_2}=\frac{n_1 R T_1}{n_2 R T_2} \Rightarrow n_1=n_2\left(\because P_1,=P_2, V_1=V_2, T_1=T_2\right)
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How many litres of ammonia gas at S.T.P. would be needed to prepare $100 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $2.5 \mathrm{M}$ ammonium hydroxide solution?
questions.solution:
Molecular weight of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}=35$
Now, $35 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ is needed to prepare $1 \mathrm{M}$ solution in 1 litre
$\Rightarrow 35 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ in $1 \mathrm{~L}$ solution $\equiv 1$ molar solution
$\Rightarrow 2.5 \times 35 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ in $1 \mathrm{~L}$ solution $\equiv 2.5$ molar solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{2.5 \times 35}{10} \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH} \text { is } 100 \mathrm{~mL} \text { solution } \equiv 2.5 \text { molar solution } \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 8.75 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH} \text { was dissolved. } \\
& \mathrm{NH}_3+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH} \\
& \quad \text { For } 35 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}, \mathrm{NH}_3 \text { needed }=22.4 \mathrm{~L} \text { at S.T.P } \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \text { for } 8.75 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}, \mathrm{NH}_3 \text { needed }=\frac{22.4}{35} \times 8.75 \mathrm{~L} \\
&=5.6 \mathrm{~L}
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate molarity of $63 \% \mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w} \mathrm{HNO}_3$ solution if density is $1.4 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mL}$.
questions.solution:
$63 \%$ nitric acid by mass means that mass of nitric acid $=63 \mathrm{~g}$
Mass of solution $=100 \mathrm{~g}$
Molar mass of $\mathrm{HNO}_3=63 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Mole of $\mathrm{HNO}_3=\frac{63 \mathrm{~g}}{63 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}=1 \mathrm{~mole}$
Density of solution $=1.4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$
$\therefore \quad$ Volume of solution
$=\frac{100 \mathrm{~g}}{1.4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}}=71.428 \mathrm{~mL}=0.071428 \mathrm{~L}$
Molarity of solution $=\frac{\text { Moles of solute }}{\text { Volume of solution in } \mathrm{L}}$
$=\frac{1.0}{0.071428} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}=14 \mathrm{M}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Mixture of two bivalent metals $A$ and $B$ having mass $2 \mathrm{~g}$ when dissolved in $\mathrm{HCl}$ at STP, $2.24 \mathrm{~L}$ $\mathrm{H}_2$ is evolved. What is the mass of $A$ present in mixture? (Atomic mass of $A=15 \mathrm{u}, B=30 \mathrm{u}$ )
questions.solution:
$\quad A+2 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow A \mathrm{Cl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2$
Mole : $\frac{x}{15}$
$\frac{x}{15}$
$B+2 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{BCl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2$
Mole : $\frac{2-x}{30}$
$\frac{2-x}{30}$
Total moles of $\mathrm{H}_2=\frac{x}{15}+\frac{2-x}{30}=\frac{2.24}{22.4}=\frac{1}{10}$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=1 \mathrm{~g}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Strength of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ is $15.18 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}$, then it is equal to
questions.solution:
Volume strength $=\frac{5.6 \times \text { Strength in } \mathrm{g} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{\text { Eq. wt. of } \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2}$
$=\frac{5.6 \times 15.18}{17}=5$ volumes

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion: One mole of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules of sodium chloride.
Reason : $58.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ also contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules of $\mathrm{NaCl}$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Ifboth assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Molar mass or mass of 1 mole of $\mathrm{NaCl}$
$$
=23+35.5=58.5 \mathrm{~g}
$$
According to mole concept,
1 mole of molecules $=$ Gram molecular mass $=6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules
$\therefore 58.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ also contains $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules of $\mathrm{NaCl}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: To a $25 \mathrm{~mL} \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ solution, excess of acidified solution of $\mathrm{KI}$ was added. The iodine liberated required $20 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.3 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ solution. The volume strength of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ solution is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { } 2 \mathrm{KI}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{I}_2 \\ & 2 \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+\mathrm{I}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{~S}_4 \mathrm{O}_6+2 \mathrm{NaI}\end{aligned}$
milli eq. of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ in $25 \mathrm{~mL}=20 \times 0.3=6$
milli eq. of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ in $1000 \mathrm{~mL}=\frac{6}{25} \times 1000=240$
Equivalent per litre $=\frac{240}{1000}$
Gram per litre of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2=\frac{240}{1000} \times 17=4.08 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{L}$
(Equivalent weight of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2=\frac{34}{2}=17$ ).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Percentage of Se (at. mass \(=78.4)\) in peroxidase anhydrase enzyme is \(0.5 \%\) by weight, then minimum molecular mass of peroxidase anhydrase enzyme is
questions.solution:
The enzyme must contain at least one atom of Se.
$\because \quad 0.5 \mathrm{~g}$ of Se is present in $100 \mathrm{~g}$ of enzyme
$\therefore \quad 78.4 \mathrm{~g}$ of Se will be present in $=\frac{100 \times 78.4}{0.5}$
$=1.576 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~g}$ of enzymes

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $0.189 \mathrm{~g}$ of a chlorine containing organic compound gave $0.287 \mathrm{~g}$ of silver chloride, then the percentage of chlorine in the organic compound is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Critical temperature of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is $304 \mathrm{~K}$, it cannot be liquified above $304 \mathrm{~K}$.
Reason : At a certain temperature, volume $\propto \frac{1}{\text { pressure }}$
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Critical temperature of a gas may be defined as that temperature above which it cannot be liqufied however high pressure may be applied on the gas.
Hence, $\mathrm{CO}_2$ cannot be liquified above its critical temperature.
According to Boyle's law
$P \propto \frac{1}{V}$ or $V \propto \frac{1}{P}$ (at constant $T$ and $\left.n\right)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Two separate bulbs contain gas $A$ and gas $B$. The density of gas $A$ is twice as that of $B$. The molecular mass of $A$ is half as that of $B$. If temperature is constant, the ratio of the pressure of $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } d=\frac{P M}{R T} \\ & \text { Given : } \frac{d_A}{d_B}=2, \frac{M_A}{M_B}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \frac{d_A}{d_B}=\frac{P_A M_A}{R T} \times \frac{R T}{P_B M_B}=2 \Rightarrow \frac{P_A}{P_B} \times \frac{M_A}{M_B}=2 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{P_A}{P_B} \times \frac{1}{2}=2 \Rightarrow \frac{P_A}{P_B}=4: 1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Assertion : Greater the value of van der Waals' constant ' $a$ ' greater is the liquefaction of gas. Reason : ' $a$ ' indirectly measures the magnitude of attractive forces between the molecules.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Considering the attractive force pressure, the ideal gas equation $(P V=n R T)$ is corrected by introducing a factor of $\frac{a n^2}{V^2}$ where $a$ is van der Waals constant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : A spherical water drop becomes flat on flat surface.
Reason : It becomes flat due to gravity.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Assertion : In a pressure cooker, the water is brought to boil. The cooker is then removed from the stove. Now on removing the lid of pressure cooker, the water starts boiling again.Reason : The impurities in water bring down its boiling point.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: In pressure cooker, water boils above $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. When the lid of cooker is opened, pressure is lowered so that boiling point decreases and water boils again.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : At critical point the densities of substance in gaseous and liquid states are same.
Reason : Critical temperature is the temperature at which the real gas exhibit ideal behaviour for considerable range of pressure.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Temperature at which the real gas exhibit ideal behaviour for considerable range of pressure is known as Boyle's temperature. $T_b=\frac{a}{b R} a$, and $b$ are van der Waal's constant. Critical temperature is the temperature above which the gas cannot be liquefied, how so ever high pressure may be applied : $T_c=\frac{8 a}{27 R b}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When $1 \mathrm{~g}$ of gas $A$ at 4 bar pressure is added to $2 \mathrm{~g}$ of gas $B$, the total pressure inside the container becomes 6 bar. Which of the following is true?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } \frac{n_1}{p_1}=\frac{n_2}{p_2} \\ & \frac{1}{\frac{M_A}{4}}=\frac{\frac{1}{M_A}+\frac{2}{M_B}}{6} \Rightarrow \frac{3}{M_A}=\frac{2}{M_A}+\frac{4}{M_B} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{M_A}=\frac{4}{M_B} \therefore M_B=4 M_A\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Gas in a cylinder is maintained at $10 \mathrm{~atm}$ pressure and $300 \mathrm{~K}$ temperature. The cylinder will explode if pressure of gas goes beyond 15 atm. What is the maximum temperature to which gas can be heated?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  } \frac{P_1}{T_1}=\frac{P_2}{T_2} \\ & \frac{10}{300}=\frac{15}{T_2} \Rightarrow T_2=450 \mathrm{~K}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the van der Waals equation, ' $a$ ' signifies
options: attraction between molecules and wall of container
questions.solution: In van der Waal's equation, $a$ signifies the intermolecular force of attraction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Two flasks $X$ and $Y$ have capacity $1 \mathrm{~L}$ and $2 \mathrm{~L}$ respectively and each of them contains 1 mole of a gas. The temperatures of the flasks are so adjusted that average speed of molecules in $X$ is twice as those in $Y$. The pressure in flask $X$ would be
questions.solution:
$P=\frac{1}{3} \frac{m n u^2}{V}$
$$
\frac{P_X}{P_Y}=\frac{n_X u_X V_Y}{n_Y u_Y V_x}=\frac{\left(N_0 / 1000\right)}{\left(N_0 / 2000\right)} \times 2 \times \frac{2}{1}=8
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Equal weight of $\mathrm{CO}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_4$ are mixed together in an empty container at $300 \mathrm{~K}$. The fraction of total pressure exerted by $\mathrm{CH}_4$ is
questions.solution:
Let the weight of $\mathrm{CO}=$ weight of $\mathrm{CH}_4=a \mathrm{~g}$
Moles of $\mathrm{CO}=\frac{a}{28}$
Moles of $\mathrm{CH}_4=\frac{a}{16}$
Total moles $=\frac{a}{28}+\frac{a}{16}$
$$
x_{\mathrm{CH}_4}=\frac{\frac{a}{16}}{\frac{a}{28}+\frac{a}{16}}=\frac{a}{16} \times \frac{28 \times 16}{44 a}=\frac{14}{22}=\frac{7}{11}
$$
$\therefore$ Fraction of pressure exerted by $\mathrm{CH}_4=7 / 11$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In \(P\) versus \(V\) graph, the horizontal line is found in which ____ exists.
questions.solution: From the graph, at point \(A, \mathrm{CO}_2\) exists as a gas, As pressure is increased, the volume of the gas decreases along the curve \(A B\). At \(B\) liquefaction of the gas starts. Hence, volume decreases rapidly along \(B C\) because liquid has much less volume than the gas. At point \(C\), liquefaction is complete. Thus, along horizontal line, gas converts into liquid.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The variation of volume $V$, with temperature $T$, keeping pressure constant is called the coefficient of thermal expansion $(\alpha)$ of a gas. i.e., $\alpha=\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P$. For an ideal gas $\alpha$ is equal to
questions.solution:
For $n$ moles of an ideal gas $P V=n R T$ or $V=n \frac{R T}{P}$
Differentiating with respect to $T$ at constant $P$, we have
$\begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P=\frac{n R}{P}=\frac{V}{T} \\ & \alpha=\frac{1}{V}\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P=\frac{1}{V} \times \frac{V}{T}=\frac{1}{T}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Critical temperatures for \(A, B, C\) and \(D\) gases are \(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 10^{\circ} \mathrm{C},-80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) and \(15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) respectively. Which gas will be liquefied more easily?
questions.solution:
Critical temperature of gas may be defined as that temperature above which it cannot be liquefied how so ever high pressure may be applied on the gas. As we know, \(T_C=\frac{8 a}{27 R b}\)
where \(a=\) van der Wal's constant which is a measure of intermolecular forces of attraction. Greater the value of \(a\) more easily the gas can be liquefied and hence larger \(T_C\) mean larger the value of \(a\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Dominance of strong repulsive forces among the molecules of the gas ( $Z=$ compressibility factor)
questions.solution: When the value of $Z < 1$, it is due to attractive forces between molecules. At high pressure, when $Z>1$, it is due to repulsive forces between electron clouds of the molecules of gases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Effusion rate of oxygen is smaller than nitrogen.
Reason : Molar mass of nitrogen is smaller than oxygen.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Rate of effusion is inversely proportional to the molar mass of gas.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Compressibility factor for hydrogen varies with pressure with positive slope at all pressures.
Reason : Even at low pressures, repulsive forces dominate hydrogen gas.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: In case of $\mathrm{H}_2$, compressibility factor increases with the pressure. At $273 \mathrm{~K}, Z>1$ which shows that it is difficult to compress the gas as compared to ideal gas. In this case repulsive forces dominate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $P$ is pressure and $\rho$ is density of a gas, then $P$ and $\rho$ are related as
questions.solution:
Ideal gas equation is given as :
$P V=n R T$
$P=$ Pressure of gas, $V=$ volume of gas
$n=$ moles of gas, $R=$ gas constant
$T=$ temperature
$$
\begin{array}{rlrl}
\Rightarrow \quad P V=\frac{w}{M} R T \quad & w & =\text { mass of gas } \\
M & =\text { Molecular mass of gas }
\end{array}
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& P=\left(\frac{w}{V}\right) \frac{R T}{M} \Rightarrow P=\rho \frac{R T}{M} \\
& \left(\operatorname{As} \rho=\frac{w}{V}\right) \\
\Rightarrow \quad & P \propto \rho
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In van der Waals equation at constant temperature $300 \mathrm{~K}$, if $a=1.4 \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~mol}^{-2}$, $V=100 \mathrm{~mL}, n=1 \mathrm{~mole}$, then what is the pressure of the gas?
questions.solution:
At moderate pressure van der Waals equation is given as :
$\begin{aligned} & \left(P+\frac{a n^2}{V^2}\right)(V)=n R T \\ & \left(P+\frac{1.4}{(0.1)^2}\right)(0.1)=1 \times 0.082 \times 300\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & (P+140) \times 0.1=24.6 \\ & 0.1 P+14=24.6 \\ & 0.1 P=10.6 \Rightarrow P=106 \mathrm{~atm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Arrange the following gases in order of their critical temperature.
$\mathrm{NH}_3, \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{CO}_2, \mathrm{O}_2$
questions.solution: Greater are the intermolecular forces of attraction, higher is the critical temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The density of a gas $A$ is thrice that of a gas $B$ at the same temperature. The molecular weight of gas $B$ is twice that of $A$. What will be the ratio of the pressures acting on $B$ and $A$ ?
questions.solution:
$\frac{d}{p}=\frac{M}{R T}$
Let density of gas $B$ be $d$.
$\therefore \quad$ density of gas $A=3 d$ and let molecular weight of $A$ be $M$.
$\therefore$ molecular weight of $B=2 M$
Since, $R$ is gas constant and $T$ is same for both gases, so
$\begin{aligned} & p_A=\frac{d_A R T}{M_A} \text { and } p_B=\frac{d_B R T}{M_B} \\ & \frac{p_B}{p_A}=\frac{d_B}{d_A} \times \frac{M_A}{M_B}=\frac{d}{3 d} \times \frac{M}{2 M}=\frac{1}{6}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Liquefaction of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ is easier than $\mathrm{CO}_2$.
Reason : $T_c$ of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is more than that of $\mathrm{NH}_3$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: $T_c$ of $\mathrm{NH}_3$ is more than that of $\mathrm{CO}_2$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The root mean square speed of the molecules of diatomic gas is \(u\). When the temperature is doubled, the molecules dissociate into two atoms. The new rms speed of the atom is
questions.solution:
$u=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}$
If $T=2 T$ and $M=M / 2$, then $u_1=\sqrt{\frac{3 R \times 2 T}{M / 2}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{u_1}{u}=\sqrt{4}=2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Compressibility factor for hydrogen varies with pressure with positive slope at all pressure.
Reason : Even at low pressure, repulsive forces dominate for hydrogen gas
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: In case of $\mathrm{H}_2$, compressibility factor increases with the pressure. At $273 \mathrm{~K}, Z>1$ which shows that it is difficult to compress the gas as compared to an ideal gas. In this case, repulsive forces dominate.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : If \(\mathrm{H}_2\) and \(\mathrm{Cl}_2\) enclosed separately in the same vessel exert pressure of 100 and \(200 \mathrm{~mm}\) respectively, their mixture in the same vessel at the same temperature will exert a pressure of \(300 \mathrm{~mm}\).
Reason : Dalton's law of partial pressures states that total pressure is the sum of partial pressures.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: $\mathrm{H}_2$ and $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ react chemically. Hence Dalton's law is not applicable. Dalton's law states that "at a given temperature, the total pressure exerted by two or more non-reacting gases occupying a definite volume is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the component gases."

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
A bulb is emitted electromagnetic radiation of $660 \mathrm{~nm}$ wavelength. The total energy of radiation is $3 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~J}$. The number of emitted photons will be
$\left(h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~s}, c=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$
questions.solution:
Number of photons emitted $=n$
$\begin{aligned} & E=\frac{n h c}{\lambda} \\ & 3 \times 10^{-18}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8 \times n}{660 \times 10^{-9}} \\ & n=\frac{30}{3}=10\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Wavelength of a particular transition for $\mathrm{H}$ atom is $400 \mathrm{~nm}$. What can be the wavelength of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$for the same transition?
questions.solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=R_{\mathrm{H}}\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right) \times Z^2$
On dividing equation (i) by (ii), we get $\lambda_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}=\frac{400}{2^2}=\frac{400}{4}=100 \mathrm{~nm}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The position of both an electron and helium atom is known within $1.0 \mathrm{~nm}$. The momentum of the electron is known within $5.0 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The minimum uncertainty in the measurement of the momentum of the helium atom is
questions.solution: The Heisenberg uncertainty principle, $\Delta x \times \Delta p \geq \frac{h}{4 \pi}$, where $\Delta x=$ Uncertainty in position, $\Delta p=$ Uncertainty in momentum and $h / 4 \pi=$ constant. As $\Delta x$ is same for electron and helium and $h / 4 \pi$ is a constant, therefore minimum uncertainty in the measurement of the momentum of the helium atom will be same as that of an electron which is $5.0 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the maximum wavelength of line of Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum?
$\left(R=1.09 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
For maximum wavelength in the Balmer series, $n_2=3$ and $n_1=2$.
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{1}{\lambda} & =1.09 \times 10^7 \times 1^2\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{3^2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\lambda} & =1.09 \times 10^7 \times 1\left(\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{9}\right) \\ & =1.09 \times 10^7 \times 1\left(\frac{5}{36}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \lambda & =\frac{36}{5 \times 1.09 \times 10^7} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =6.60 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}=660 \mathrm{~nm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate the velocity of an electron placed in the second orbit of $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ ion.
questions.solution:
The velocity of an electron in $n^{\text {th }}$ shell of the hydrogen like atom $=\frac{2.18 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \times Z}{n}$ On putting $Z=3$ and $n=2$,
$v=\frac{2.18 \times 10^6 \times 3}{2}=3.27 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ionisation potential of hydrogen atom is $13.6 \mathrm{eV}$. Hydrogen atom in ground state is excited by monochromatic light of energy $12.1 \mathrm{eV}$. The spectral lines emitted by hydrogen according to Bohr's theory will be
questions.solution:
Since ionisation potential of hydrogen atom is $13.6 \mathrm{eV}$.
$\therefore \quad E_1=-13.6 \mathrm{eV}$
Now, $E_n-E_1=\frac{-13.6}{n^2}-(-13.6)=12.1$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{-13.6}{n^2}+13.6=12.1 \\ & \therefore \quad n=3\end{aligned}$
After absorbing $12.1 \mathrm{eV}$, the electron of $\mathrm{H}$-atom is excited to $3^{\text {rd }}$ shell.
Thus, possible transitions are 3 i.e., $3 \rightarrow 2$, $2 \rightarrow 1$ and $3 \rightarrow 1$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The chemical properties of different isotopes are same.
Reason: Isotopes have same number of neutrons.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Isotopes have different number of neutrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: According to Bohr's theory, which of the following correctly represents the variation of energy and radius of an electron in $n^{\text {th }}$ orbit of $\mathrm{H}$-atom?
questions.solution:
According to Bohr's theory,
$r=\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m Z e^2 k}$ and $E_n=\frac{-k^2 2 \pi^2 m Z^2 e^4}{n^2 h^2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\alpha$-Particles can be detected by using
questions.solution: Rutherford first of all used zinc sulphide ( $\mathrm{ZnS})$ as phosphor in detection of $\alpha$-particles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : For hydrogen like species, energy of an electron in a particular orbit increases with increase in value of $Z$.
Reason : Electronegativity decreases across a period.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
For hydrogen like species, energy of an electron in $n^{\text {th }}$ orbit is given by
$E_n=-\frac{2 \pi^2 m Z^2 e^4 k^2}{n^2 h^2}$
i.e., $E_n \propto Z^2$.
Electronegativity increases across a period with decrease in size of atoms.

questions.q:
Which of the following arrangement is possible?
$\begin{array}{llll}n & l & m & s\end{array}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Choose the correctly paired gaseous cation and its magnetic (spin only) moment (in B.M.)
questions.solution: Using expression, $\mu=\sqrt{n(n+2)}$ B.M. (where, $n=$ no. of unpaired electrons)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following pairs represent isotones?
questions.solution:
Isotones have the same number of 
neutrons.
As $=77-33=44 ; \mathrm{Se}=78-34=44$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Assertion: $\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{IV})$ is known but $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{IV})$ is not. Reason : $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{IV})$ has $d^4$ electronic configuration.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Both Co and Ni have (IV) oxidation state. $\mathrm{Ni}$ (IV) has $3 d^6$ electronic configuration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A particle is moving 3 times faster than the speed of electron. If the ratio of wavelength of particle and electron is $1.8 \times 10^{-4}$, then particle is
questions.solution:
Given $: \frac{\lambda_{\text {particle }}}{\lambda_{\text {electron }}}=1.8 \times 10^{-4}$
and $\frac{v_{\text {particle }}}{v_{\text {electron }}}=3$
According to de-Broglie equation,
$\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}$
$\frac{\lambda_{\text {particle }}}{\lambda_{\text {electron }}}=\frac{h}{m_{\text {particle }} \times v_{\text {particle }}} \times \frac{m_{\text {electron }} \times v_{\text {electron }}}{h}$
$=\frac{m_{\text {electron }}}{m_{\text {particle }}} \times \frac{v_{\text {electron }}}{v_{\text {particle }}}$
$\Rightarrow 1.8 \times 10^{-4}=\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}}{m_{\text {particle }}} \times \frac{1}{3}$
$m_{\text {particle }}=\frac{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}{1.8 \times 10^{-4} \times 3}$
$=1.6852 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$
Actual mass of neutron is $1.67493 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$. Hence, the particle is neutron.

Assertion : For Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum, the value $n_1=2$ and $n_2=3,4,5 \ldots$.
Reason : The value of $n_2$ for a line in Balmer series of hydrogen spectrum having the highest wavelength is 6 .
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Ifboth assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
The wavelength of the line can be calculated by the Rydberg formula :
$$
\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right), R=\text { Rydberg constant }
$$
Therefore, wavelength will be highest in Balmer series $\left(n_1=2\right.$ ) when $n_2$ is 3 .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Nuclear binding energy per nucleon is in the order $-{ }_4^9 \mathrm{Be}>{ }_3^7 \mathrm{Li}>{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}$.
Reason : Binding energy per nucleon increases linearly with difference in number of neutrons and protons.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Binding energy per nucleon of ${ }_3 \mathrm{Li}^7(5.38$ $\mathrm{MeV})$ is lesser than ${ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4(7.08 \mathrm{MeV})$ as helium is found to be more stable than Li. As the atomic mass number increases, the binding energy per nucleon decreases. As the atomic number and the atomic mass number increase, the repulsive electrostatic forces within the nucleus increase due to the greater number of protons in the heavy elements. To overcome this increased repulsion, the proportion of neutrons in the nucleus must increase to maintain stability. This increase in the neutron-to-proton ratio only partially compensates for the growing proton-proton repulsive force in the heavier, naturally occurring elements. Because the repulsive forces are increasing, less energy must be supplied, on the average, to remove a nucleon from the nucleus. The binding energy per nucleon has decreased. The binding energy per nucleon of a nucleus is an indication of its degree of stability. Generally, the more stable nuclides have higher binding energy per nucleon than the less stable ones. The increase in binding energy per nucleon as the atomic mass number decreases from 260 to 60 is the primary reason for the energy liberation in the fission process. The increase in the binding energy per nucleon as the atomic mass number increases from 1 to 60 is the reason for the energy liberation in the fusion process, which is the opposite reaction of fission.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Quantum numbers of an atom can be defined on the basis of
questions.solution: Each electron in an atom is designated by a set of four quantum numbers. According to Pauli's exclusion principle, no two electron in an atom have same values of all the four quantum numbers. Therefore consequently, an orbital accommodates two electrons with opposite spins, these two electrons have the same value of quantum number ' $n$ ', ' $l$ ' and ' $m$ ' but value of ' $s$ ' will be different.

questions.solution:
Following the conservation of energy 
principle,
Kinetic energy $\left(\frac{1}{2} m_e v^2\right)=h\left(v-v_0\right)$
$=\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~s}\right)\left(1 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}-5 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\right)$
$=\left(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~s}\right)\left(5 \times 10^{13} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\right)$
$=3.313 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{~J}$

options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Absorption spectrum is produced when white light is passed through a substance and transmitted light is analysed by a spectrograph. The dark spaces corresponds to the light radiation absorbed by the substance. And emission spectrum is produced by analysing the radiant energy emitted by an excited substance by a spectrograph. Thus, discontinuous spectra consisting of a series of sharp lines and separated by dark bands are obtained.

$$

$$
Therefore, for calculating highest wavelength in Balmer series $\left(n_1=2\right), n_2$ should be 3 .

The ' $s$ ' value can be $\pm 1 / 2$
Hence the arrangement, $n=5, l=2, m=2, s=+1 / 2$ is possible for an electron.

& \operatorname{Be}(Z=4): 1 s^2, 2 s^2 \\
& \operatorname{B}(Z=5): 1 s^2, 2 s^2, 2 p^1
\end{aligned}
$$
Ionisation potential of $\mathrm{Be}$ is greater than that of boron as first electron released from $B e$ is from $s$-orbital while it is from $p$-orbital in boron.

\text { [m=mass, } v=\text { velocity }]
$$
or, $0.5=\frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times v^2$ or, $v=1 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$.
de Broglie wavelength, $\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}$
or, $\lambda=\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{1 \mathrm{~kg} \times 1 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}}=6.626 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{~m}$.

questions.q:
Assertion : Activity of an enzyme is $\mathrm{pH}$ dependent.
Reason : Change in $\mathrm{pH}$ affects the solubility of the enzyme in water.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Enzymes are proteins which act as catalyst for bio-chemical reactions. $\mathrm{pH}$ affect their activity as protonation-deprotonation may cause denaturation of the protein structure. Solubility is also affected.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Haemoglobin is an oxygen carrier. 
Reason : Oxygen binds as $\mathrm{O}_2^{-}$to $\mathrm{Fe}$ of haemoglobin.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Haemoglobin is the oxygen carrier in the human blood. It consists of four subunits and one molecule of haemoglobin can carry 4 molecules of $\mathrm{O}_2 . \mathrm{O}_2$ binds to $\mathrm{Fe}$ of heme part.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Enzymes with two sites are called
questions.solution: Enzymes with two active sites are called allosteric enzymes. One of these sites is the catalytic site for the concerned reaction. Other site may be the site for inhibition of catalysis by enzyme or to activate the enzyme further.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Assertion : The micelle formed by sodium stearate in water has $-\mathrm{COO}^{-}$groups at the surface. Reason : Surface tension of water is reduced by the addition of stearate.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Micelle is formed if molecules with polar and non-polar ends assemble in bulk to give nonpolar interior and polar exterior.

questions.subject: Chemistry

Reason : Soap reduces surface tension.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: For emulsification, soap is added in $\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{W}$ emulsion as it reduces surface tension and forms better colloid.

options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.

questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The size of colloidal particle is
questions.solution: A system is said to be in colloidal state if particles of one or more components have the same size range $10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}$ to $10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}$. In other words, the colloidal solution is a heterogenous dispersion of solute particles size ranging from $10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}$ into the solvent. Particles are visible only with an ultra microscope.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Physical absorption of molecules takes place on surface only.
Reason : In this process, the bonds of the absorbed molecules are broken.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: In physical absorption, the substance absorbed is retained not only on the surface but also more or less uniformly distributed throughout the entire body. And absorbed molecules are not broken under ordinary conditions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion: $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ can be used for coagulation of $\mathrm{As}_2 \mathrm{~S}_3$ sol.
Reason: $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ reacts with $\mathrm{As}_2 \mathrm{~S}_3$ to give $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{~S}_3$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Ifboth assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ is positively charged whereas $\mathrm{As}_2 \mathrm{~S}_3$ is negatively charged sol. Their charges are neutralised on mixing hence, mutual precipitation (coagulation) occurs.

questions.q: A packet of colloidal system is taken in which colloidal particles are still. Two electrodes are taken in system and voltage is applied so that liquid medium moves under the influence of electric field. This phenomenon is called
questions.solution: Electroosmosis : When electrophoresis of dispersed particles in a colloidal system is presented by some suitable means, it is observed that the dispersion medium itself begins to move in an electric field. This phenomenon is called as electroosmosis.

Reason : Stronger the inter-molecular attractive forces, fast is the rate of evaporation at a given temperature.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: More is the surface area, fast is the rate of evaporation at a given temperature this is due to the fact that more surface area means more number of molecules at the surface which can then be evaporated easily. Strong inter-molecular forces of attraction slows down the rate of evaporation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A catalyst
options: increases the forward and backward reactions at different speeds
follows same mechanism for the reaction.
questions.solution: A substance which increases the speed of a reaction without being consumed in the reaction is called a catalyst. Catalyst reduces the height of barrier by providing an alternative path for the reaction and lowers the activation energy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 1 mole of an ideal gas expands isothermally reversibly from 2 litre to 4 litre and 3 moles of the same gas expands from 2 litre to $x$ litre and doing same work, what is ' $x$ '?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } w=n R T \ln \frac{V_2}{V_1} \\ & R T \ln \frac{4}{2}=3 R T \ln \frac{x}{2} \\ & \ln 2=\ln \left(\frac{x}{2}\right)^3 \\ & x^3=16 \Rightarrow x=(16)^{\frac{1}{3}}=4^{\frac{2}{3}}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If one monoatomic gas is expanded adibatically from $2 \mathrm{~L}$ to $10 \mathrm{~L}$ at $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ external pressure then the value of $\Delta U$ (in atm $\mathrm{L}$ ) is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & Q=0 \quad(\because \text { process is adiabatic. }) \\ & \Delta U=w=-P_{\mathrm{ext}} \Delta V \\ & =-1(10-2) \text { atm } \mathrm{L}=-8 \text { atm L } \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the entropy change in $2 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~N}_2$, when its temperature is taken from $400 \mathrm{~K}$ to $800 \mathrm{~K}$, at constant pressure?
questions.solution: $\Delta S=n C_p \ln \frac{T_2}{T_1}=2 \times \frac{7}{2} R \times \ln \frac{800}{400}=40 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{K}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In isolated system, find the condition for spontaneous reaction.
questions.solution:
For an isolated system, $q=0, w=0$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \Delta U=0 \\ & \Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S\end{aligned}$
For spontaneous reaction, $\Delta G=-v$ e. $\Delta H=0$ for an isolated system.
Thus, $\Delta S>0$ for the reaction to be spontaneous.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: At $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, when 1 mole of butane is heated, $\mathrm{CO}_2$ and liquid $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ are formed, then the work done is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } \mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_{10(g)}+\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(l)} \\ & \Delta n_g=4-\left(1+\frac{13}{2}\right)=-\frac{7}{2} \\ & w=-\Delta n_g R T=-\left(-\frac{7}{2} \times 0.0821 \times 298\right) \\ & =85.63 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The heat is liberated when $1.89 \mathrm{~g}$ of benzoic acid is burnt in a bomb calorimeter at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and it increases the temperature of $18.94 \mathrm{~kg}$ of water by $0.632^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the specific heat of water at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $0.998 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{g}$-deg, then value of the heat of combustion of benzoic acid is
questions.solution:
Mass of benzoic acid $=1.89 \mathrm{~g}$
Temperature of bomb calorimeter
$=25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=298 \mathrm{~K}$
Mass of water $(m)=18.94 \mathrm{~kg}=18940 \mathrm{~g}$
[ncrease in temperature $(\Delta t)=0.632^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
Specific heat of water $(s)=0.998 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{g}-\mathrm{deg}$
Heat gained by water or heat liberated by benzoic $\operatorname{acid}(Q)=m s \Delta t$
$=18940 \times 0.998 \times 0.632=11946.14 \mathrm{cal}$
Since, $1.89 \mathrm{~g}$ of acid, liberates $11946.14 \mathrm{cal}$ of heat, therefore heat liberated by $122 \mathrm{~g}$ (mol.
$\begin{aligned} & \text { wt. of benzoic acid) of acid }=\frac{11946.14 \times 122}{1.89} \\ & =771126.5 \mathrm{cal} \approx 771.1 \mathrm{kcal}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Reaction, $A \rightleftharpoons B+3 C$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is in equilibrium. If equilibrium constant and Gibbs' free energy for this reaction are $y$ and $x$ respectively, then the Gibbs' free energy for reaction, $\frac{1}{2} A \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} B+\frac{3}{2} C$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathbf{} A \rightleftharpoons B+3 C \\ & K=\frac{[B][C]^3}{[A]} \\ & \Delta G^{\circ}=-R T \ln K=x\end{aligned}$
For the reaction, $\frac{1}{2} A \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2} B+\frac{3}{2} C$
$\begin{aligned} & K^{\prime}=\frac{[B]^{1 / 2}[C]^{3 / 2}}{[A]^{1 / 2}} \quad \text { or } \quad\left(K^{\prime}\right)^2=\frac{[B][C]^3}{[A]}=K \\ & \Delta G_2^{\circ}=-R T \ln K^{\prime}=-R T \ln \sqrt{K} \\ & =-\frac{1}{2} R T \ln K=\frac{x}{2}\end{aligned}$

If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : A reaction which is spontaneous and accompanied by decrease of randomness must be exothermic.
Reason : All exothermic reactions are accompanied by decrease of randomness.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
$\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S$
As the reaction is accompanied by decrease in randomness,
$\Delta G=\Delta H+T \Delta S \quad[\because \Delta S$ is negative $]$
For reaction to be spontaneous $\Delta G$ should be
negative which is possible only if reaction is highly exothermic i.e $\Delta H$ is negative. Exothermic reactions may be accompanied by increase or decrease of randomness.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A diatomic gas at pressure $P$, compressed adiabatically to half of its volume, what is the final pressure?
questions.solution:
For adiabatic conditions, $P V^\gamma=$ Constant
$P_1 V_1^\gamma=P_2 V_2^\gamma ; V_2=\frac{1}{2} V_1$
$P_2=P_1\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^\gamma \quad$ [For diatomic gas, $\gamma=1.4$ ]
$P_2=P_1\left(\frac{V_1 \times 2}{V_1}\right)^{1.4}$
$P_2=P_1(2)^{1.4}=(2)^{1.4} P$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Energy of activation of forward reaction for an endothermic process is $50 \mathrm{~kJ}$. If enthalpy change for forward reaction is $20 \mathrm{~kJ}$ then enthalpy change for backward reaction will be
questions.solution: For endothermic reaction, $E_{a, b}=E_{a, f}-\Delta H^{\circ}$ $=50 \mathrm{~kJ}-20 \mathrm{~kJ}=30 \mathrm{~kJ}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction of one mole of zinc dust with one mole of sulphuric acid in a bomb calorimeter, $\Delta U$ and $w$ correspond to
questions.solution:
Bomb calorimeter commonly used to find the heat of combustion of organic substances, consists of a sealed combustion chamber, called a bomb.
The reaction in a bomb calorimeter proceeds at constant volume so, $w=0$ and $\Delta U=q$. Hence, $\Delta U < 0, w=0$.

Reason : $\mathrm{Cl}_2$ has more electronic repulsion than $\mathrm{F}_2$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Bond dissociation energy is $\mathrm{F}_2 < \mathrm{Cl}_2$ because of relatively large electron-electron repulsion among the lone pairs in $\mathrm{F}_2$ molecule where they are much closer to each other than in $\mathrm{Cl}_2$.

If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.

For an isolated system,
$W=q=0$
$\Delta U=q+W$
Hence, $\Delta U=0$
$W=P \Delta V$
as $W=0 \mathrm{so}, \Delta V=0$

questions.solution: For adiabatic process, no exchange of heat takes place between the system and surroundings. i.e. $q=0$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Bond dissociation energy of $\mathrm{CH}_4$ is $360 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ and $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$ is $620 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. Then bond dissociation energy of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bond is
questions.solution:
Dissociation energy of methane $=$$360 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\therefore \quad$ Bond energy of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond $=\frac{360}{4}=90 \mathrm{~kJ}$ Bond energy of ethane,
$1(\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C})+6(\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H})=620 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$
$(C-C)+6 \times 90=620$
$(C-C)+540=620$
$C-C=620-540$
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}=80 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Bond dissociation of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{C}$ bond $=80 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.

questions.solution: Thermodynamic functions are : Internal energy, enthalpy, entropy, pressure, volume, temperature, free energy, number of moles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{CaO}$ and $\mathrm{NaCl}$ have the same crystal structure and approximately the same ionic radii. If $U$ is the lattice energy of $\mathrm{NaCl}$, the approximate lattice energy of $\mathrm{CaO}$ is
questions.solution:
$$
\text { Lattice energy }=-\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}
$$
where $q_1$ and $q_2$ are charges on ions and $r$ is the distance between them. Since interionic distances in $\mathrm{CaO}$ and $\mathrm{NaCl}$ are similar, (larger cation has smaller anion and vice versa) therefore, $r$ is almost the same. Therefore, lattice energy depends only on charge. Since the magnitude of charge on $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions is unity and that on $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions is 2 each, therefore, the lattice energy of $\mathrm{CaO}$ is four times the lattice energy of $\mathrm{NaCl}$, i.e., $4 U$.

From thermodynamics, $d G=V d P-S d T$
At constant $T, d T=0$ so that $\left(\frac{\delta G}{\delta P}\right)_T=V$
At constant $P, d P=0$ so that $\left(\frac{\delta G}{\delta T}\right)_P=-S$
$\mathrm{Also} \mu_{J T}=\left(\frac{\delta T}{\delta P}\right)_H$

Assertion : Bond energy and bond dissociation energy have identical value for diatomic molecules.
Reason : Greater the bond dissociation energy, less reactive is the bond.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Bond dissociation energy is the energy required to break a particular bond in one mole of a gaseous molecule. Bond energy is the average values of dissociation energies of the same type of bond present in the molecule. Bond energy of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond in methane is $99.2 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{mol}$. Thus for polyatomic molecules, average bond energy is taken as the dissociation bond energy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: \(\Delta H_{\text {fusion }}\) of a substance is ' \(x\) ' and \(\Delta H_{\text {vap }}\) is ' \(y\) ', then \(\Delta H_{\text {sublimation }}\) will be
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text {Solid } \stackrel{\text { fusion }}{\longrightarrow} \text { Liquid }\qquad \Delta H_{\text {fusion }}=x \\
& \text { Liquid } \stackrel{\text { vaporisation }}{\longrightarrow} \text { Gas } \qquad \Delta H_{\text {vap }}=y \\
& \text { Solid } \stackrel{\text { sublimation }}{\longrightarrow} \text { Gas } \qquad \Delta H_{\text {sub }} \\
&
\end{aligned}\)
So, \(\Delta H_{\text {sub }}=\Delta H_{\text {fusion }}+\Delta H_{\text {vap } \cdot}, \Delta H_{\text {sub }}=x+y\).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which thermodynamic parameter is not a state function?
questions.solution:
$H$ and $U$ are state functions but $W$ and $q$ are not state functions.
From the equation, $\Delta H=\Delta U+\Delta P V$ 
At constant pressure, $\Delta H=\Delta U+P \Delta V$ 
At constant volume, $\Delta H=\Delta U+V \Delta P$ 
At constant pressure, $\Delta P=0, \Delta H=q_p$
so, it is a state function.
At constant volume, $\Delta V=0, \Delta U=q_v$ so, it is a state function.
Work done in any adiabatic process is state function.
$\Delta U=q-W \quad(\because q=0)$
$\Delta U=-W$
Work done in isothermal process is not a state function.
$W=-q \quad(\because \Delta T=0, q \neq 0)$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a spontaneous process the correct statement is
options: entropy of the system always increases
free energy of the system always increases
total entropy change is always negative
total entropy change is always positive.
questions.solution: $\Delta S_{\text {total }}=\Delta S_{\text {system }}+\Delta S_{\text {surroundings }}$ For a spontaneous process $\Delta S_{\text {total }}$ must be positive. i.e. $\Delta S_{\text {total }}>0$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The enthalpy change $(\Delta H)$ for the reaction,
$$
\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}
$$
is $-92.38 \mathrm{~kJ}$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$. The internal energy change $\Delta U$ at $298 \mathrm{~K}$ is
questions.solution:
$\Delta H=\Delta U+\Delta n R T$
[where $\Delta n=$ no. of molecules of products
- no. of molecules of reactants]
or, $-92.38 \times 1000=\Delta U-2 \times 8.314 \times 298$
or, $\Delta U=-87424 \mathrm{~J}=-87.424 \mathrm{~kJ}$.
