questions.q:
Assertion : Water in liquid state is more stable than ice at room temperature.
Reason : Water in liquid form has higher entropy than ice.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true, but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution: Fusion of ice is a spontaneous process, because the process again proceeds in the direction of more random state, i.e. liquid state is more random (higher entropy) in comparison to solid state. i.e. ice.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\Delta H_f^{\circ}(298 \mathrm{~K})$ of methanol is given by the chemical equation

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction of one mole of zinc dust with one mole of sulphuric acid in a bomb calorimeter, $\Delta U$ and $w$ corresponds to
questions.solution:
Bomb calorimeter is commonly used to find the heat of combustion of organic substances which consists of a sealed combustion chamber, called a bomb. If a process is run in a sealed container then no expansion or compression is allowed, so $w=0$ and $\Delta U=q$.
$$
\Delta U < 0, w=0 .
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Entropy of system increases for a spontaneous reaction.
Reason : Enthalpy of reaction always decreases for spontaneous reaction.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: (a) : $\Delta S$ is + ve and $\Delta H$ is - ve for a spontaneous reaction at all temperatures.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How much energy is released when 6 moles of octane is burnt in air? Given $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ for $\mathrm{CO}_{2(g)}, \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})}$ and $\mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18(l)}$ respectively are $-490,-240$ and $+160 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$.
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{array}{r}
\text {  } \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta H_f^{\circ}=-490 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \times 8 \\
\mathrm{H}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} ; \Delta H_f^{\circ}=-240 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \times 9 \\
8 \mathrm{C}+18 \mathrm{H} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18} ; \Delta H_f^{\circ}=+160 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\
8 \mathrm{C}+8 \mathrm{O}_2+9 \mathrm{H}_2+\frac{9}{2} \mathrm{O}_2-8 \mathrm{C}-18 \mathrm{H} \\
\rightarrow 8 \mathrm{CO}_2+9 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18} \\
\Delta H_f^{\circ}=-3920-2160-160 \\
\mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_{18}+\frac{25}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow 8 \mathrm{CO}_2+9 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} ; \Delta H^{\circ}=-6240 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} \\
\Delta H^{\circ} \text { for } 6 \mathrm{moles} \text { of octane }=-6240 \times 6=-37440 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol} .
\end{array}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the equilibrium $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(l)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\mathrm{g})}$ at 1 atm and $298 \mathrm{~K}$,
options: standard free energy change is equal to zero $\left(\Delta G^{\circ}=0\right)$
free energy change is less than zero $(\Delta G < 0)$
standard free energy change is less than zero $\left(\Delta G^{\circ} < 0\right)$
standard free energy change is greater than zero $\left(\Delta G^{\circ}>0\right)$.
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(l)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(g)}$
This is a spontaneous process at standard conditions thus, the standard free energy change must be less then zero, i.e. $\Delta G^{\circ} < 0$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Molar entropy of vaporisation of water is different from ethanol.
Reason : Water is more polar than ethanol.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Molar entropy of vaporization of water is more than ethanol, as water has greater forces of attraction than ethanol.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: One gram sample of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3$ is decomposed in a bomb calorimeter. The temperature of the calorimeter increases by $6.12 \mathrm{~K}$. The heat capacity of the system is $1.23 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{deg}$. What is the molar heat of decomposition for $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3$ ?
questions.solution:
Heat of decomposition, $\Delta E=m s \Delta T$
$$
=1 \times 1.23 \times 6.12=7.5276 \mathrm{~kJ} \text {. }
$$
Molar heat of decomposition for $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3$
$$
=7.5276 \times 80=602.2 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The potential energy diagram for a reaction $R \rightarrow P$ is given in the figure. $\Delta H^{\circ}$ of the reaction corresponds to the energy
questions.solution:
$\Delta H=$ activation energy of forward reaction - activation energy of backward reaction
$$
\therefore \quad \Delta H=b-a=c \text {. }
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : The increase in internal energy $(\Delta E)$ for the vapourization of one mole of water at $1 \mathrm{~atm}$ and $373 \mathrm{~K}$ is zero.
Reason : For all isothermal processes, $\Delta E=0$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
At $373 \mathrm{~K}$ or $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the water starts boiling and at this temperature the vaporization of water is an isothermal process.
The internal energy change $(\Delta E)$, depend only on temperature, it follows that at constant temperature, the internal energy of the gas remains constant, i.e. $\Delta E$ is zero.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The heat of neutralization of a strong base and a strong acid is $57 \mathrm{~kJ}$. The heat released when 0.5 mole of $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ solution is added to 0.20 moles of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution, is
questions.solution:
Heat of neutralization $(\Delta H)=57 \mathrm{~kJ}$, mole of $\mathrm{HNO}_3=0.5$ mole and mole of $\mathrm{NaOH}=0.2$ mole. When $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ solution is added to $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution, then 0.2 mole of $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ solution will combine with 0.2 mole of $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ions of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution.
$\therefore \quad$ Heat released $=\Delta H \times 0.2=57 \times 0.2=11.4 \mathrm{~kJ}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : During an adiabatic process, heat energy is not exchanged between system and its surroundings.
Reason : The temperature of a gas increases when it undergoes an adiabatic expansion.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
In adiabatic process, no heat enters or leaves the system. The system is completely insulated from its surroundings. From first law of thermodynamics, change in internal energy,
$$
\Delta E=q-W=0-W=-W .
$$
Since the work is done at the expense of internal energy, therefore internal energy of the system decreases and hence temperature of the gas falls.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Mass and volume are extensive properties.
Reason : Mass/volume is also an extensive parameter.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Extensive properties are dependent upon the amount of the substance. .e.g. mass, volume etc.
Intensive properties are independent of the amount of the substance. e.g. temperature, density.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In an isobaric process, when temperature changes from $T_1$ to $T_2, \Delta S$ is equal to
questions.solution:
The entropy change for a process, when $T$ and $P$ are the variables is given by
$\Delta S=C_P \ln \frac{T_2}{T_1}-R \ln \frac{P_2}{P_1}$
For an isobaric process $P_1=P_2$. Hence the above equation reduces to
$C_P \ln \frac{T_2}{T_1}=\Delta S$
or $\Delta S=2.303 C_P \log \frac{T_2}{T_1}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : $\Delta H$ and $\Delta E$ are almost the same for the reaction, $\mathrm{N}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g}$.
Reason : All reactants and products are gases.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true and the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
$\Delta H=\Delta E+\Delta n R T$
$\Delta n=$ change in number of moles of products and reactant species.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{N}_{2(g)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_{(g)} \\
\Delta n= & 2-2=0 \\
\Delta H= & \Delta E+0 \times R T \text { or, } \Delta H=\Delta E .
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Absolute values of internal energy of substances can not be determined.
Reason : It is impossible to determine exact values of constituent energies of the substances.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Internal energy is given by :
$E=E_{\text {translational }}+E_{\text {rotaional }}+E_{\text {vibrational }}+E_{\text {bonding }}+E_{\text {electronic }}$
Accurate measurement of some forms of energy which contribute to the absolute value of internal energy for a given substance in a given state is impossible. So difference of the initial state and final state is calculated and is denoted by
$$
\Delta E=E_f-E_i
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is always feasible?
options: $\Delta H$ (-ve), $T \Delta S$ (+ve) and $\Delta H < T \Delta S$
questions.solution:
Feasibility of reaction is determined by free energy change value $(\Delta G) . \Delta G$ is given by Gibbs - Helmholtz equation :
$$
\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S
$$
Reaction is feasible if $\Delta G$ value is negative. Therefore, if $\Delta H$ is negative and $T \Delta S$ is positive then the $\Delta G$ will always be feasible.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Given that $\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta H^{\circ}=-a \mathrm{~kJ}$.
$$
2 \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta H^{\circ}=-b \mathrm{~kJ}
$$
The heat of formation of $\mathrm{CO}$ is
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& : \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta H^{\circ}=-a \mathrm{~kJ} \\
& 2 \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta H^{\circ}=-b \mathrm{~kJ}
\end{aligned}
$$
Formation of $\mathrm{CO}$ can be written as :
$$
\mathrm{C}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{CO} ; \Delta H^{\circ}=?
$$
Adding equation: $\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2 ; \Delta H^{\circ}=-a \mathrm{~kJ}$
$$
\mathrm{CO}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 ; \Delta H^{\circ}=\frac{b}{2} \mathrm{~kJ}
$$
We get
$$
\Delta H_f^{\circ}(\mathrm{CO})=\frac{b}{2}-a=\frac{b-2 a}{2}
$$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: \(\Delta S_{\text {surr }}\) for an exothermic reaction is
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : Many endothermic reactions that are not spontaneous at room temperature become spontaneous at high temperature.
Reason : Entropy of the system increases with increase in temperature.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The factor $T \Delta S$ increases with increase in temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Assertion : As a salt such as \(\mathrm{NaCl}\) dissolves, the \(\mathrm{Na}^{+}\)and \(\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\)ions leaving the crystal lattice acquire far greater freedom.
Reason : In thermodyanamic terms, the formation of solution occurs with a favourable change in free energy, i.e., \(\Delta H\) has a high positive value and \(T \Delta S\) a low negative value.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
In thermodynamic terms, formation of solution occurs with a favourable change in free energy
\(\Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S\)
where \(\Delta H\) has a small positive value and \(T \Delta S\) a large positive value; thus \(\Delta G\) is negative.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For a phase change
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
(l)
(s)
questions.solution:
$\Delta G^{\circ}=-R T \ln K$
[ $\Delta G^{\circ}=$ standard free energy change, $K=$ equilibrium constant]
If a substance is in equilibrium between two phases at constant temperature and pressure, its chemical potential must have the same value in both the phases.
$$
\therefore \Delta G^{\circ}=0 \text {. }

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following has $\Delta S^{\circ}$ greater than zero?
questions.solution: Here, the number of product species is greater than the number of reactant species. So it leads to positive value of entropy.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following are extensive properties?
questions.solution: Extensive properties depend upon the quantity of the matter contained in the system. e.g., volume and enthalpy, etc. Intensive properties depend only upon the nature of the substance and are independent of the amount of the substance present in the system e.g., temperature, pressure and specific heat, etc.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Calculate change in internal energy if \(\Delta H=-92.2 \mathrm{~kJ}, P=40 \mathrm{~atm}\) and \(\Delta V=-1 \mathrm{~L}\).
questions.solution:
\(\Delta H=\Delta E+P \Delta V\)
\(\begin{aligned}
\Delta E & =\Delta H-P \Delta V=-92.2-40 \times(-1) \times 101 \times 10^{-3} \\
& =-92.2+4.04=-88.16 \mathrm{~kJ} \approx-88 \mathrm{~kJ} .
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transformer with efficiency $80 \%$ works at $4 \mathrm{~kW}$ and $100 \mathrm{~V}$. If the secondary voltage is $200 \mathrm{~V}$, then the primary and secondary currents are respectively
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { (a) : Here, } \eta=80 \% \\ & P_i=4 \mathrm{~kW}=4000 \mathrm{~W} \\ & V_p=100 \mathrm{~V}, V_s=200 \mathrm{~V} \\ & I_p=\frac{P_i}{V_p}=\frac{4000}{100}=40 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \eta=\frac{V_s I_s}{V_p I_p} ; \frac{80}{100}=\frac{200 I_s}{4000}=\frac{I_s}{20} \\ & \text { or } I_s=\frac{20 \times 80}{100}=16 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a transformer number of turns in primary circuit is 500 and in secondary circuit number of turns is 10 . The load resistance is $10 \Omega$ and voltage of secondary coil is $50 \mathrm{~V}$. Then find the current in primary circuit.
questions.solution:
Given, $N_1=500, N_2=10, R_L=10 \Omega$
$V_2=50 \mathrm{~V}$
Current in the secondary circuit is
$\begin{aligned} & i_2=\frac{V_2}{R_L}=\frac{50}{10}=5 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \frac{i_1}{i_2}=\frac{N_2}{N_1} \Rightarrow \frac{i_1}{5}=\frac{10}{500} \Rightarrow i_1=0.1 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : At resonance, $L C R$ series circuit have a minimum current.
Reason : At resonance, in LCR series circuit, the current and e.m.f are not in phase with each other.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
At resonance $X_L=X_C$ or $\omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}$.
Because of this impedance of $L C R$ series
circuit become equal to resistance of circuit
$\left(Z=\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}\right)$.
Therefore from $I=\frac{E}{Z}=\frac{E}{R}$, at resonance, current in LCR series circuit is maximum. Correspondingly phase angle is also equal to zero. Therefore emf and current are in phase in $L C R$ series circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A series resonant $L C R$ circuit has a quality factor (Q-factor) 0.4 . If $R=2 \mathrm{k} \Omega, C=0.1 \mu \mathrm{F}$, then the value of inductance is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  } Q=\frac{1}{R} \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} \text { or } \frac{L}{C}=(Q R)^2 \\ & \therefore \quad L=\left(0.4 \times 2 \times 10^3\right)^2 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}=0.064 \mathrm{H}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In an $L C R$ circuit as shown in figure, both switches are open initially. Now switch $S_1$ is closed, $S_2$ kept open. ( $q$ is charge on the capacitor and $\tau=R C$ is capacitive time constant).
Which of the following statement is correct?
options: Work done by the battery is half of the energy dissipated in the resistor
questions.solution:
As switch $S_1$ is closed and switch $S_2$ is kept open. Now, capacitor is charging through a resistor $R$. Charge on a capacitor at any time $t$ is $q=q_0\left(1-e^{-t / \tau}\right)=C V\left(1-e^{-t / \tau}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { At } t=\frac{\tau}{2}, q=C V\left(1-e^{-\tau / 2 \tau}\right)=C V\left(1-e^{-1 / 2}\right) \\ & \text { At } t=\tau, q=C V\left(1-e^{-\tau / \tau}\right)=C V\left(1-e^{-1}\right) \\ & \text { At } t=2 \tau, q=C V\left(1-e^{-2 \tau / \tau}\right)=C V\left(1-e^{-2}\right)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The voltage across a pure inductor is represented in figure. Which one of the following curves in the figure will represent the current?
questions.solution: Current lags behind the voltage by $\frac{\pi}{2}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a $L C R$ oscillatory circuit, find the energy stored in inductor at resonance if voltage of source is $10 \mathrm{~V}$, resistance is $10 \Omega$ and inductance $=1 \mathrm{H}$.
questions.solution: At resonance, current $I=\frac{V}{Z_{\min }}=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{10}{10}=1 \mathrm{~A}$ Energy of inductor $=\frac{1}{2} L I^2=\frac{1}{2}(1)(1)^2=0.5 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric bulb has a rated power of $50 \mathrm{~W}$ at $100 \mathrm{~V}$. If it is used on an AC source of $200 \mathrm{~V}$, $50 \mathrm{~Hz}$, a choke has to be used in series with it. This choke should have an inductance of
questions.solution:
Here, $P=50 \mathrm{~W}, V=100 \mathrm{~V}$
$I=\frac{P}{V}=\frac{50}{100}=0.5 \mathrm{~A}, R=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{100}{0.5}=200 \Omega$
Let $L$ be the inductance of the choke coil
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad I_v=\frac{E_v}{Z} \text { or } Z=\frac{E_v}{I_v}=\frac{200}{0.5}=400 \Omega \\
& =100 \sqrt{12} \Omega \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, $X_L=\sqrt{Z^2-R^2}=\sqrt{400^2-200^2}$
$=100 \sqrt{12} \Omega$
or, $\omega L=100 \times 2 \sqrt{3} \Omega$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow L=\frac{200 \sqrt{3}}{\omega}=\frac{200 \sqrt{3}}{2 \pi v}=\frac{200 \sqrt{3}}{100 \pi} & =\frac{2 \times 1.732}{3.14} \\ & =1.1 \mathrm{H}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In LCR series circuit, source voltage is $120 \mathrm{~V}$ and voltage across inductor $50 \mathrm{~V}$. If voltage drop across resistance is $40 \mathrm{~V}$, then determine voltage in capacitor.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & 120^2=40^2+\left(V_C-50\right)^2 \\ & V_C=40 \sqrt{8}+50=10(5+8 \sqrt{2}) \mathrm{V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The most suitable metal for making electromagnets and transformer cores is
questions.solution: Iron is most suitable for electromagnets and transformers, because of low energy losses.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an oscillating system, a restoring force is a must. In an $L-C$ circuit, restoring force is provide by
questions.solution: Restoring force is produced by inductor as it acts as a source of energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an $A C$ circuit, voltage $V=V_0 \sin \omega t$ and inductor $L$ is connected across the circuit. Then the instantaneous power will be
questions.solution:
If $\frac{d I}{d t}$ is the rate of change of current through inductor $L$ of any instant, then induced emf in the inductor of the same instant is $=\left(-\frac{L d I}{d t}\right)$ To maintain the flow of current in the circuit applied voltage must be equal and opposite to the induced voltage. $\therefore \quad V=-\left(-L \frac{d I}{d t}\right) \Subset$ Given, $V=V_0 \sin \omega t$
$\therefore \quad V_0 \sin \omega t=\frac{L d I}{d t}$ $\frac{d I}{d t}=\frac{V_0}{L} \sin \omega t$ or $I=\frac{-V_0}{L} \frac{\cos \omega t}{\omega}$
Instantaneous power, $\because \quad P=V I$ $=\left(V_0 \sin \omega t\right)\left(-\frac{V_0 \cos \omega t}{L \omega}\right) \quad(\mathrm{Using}$ (i) \& (ii)) $=\frac{-V_0^2 \sin 2 \omega t}{2 L \omega}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductive circuit contains a resistance of $10 \Omega$ and an inductance of $2 \mathrm{H}$. If an $\mathrm{AC}$ voltage of $120 \mathrm{~V}$ and frequency $60 \mathrm{~Hz}$ is applied to this circuit, the current would be nearly
questions.solution:
Here, $R=10 \Omega, L=2 \mathrm{H}$,
$V=120 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{v}=60 \mathrm{~Hz}$
$X_L=\omega L=2 \pi v L=2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 60 \times 2=754.3 \Omega$
$Z=\sqrt{R^2+X_L^2}=\sqrt{10^2+(754.3)^2}=754.35 \Omega$
$I_V=\frac{V}{Z}=\frac{120}{754.3} \mathrm{~A}=0.16 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a given series $L C R$ circuit $R=4 \Omega, X_L=5 \Omega$ and $X_C=8 \Omega$, the current
questions.solution:
The phase angle
$\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{X_C-X_L}{R}\right)$
$=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{8-5}{4}\right)$
$\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)$
Therefore, the current leads the voltage by $\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Alternating current cannot be measured by D.C. ammeter because
options: A.C. cannot pass through D.C. ammeter
average value of current for complete cycle is zero
questions.solution: Average value of A.C. for complete cycle is zero. Hence A.C. can not be measured by D.C. ammeter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the mechanical equivalent of spring constant $k$ in LC oscillating circuit?
questions.solution: The mechanical equivalent of spring constant in $L C$ oscillating circuit is, $k=\frac{1}{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following parameter in the series LCR circuit is analogous to driving force $F(t)$ in mechanics?
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The core of any transformer is laminated so as $\mathrm{to}$
options: reduce the energy loss due to eddy currents
questions.solution: The energy loss due to eddy currents is reduced by using laminated core in a transformer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a series $L C R$ circuit, the voltage across the resistance, capacitance and inductance is $10 \mathrm{~V}$ each. If the capacitance is short circuited the voltage across the inductance will be
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text {  As } V_R=V_L=V_C \\ & \mathrm{R}=X_L=X_{C,} \Rightarrow Z=R \\ & V=I R=10 \text { volt }\end{aligned}$
When capacitor is short circuited,
$Z=\sqrt{R^2+X_L^2}=\sqrt{R^2+R^2}=R \sqrt{2}$
New current $I^{\prime}=V / Z=V / R \sqrt{2}=\frac{10}{R \sqrt{2}}$
Potential drop across inductance
$=I^{\prime} X_L=I^{\prime} R=\frac{10 \times R}{R \sqrt{2}}=\frac{10}{\sqrt{2}}$ volt

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an \(A C\) circuit the potential differences across an inductance and resistance joined in series are respectively \(16 \mathrm{~V}\) and \(20 \mathrm{~V}\). The total potential difference of the source is
questions.solution:
\(V_L=16 \mathrm{~V}, V_R=20 \mathrm{~V}\). In the inductor the current lags by \(\frac{\pi}{2}\).
\(\begin{array}{ll} \therefore \quad V=\sqrt{V_L^2+V_R^2}=\sqrt{256+400} \\ \therefore \quad V_{\text {total }}=25.6 \mathrm{~V} . \end{array}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion: Transformers are used only in alternating current source not in direct current.
Reason : Only a.c. can be stepped up or down by means of transformers.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Transformers which convert low voltages into higher ones are called step-up transformers, while those which convert high voltages into lower ones are called step-down transformers. This is possible only for a.c. source and not for d.c. source.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Given below is the circuit diagram of an AM demodulator. For good demodulation of AM signal of carrier frequency $f$, the value of $R C$ should be
questions.solution:
For good demodulation,
$$
\frac{1}{f} \ll R C \text { or, } R C \gg \frac{1}{f} \text {. }
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : No power loss associated with pure capacitor in ac circuit.
Reason : No current is flowing in this circuit.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Assertion is true. When a capacitor is connected to an a.c. circuit, \(\varepsilon_{r m s}=i_{r m s}\left(\frac{1}{C \omega}\right)\) where \(\frac{1}{C \omega}\) is the impedance due to capacitative resistance. The power consumed in this circuit is zero because the phase difference \(\phi\) between \(\mathrm{emf}\) and current in a pure capacitance a.c. circuit is \(\frac{\pi}{2}\). Power factor \(\cos \phi=0\). It is only for d.c. that a current does not flow. Reason is false.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $50 \mathrm{~Hz}$ a.c. source of 20 volts is connected across $R$ and $C$ as shown in figure. The voltage across $R$ is 12 volt. The voltage across $C$ is
options: not possible to determine unless values of $R$ and $C$ are given.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A capacitor of suitable capacitance can be used in an AC circuit in place of the choke coil.
Reason : A capacitor blocks DC and allows AC only.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: We can use a capacitor of suitable capacitance as a choke coil, because average power consumed in an ideal capacitor is zero. Therefore, like a choke coil, a condenser can reduce $\mathrm{AC}$ without power dissipation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil of inductance $8 \mu \mathrm{H}$ is connected to a capacitor of capacitance $0.02 \mu \mathrm{F}$. To what wavelength is this circuit tuned?
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Here, } L=8 \mu \mathrm{H}=8 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{H} \text {; } \\
& C=0.02 \mu \mathrm{F}=0.02 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F} \\
& \therefore \text { Resonant frequency, } \\
& f_r=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L C}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{8 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.02 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
& =3.98 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~Hz} \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
If $c\left(=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\right)$ is the velocity of the electromagnetic wave, then,
Wavelength, $\lambda=\frac{c}{f}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{3.98 \times 10^5}=7.54 \times 10^2 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A series combination of resistor $(R)$ and capacitor $(C)$ is connected to an AC source of angular frequency $\omega$. Keeping the voltage same, if the frequency is changed to $\frac{\omega}{3}$, the current becomes half of the original current. Then the ratio of the capacitive reactance and resistance at the former frequency is
questions.solution:
In the first case, capacitive reactance,
$X_C=\frac{1}{\omega C}$
Impedance of the circuit, $Z=\sqrt{R^2+X_C^2}$
Current in the circuit, $I=\frac{V}{Z}=\frac{V}{\sqrt{R^2+X_C^2}} \ldots$ (i)
In the second case, $\omega^{\prime}=\frac{\omega}{3}$
$\therefore \quad$ Capacitive reactance,
$X_C^{\prime}=\frac{1}{\omega^{\prime} C}=\frac{1}{\frac{\omega}{3} C}=\frac{3}{\omega C}=3 X_C$
Impedance of the circuit,
$Z^{\prime}=\sqrt{R^2+X_C^{\prime 2}}=\sqrt{R^2+9 X_C^2}$
Current in the circuit,
$\frac{I}{2}=\frac{V}{Z^{\prime}}=\frac{V}{\sqrt{R^2+9 X_C^2}}$
Divide (ii) by (i), we get
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{R^2+X_C^2}}{\sqrt{R^2+9 X_C^2}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}=\frac{R^2+X_C^2}{R^2+9 X_C^2} \\ & 5 X_C^2=3 R^2 \text { or } \frac{X_C}{R}=\sqrt{\frac{3}{5}}=\sqrt{0.6}\end{aligned}$
