questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting ring of radius 1 meter is placed in a uniform magnetic field $B$ of 0.01 tesla oscillating with frequency $100 \mathrm{~Hz}$ with its plane at right angle to $B$. What will be the induced electric field?
questions.solution:
As a constant magnetic field conducting ring oscillates with a frequency of $100 \mathrm{~Hz}$. i.e. $T=\frac{1}{100} \mathrm{~s}$, for $\frac{T}{2}$, it goes up to $B$ $\therefore$ The corresponding frequency is $200 \mathrm{~Hz}$. $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Induced emf }=\frac{\text { change in flux }}{\text { time }} \\ & =\frac{2 B A \cos \theta}{T}=2 B A f \cos \theta \\ & =\pi r^2 \times 2 \times 0.01 \times 200 \text { as } r=1 \mathrm{~m} \\ & =\pi 1^2 \times 2 \times 0.01 \times 200=4 \pi \mathrm{V} . \end{aligned} $$
Non-electrostatic induced electric field along the circle, $$ E=\frac{1}{2 \pi r} \times\left(\pi r^2 \times \frac{d B}{d t}\right)=\frac{e}{2 \pi r}=\frac{4 \pi}{2 \pi r}=2 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m} . $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A magnet is made to oscillate with a particular frequency, passing through a coil as shown in figure. The time variation of the magnitude of e.m.f. generated across the coil during one cycle is
questions.solution: As the north pole approaches, a north pole is developed at that face i.e. the current flows anticlockwise. Finally when it completes the oscillation, when it is ready, no emf is present. Now south pole approaches the other side R.H.S. the current flows clockwise to repel the south pole. This means the current is anticlockwise at the L.H.S. as before. This is possible in (1). The breaks show when the pendulum is at the extreme and momentarily stationary.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an ideal parallel $L C$ circuit, the capacitor is charged by connecting it to a d.c. source which is then disconnected. The current in the circuit
questions.solution: When the capacitor is connected to a d.c. source and then disconnected, it gets charged and then it starts discharging through the inductor. An induced emf is produced in the circuit which opposes the growth of current in $L$. When the capacitor is fully discharged, the electric energy stored in the capacitor (i.e. $1 / 2 q^{2 / C}$ ) gets converted fully into magnetic field energy $\left(1 / 2 L I^2\right)$. As soon as the discharge of the capacitor is complete, current stops and the magnetic flux linked with $L$ starts collapsing. Therefore, an induced emf is again developed which starts recharging the capacitor in the opposite direction. When the recharging is completed, all magnetic energy stored in $L$ appears as the electric energy between the plates of the capacitor. The entire process keeps on repeating and the energy taken from the cell keeps on oscillating between $C$ and $L$. Hence the current in the circuit starts oscillating as soon as the d.c. source charging the capacitor is disconnected.

questions.solution:
Change in magnetic flux $(\phi)=2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~Wb}$ and change in current $(I)=0.01 \mathrm{~A}$.
Change in magnetic flux, $\phi=2 \times 10^{-2}=M I$
$$
=M \times 0.01 \quad \text { or } M=\frac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{0.01}=2 \mathrm{H}
$$
(where $M=$ coefficient of mutual inductance).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Only a change in magnetic flux will maintain an induced current in the coil.
Reason : The presence of large magnetic flux through a coil maintains a current in the coil if the circuit is continuous.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Induced current (emf) in a coil is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux linked with the coil. If there is no change in the flux, there is no induced current also.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : The quantity $L / R$ possesses dimension of time.
Reason : To reduce the rate of increase of current through a solenoid, we should increase the time constant $(L / R)$.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Induced e.m.f. $(\varepsilon)=\frac{L d i}{d t}$
and current $(i)=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{1}{R} \times \frac{L d i}{d t}$
$$
\text { or, } \frac{L}{R}=\frac{i d t}{d i}=\frac{\text { current } \times \text { time }}{\text { current }}=\text { time. }
$$
In order to increase the rate of increase of current through a solenoid, we increase the time constant $(L / R)$. Since $L$ of the given solenoid is constant, therefore we use a high resistance in series with it.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For MRI, a patient is slowly pushed in a time of $10 \mathrm{~s}$ within the coils of the magnet where magnetic field is $B=2.0 \mathrm{~T}$. If the patient's trunk is $0.8 \mathrm{~m}$ in circumference, the induced emf around the patient's trunk is
questions.solution:
Change in magnetic field in $10 \mathrm{~s}=2.0 \mathrm{~T}$
As $\varepsilon=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}=-A \frac{d B}{d t}$
$(\because \phi=B A)$
Circumference of patient's trunk, $2 \pi r=0.8 \mathrm{~m}$ (given)
$\therefore \quad r=\frac{0.8}{2 \pi} \mathrm{m}=\frac{0.4}{\pi} \mathrm{m}$
Areaofcrosssection, $A=\pi r^2=\pi\left(\frac{0.4}{\pi}\right)^2=\frac{0.16}{\pi} \mathrm{m}^2$
$\therefore|\varepsilon|=\frac{0.16}{\pi} \times \frac{2}{10} \mathrm{~V} \approx 10.18 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V}$

options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Faraday's laws involve conservation of mechanical energy into electrical energy. This is in accordance with the law of conservation of energy. We also know that in pure resistance, the e.m.f. is in phase with the current.

It is clear that mutual inductance of coils can be increased by increasing the number of turns in the coils.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: With the decrease of current in the primary coil from 2 amperes to zero value in \(0.01 \mathrm{~s}\) the emf generated in the secondary coil is 1000 volts. The mutual inductance of the two coils is
questions.solution:
Induced \(\operatorname{emf} \varepsilon=\frac{-M d I_P}{d t}\)
Where \(M\) is the mutual inductance of two coils and \(\frac{d I_P}{d t}\) is the rate of change of current in the primary.
\(\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad 1000 \mathrm{~V}=\frac{-M(0 \mathrm{~A}-2 \mathrm{~A})}{0.01 \mathrm{~s}} \\
& \text { or } M=\frac{(1000 \mathrm{~V})(0.01 \mathrm{~s})}{(2 \mathrm{~A})}=5 \mathrm{H} .
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular coil of radius $10 \mathrm{~cm}, 500$ turns and resistance $2 \Omega$ is placed with its plane perpendicular to the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field. It is rotated about its vertical diameter through $180^{\circ}$ in $0.25 \mathrm{~s}$. The current induced in the coil is (Horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field at that place is $3.0 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$ )
questions.solution:
Initial magnetic flux through the coil,
$\begin{aligned} \phi_i & =B_H A \cos \theta=3.0 \times 10^{-5} \times\left(\pi \times 10^{-2}\right) \times \cos 0^{\circ} \\ & =3 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~Wb}\end{aligned}$
Final magnetic flux after the rotation
$\begin{aligned} \phi_f & =3.0 \times 10^{-5} \times\left(\pi \times 10^{-2}\right) \times \cos 180^{\circ} \\ & =-3 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~Wb}\end{aligned}$
Induced emf, $\varepsilon=-N \frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{N\left(\phi_f-\phi_i\right)}{t}$
$\begin{aligned} & =-\frac{500\left(-3 \pi \times 10^{-2}-3 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right)}{0.25} \\ & =\frac{500 \times\left(6 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right)}{0.25}=3.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V} \\ & I=\frac{\varepsilon}{R}=\frac{3.8 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V}}{2 \Omega}=1.9 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

Assertion : The energy of X-ray photon is greater than that of light (visible) photon.
Reason : X-ray photon in vacuum travels faster than light photon.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Energy of a photon $E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}$. As $\lambda$ for X-ray is smaller than light, so energy of X-rays is greater than that of light. Therefore, assertion is true. Here, reason is false as X-rays and light travel with same speed in vacuum.

questions.solution: In physics (namely astrophysics), redshift happens when light or other electromagnetic radiation from an object moving away from the observer is increased in wavelength or shifted to the red end of the spectrum.

questions.q: Assume that a radio station is about $200 \mathrm{~km}$ away from your location and the station operates at $972 \mathrm{kHz}$. How long does it take for an electromagnetic signal to travel from the station to you and how many wave crests does it send out per second?
questions.solution:
Time taken by wave to travel
$=\frac{200 \mathrm{~km}}{3 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}=6.667 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~s} \approx 666 \mu \mathrm{s}$
Number of crests per second $=$ Frequency
Frequency $=972 \mathrm{kHz}=9.72 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~Hz}$
$\therefore \quad$ There are $9.72 \times 10^5$ crests per second.

questions.q: Solid targets of different elements are bombarded by highly en ergetic electron beams. The frequency (f) of the characteristic X-rays emitted from different targets varies with atomic number $Z$ as
questions.solution:
According to Moseley's law
$(Z-\sigma) \propto \sqrt{f} \quad$ or, $f \propto(Z-\sigma)^2$.
Note : The correct answer should have been $f \propto(Z-\sigma)^2$ where $\sigma$ is the screening constant. Many authors use at this level, $\sigma=1$ approximately.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Velocity of light is equal to
questions.solution:
Relation for velocity of light $(c)=\sqrt{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0 \mu_0}}$ (where $\varepsilon_0=$ permittivity constant equal to $8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~F} / \mathrm{m}$ and $\mu_0=$ permeability constant equal to $1.26 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{H} / \mathrm{m}$.
Numerically velocity of light $(c)=\sqrt{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0 \mu_0}}$
$$
=\sqrt{\frac{1}{\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right) \times\left(1.26 \times 10^{-6}\right)}} \approx 3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \text {. }
$$

Assertion : A laser beam of $0.2 \mathrm{~W}$ power can drill holes through a metal sheet, whereas a $1000 \mathrm{~W}$ torch-light cannot.
Reason : The frequency of laser light is much higher than that of torch light.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.

questions.solution: A laser is a highly monochromatic and nearperfect parallel beam of light, due to which the beam can be focussed by a converging lens to a very small spot. As the intensity of the beam is too high, it can drill holes through a metal sheet even if the power is $0.2 \mathrm{~W}$. But even a torch-light of $1000 \mathrm{~W}$ power cannot drill holes in such a metal sheet, because the light is less intense and the beam is not parallel.

questions.q: Characteristic X-rays are produced due to
options: transfer of momentum in collision of electrons with target atoms
transition of electron from higher to lower electrons orbits in an atom
transfer of energy in collision of electrons with atoms in the target.
questions.solution: When the high energy electron hits the target and takes out an electron from the inner orbit, there is a possibility of an outer electron to fall down to fill up that vacancy. And when such a transition of electron takes place from a higher energy level to a lower energy level, the difference of energy between the two levels is given off in the form of radiation known as characteristic X-rays.

questions.q: The charge of a parallel plate capacitor is varying as $q=q_0 \sin 2 \pi v t$. The plates are very large and close together. Neglecting edge effects, the displacement current through the capacitor is
questions.solution: $I=\frac{d q}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(q_0 \sin 2 \pi v t\right)=q_0 2 \pi v \cos 2 \pi v t$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four point charges $-Q,-q, 2 q$ and $2 Q$ are placed, one at each corner of the square. The relation between $Q$ and $q$ for which the potential at the centre of the square is zero is
questions.solution: Let $a$ be the side length of the square $A B C D$. $\begin{aligned} & A C=B D=\sqrt{a^2+a^2}=a \sqrt{2} \\ & O A=O B=O C=O D=\frac{a \sqrt{2}}{2}=\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\end{aligned}$ Potential is a scalar quantity. Potential at the centre $O$ due to given charge configuration is $\begin{aligned} & V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{(-Q)}{\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}+\frac{(-q)}{\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}+\frac{(2 q)}{\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}+\frac{2 Q}{\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}\right]=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-Q-q+2 q+2 Q=0 \\ & \text { or } Q+q=0 \text { or } Q=-q\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long solid non-conducting cylinder of radius $R$ is charged such that the volume charge density is proportional to $r$ where $r$ is the distance from axis. The electric field $E$ at a distance $r(r < R)$ will depend on $r$ as
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & (\mathbf{b}): \rho \propto r ; \rho=K r \\ & d Q=\rho V \\ & \quad=K r(2 \pi r d r) \times l\end{aligned}$ Using Gauss's Law, $\begin{aligned} & E .2 \pi r l=\frac{\int_0^r K r .2 \pi r l . d r}{\varepsilon_0} \\ & \Rightarrow E=K \frac{r^2}{3 \varepsilon_0} \\ & \therefore E \propto r^2\end{aligned}$

Reason : Flow of charge between two bodies connected by a thin wire is determined by the charges on them.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Charge flows from a body at higher potential to the body at lower potential. A condenser of higher capacity may be at higher potential. So the charge may flow from it to the condenser of lower capacity, which may be at lower potential. Therefore, assertion and reason are wrong.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An early model for an atom considered it to have a positively charged point nucleus of charge $Z e$, surrounded by a uniform density of negative charge upto a radius $R$. The atom as a whole is neutral. The electric field at a distance $r$ from the nucleus is $(r < R)$
questions.solution:
Charge on nucleus $=+Z e$ Total negative charge $=-\mathrm{Ze}$ ( $\because$ atom is electrically neutral) Negative charge density, $\rho=\frac{\text { charge }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{-Z e}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3}$
Consider a Gaussian surface with radius $r$. By Gauss's theorem,
Charge enclosed by Gaussian surface, $q=Z e+\frac{4 \pi r^3}{3} \rho=Z e-Z e \frac{r^3}{R^3}$ (Using (i)) From (ii), $E(r)=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}=\frac{\mathrm{Z} e-\mathrm{Z} e \frac{r^3}{R^3}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}=\frac{Z e}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{1}{r^2}-\frac{r}{R^3}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a certain region of space, electric field is along the $z$-direction throughout. The magnitude of electric field is however not constant, but increases uniformly along the positive $z$-direction at the rate of $10^5 \mathrm{~N} \mathrm{C}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$. The force experienced by the system having a total dipole moment equal to $10^{-7} \mathrm{C} \mathrm{m}$ in the negative $z$-direction is
questions.solution: Consider an electric dipole with $-q$ charge at $A$ and $+q$ charge at $B$, placed along $z$-axis, such that its dipole moment is in negative $z$ direction. i.e., $p_{\mathrm{z}}=-10^{-7} \mathrm{Cm}$ The electric field is along positive direction of $z$-axis, such that $\frac{d E}{d z}=10^5 \mathrm{NC}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$ From, $F=q d E$ $=(q \times d z) \times \frac{d E}{d z}=p \frac{d E}{d z}$ Force experienced by the system in the negative $z$-direction, $F=-p \times\left(-\frac{d E}{d z}\right)=10^{-7} \times\left(-10^5\right)=-10^{-2} \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge particle is moving along electric field as shown in figure. What is horizontal displacement of the charge particle when it descends a distance of $y$ meter? Given $Q / m=9.6 \times 10^7 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{kg}, E=5 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$, $y=84 \mathrm{~cm}, g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
questions.solution:
Suppose particle falls down a distance $y$ in time $t$ so
$y=\frac{1}{2} g t^2 ; t=\sqrt{\frac{2 y}{g}}$
Now for $x$-axis
$\begin{aligned} & x=\frac{1}{2} a_x t^2=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{Q E}{m} \cdot \frac{2 y}{g} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{9.6 \times 10^7 \times 5 \times 10^5 \times 2 \times 84 \times 10^{-2}}{10} \\ & =403.2 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~m}=4.03 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

If both assertion and reason are false.

Reason : Electric field will induce dipole moment opposite to field direction.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Dipole moment will be in the same direction as the external field. The collective effect of dipole moments produces a field that opposes the external field and hence, the net electric field inside the dielectric is less than the external electric field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three identical charges are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle. The force experienced by each charge, (if $k=1 / 4 \pi \varepsilon_0$ ) is
questions.solution: Force on charge $q$ at $A$ : (i) Due to $B, f=k q \times \frac{q}{r^2}$ or $f=\frac{k q^2}{r^2}$ along $B A$ (ii) Due to $C$, $f=\frac{k q^2}{r^2}$ along $C A$ Resultant force $=F$ $\begin{aligned} & F^2=f^2+f^2+2 f \times f \times \cos 60^{\circ} \\ & F^2=2 f^2+\frac{2 f^2 \times 1}{2}=3 f^2 \text { or } \quad F=\sqrt{3} f \\ & \text { or Resultant force }=\sqrt{3} \frac{\mathrm{kq}}{r^2}\end{aligned}$

options: at an angle tilted towards the diameter
at an angle tilted away from the diameter
questions.solution: When the point is on the diameter and away from the centre of hemisphere which is charged uniformly and positively, the component of electric field intensity parallel to the diameter cancel out. So the electric field is perpendicular to the diameter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two charged spheres separated by a distance ' $d$ ' exert some force on each other. If they are immersed in a liquid of dielectric constant 2 , then what is the force exerted, if all other conditions are same?
questions.solution:
Given: Distance between two charges $=d$ and dielectric constant $(k)=2$. Force between two charged spheres $(F)=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_2}$, where $\varepsilon_0$ is permitivity of free space. Force for a medium with dielectric constant $k$,
$F^{\prime}=\frac{1}{k} \times \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}=\frac{F}{k}=\frac{F}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A hexagon of side $8 \mathrm{~cm}$ has a charge $4 \mu \mathrm{C}$ at each of its vertices. The potential at the centre of the hexagon is
questions.solution: As shown in the figure, $O$ is the centre of hexagon $A B C D E F$ of each side $8 \mathrm{~cm}$. As it is a regular hexagon $O A B, O B C$, etc are equilateral triangles. $$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad O A=O B=O C= & O D=O E=O F=8 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & =8 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned} $$ The potential at $O$ is $$ \begin{aligned} V & =6 \times \frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} \\ & =\frac{6 \times 9 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{8 \times 10^{-2}}=2.7 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metallic solid sphere is placed in a uniform electric field. Which path, the lines of force follow as shown in figure?
questions.solution: When metallic solid sphere is placed in a uniform electric field, the electrons of the sphere will move against the direction of the electric field. Consequently, the left face of the sphere acquires negative charge while the right face attains positive charge. The field lines will terminate at the left face of sphere and restart from its right face. The electric field inside the sphere is zero. On the surface of the sphere, the field lines are normal at every point i.e., directed towards the centre. Therefore, the correct field line is represented by line 4 .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion: If an electron and proton possessing same kinetic energy enter an electric field in a perpendicular direction, the path of the electron is more curved than that of the proton.
Reason: Electron forms a larger curve due to its small mass.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
As $q E=\frac{m v^2}{r}, r=\frac{m v^2}{q E}=\frac{2 K}{q E} \quad\left(\right.$ where $\left.K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2\right)$
i.e., $r \propto \frac{1}{q}$.
Since electron and proton have equal charge, $r$ is the same for both of them.
Here, $r$ depends on $K$ (which is given to be the same for both the particles) and as such not on their masses.

Since, $\vec{v}=v_0 \hat{i}$
$\vec{E}=E_0 \hat{j}$
The electron is moving in $x$-direction perpendicular to the electric field which is in $y$-direction.
Thus, the path of electron moving perpendicular to field will be a parabola.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniformly charged non conducting disc with surface charge density $10 \mathrm{nC} / \mathrm{m}^2$ having radius $3 \mathrm{~cm}$. Then find the magnitude of electric field at a point on the perpendicular bisector at a distance $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ from origin of the disc.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } E=\frac{\sigma}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}(2 \pi)\left[1-\frac{x}{\sqrt{R^2+x^2}}\right] \\ & =9 \times 10^9 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 6.28\left[1-\frac{2}{\sqrt{4+9}}\right] \\ & \therefore E=90 \times 6.28\left[1-\frac{2}{\sqrt{13}}\right]=251 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A large solid sphere with uniformly distributed positive charge has a smooth narrow tunnel through its centre. A small particle with negative charge, initially at rest far from the sphere, approaches it along the line of the tunnel, reaches its surface with a speed $v$, and passes through the tunnel. Its speed at the centre of the sphere will be
questions.solution:
Potential at infinity $=V_{\infty}=0$
Potential at the surface of the sphere, $V_s=k \frac{Q}{R}$
Potential at the centre of the sphere, $V_c=\frac{3}{2} k \frac{Q}{R}$
Let $m$ and $-q$ be the mass and the charge of the particle respectively.
Let $v_0=$ speed of the particle at the centre of the sphere.
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} m v^2=-q\left[V_{\infty}-V_s\right]=q k \frac{Q}{R} \\ & \frac{1}{2} m v_0^2=-q\left[V_{\infty}-V_c\right]=q \cdot \frac{3}{2} k \frac{Q}{R}\end{aligned}$
Dividing eqn. (ii) by eqn. (i),
$\frac{v_0^2}{v^2}=\frac{3}{2}=1.5$ or $\quad v_0=\sqrt{1.5} v$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electric field at the centre of a uniformly charged semicircle of radius \(a\) is
questions.solution: Electric field intensity at $O$ due to small elemental length $d l$ of charged ring, $\begin{aligned} & d E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\lambda d l}{a^2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\lambda a d \theta}{a^2} \\ & d E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{a} d \theta\end{aligned}$ $\therefore$ Net electric field at centre $O$ is $\begin{aligned} E=\int d E \sin \theta & =\int_0^\pi \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\lambda}{a} \sin \theta d \theta \\ & =\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 a}[-\cos \theta]_0^\pi \\ \therefore \quad E & =\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 a} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge \(q\) is uniformly distributed on a ring of radius \(r\). A sphere of an equal radius is constructed with its centre lying on the periphery of the ring. The flux of electric field through the surface of the sphere will be
questions.solution: Charge on ring $q$, centre of ring $=O$ Centre of sphere $=O^{\prime}$ Linear charge density of ring, $\lambda=\frac{q}{2 \pi a}$ Charge on arc $A B$ of ring, $\begin{aligned} & q_{A B}=\lambda(\operatorname{arc} A B)=\frac{1}{2 \pi a} \cdot q \cdot \frac{2 \pi a}{3} \\ & q_{A B}=q / 3\end{aligned}$ i.e., charge enclosed by sphere $=q / 3$ $\therefore$ Flux coming out of sphere $=q / 3 \varepsilon_0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three charges are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side $a$ as shown in the given figure. The force experienced by the charge placed at the vertex $A$ in a direction normal to $B C$ is
questions.solution:
The force experienced by $A$ due to $B$ is $F_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q Q}{a^2}$ along $\overrightarrow{A B}$ (attractive) The force experienced by $A$ due to $C$ is $F_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q^2}{a^2}$ along $\overrightarrow{C A}$ produced (repulsive)
Now the force $\vec{F}_1$ is having a component $F_1 \cos 30^{\circ}$ along and the force $\vec{F}_2$ is having a component $F_2 \cos 30^{\circ}$ along $\overrightarrow{A E}, \overrightarrow{A E}$ and $\overrightarrow{A F}$ are both normal to $\overrightarrow{B C}$ but they are mutually opposite to each other. And as $\left|\vec{F}_1\right|=\left|\vec{F}_2\right|$, so the forces along $\overrightarrow{A E}$ and $\overrightarrow{A F}$ will both cancel each other. And so the force experienced by the charge at $A$ in the direction normal to $\overrightarrow{B C}$ is zero. $$ \begin{aligned}

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Figure shown is a distribution of charges. The flux of electric field due to these charges through the surface $S$ is
questions.solution:
According to Gauss's law, the total electric flux through a closed surface is given by
$$
\phi=\oint_s \vec{E} \cdot d \vec{s}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}
$$
where $Q$ is the total charge enclosed by the surface. The total charge enclosed by the given surface is $Q=+q-q=0 . \quad \therefore$ The total flux $\phi=0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In figure, a particle having mass $m=5 \mathrm{~g}$ and charge $q^{\prime}=2 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}$ starts from rest at point $a$ and moves in a straight line to point $b$. What is its speed $v$ at point $b$ ?
questions.solution: According to conservation of energy, we get $K_a+U_a=K_b+U_b$ Here, $K_a=0$ and the potential energies are $U_a=q^{\prime} V_a$ and $U_b=q^{\prime} V_b$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore 0+q^{\prime} V_a=\frac{1}{2} m v^2+q^{\prime} V_b \\ & \text { or } v=\sqrt{\frac{2 q^{\prime}\left(V_a-V_b\right)}{m}} \\ & V_a=\left(9.0 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{array}{r} \left(\frac{3 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}}{0.01 \mathrm{~m}}+\frac{-3 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}}{0.02 \mathrm{~m}}\right)=1350 \mathrm{~V} \\ V_b=\left(9.0 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-2}\right) \\ \left(\frac{3 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}}{0.02 \mathrm{~m}}+\frac{-3 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}}{0.01 \mathrm{~m}}\right)=-1350 \mathrm{~V} . \end{array} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \therefore & v=\sqrt{\frac{2\left(2 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}\right)(2700 \mathrm{~V})}{5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kg}}}=4.65 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\ & =4.65 \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$

questions.solution: Since the potential at each point of an equipotential surface is the same, the potential does not change while we move a unit positive charge from one point to another. Therefore work done in the process is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron having charge $e$ and mass $m$ is moving in a uniform electric field $E$. Its acceleration will be
questions.solution:
Charge on electron $=e$
Mass of electron $=m$ and intensity of uniform electric field $=E$.
Acceleration of the electron,
$$
\begin{aligned}
a & =\frac{\text { Force on the electron }}{\text { Mass of electron }} \\
& =\frac{\text { Electric charge } \times \text { electric field }}{\text { Mass of electron }}=\frac{e E}{m} .
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.solution: Since the density of the electric lines of force at $A$ is more than that at $B$, the electric field at $A, E_A>E_B$ (elecric field at $B$ ).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire of length $l$ and mass $m$ is bent in the form of a semicircle. The gravitational field intensity at the centre of semicircle is
questions.solution:
Consider two small elements each of length $d l=r d \theta$ symmetrically. $\pi r=l$
Resolve the elemental field intensities $\because\left|d E_1\right|=\left|d E_2\right|$ $d E=2 d E_1 \cos \theta=\frac{2 G r d \theta}{r^2} \cos \theta$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{G}=\text { Gravitational constant } \\ & \lambda=\text { Linear massdensity }=\frac{m}{l}\end{aligned}$ or $d E=\frac{2 \lambda G}{r} \int_0^{90^{\circ}} \cos \theta d \theta=\frac{2 \lambda G}{r}=\frac{2 \pi G m}{l^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge is placed at the centre of cube of side $a$ then flux linked with one of its given faces will be
questions.solution: Gauss's law tells that the total flux through an area enclosing a charge $Q$ is $\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0}$. Now as the cube is having six faces and as we can assume a symmetrical distribution of fluxes among its faces, the flux associated with one of its faces is $\frac{Q}{6 \varepsilon_0}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In case of infinite long wire electric field is proportional to
questions.solution:
Electric field due to an infinite long wire of linear charge density \(\lambda\) is given by
\(E=\frac{\lambda}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two concentric conducting thin spherical shells $A$ and $B$ having radii $r_A$ and $r_B\left(r_B>r_A\right)$ are charged to $Q_A$ and $-Q_B\left(\left|Q_B\right|>\left|Q_A\right|\right)$. The electrical field along a line, (passing through the centre is)
questions.solution:
$\left|Q_B\right|>\left|Q_A\right|$ (given). Inside the shell $B$, the potential is a constant and negative.
But $V_A$ inside $r_A$ is positive and greater than that of $B$ because $r_A < r_B$. Upto $r_B$, one as hence positive potential. Beyond $r_A, V_A \propto(1 / r)$, this is $>V_B$, one gets the course $\propto 1 / r$. At $B$, the negative potential is more therefore the potential line is still negative. (c) explains all these factors.
Note: However the question asked is the field and the diagrams given are for the potential. This is an error.

$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the basic $\mathrm{CsCl}$ crystal structure, $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions are arranged in a bcc configuration as shown in the figure. The net electrostatic force exerted by the eight $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$ions on the $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions is
questions.solution: The electrostatic force due to one $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$ion is balanced by diagonally opposite other $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$. Thus the net electrostatic force on $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ion due to eight $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$ ions is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting sphere of radius $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ is charged with $10 \mu \mathrm{C}$. Another uncharged sphere of radius $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ is allowed to touch it for some time. After that if the spheres are separated, then surface density of charges on the spheres will be in the ratio of
questions.solution: Radius of conducting sphere $\left(R_1\right)=10 \mathrm{~cm}$ charge on the conducting sphere $=10 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and radius of uncharged sphere $\left(R_2\right)=20 \mathrm{~cm}$ For a spherical conductor, the density of charge is proportional to its radius. Therefore ratio of density of charges of the spheres will be $1: 2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The number 0 (zero) is required for
questions.solution: A computer is working on a binary system of calculations based on zero and 1 .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two mole of oxygen is mixed with eight mole of helium. The effective specific heat of the mixture at constant volume is
questions.solution:
For mixture of gases,
$C_V=\frac{n_1\left(C_V\right)_1+n_2\left(C_V\right)_2}{n_1+n_2}$
Here, $\left(C_V\right)_1=\frac{5}{2} R,\left(C_V\right)_2=\frac{3}{2} R$
$\therefore \quad C_V=\frac{2 \times \frac{5}{2} R+8 \times \frac{3}{2} R}{2+8}=\frac{17}{10} R=1.7 R$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: 3 mole of hydrogen is mixed with 1 mole of neon. The molar specific heat at constant pressure is
questions.solution:
For mixture, $C_V=\frac{n_1 C_{V_1}+n_2 C_{V_2}}{n_1+n_2}$
$=\frac{3 \times \frac{5}{2} R+1 \times \frac{3}{2} R}{3+1}=\frac{9 R}{4}$
$C_P=C_V+R=\frac{9 R}{4}+R=\frac{13 R}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A satellite is moving with a constant speed $v$ in a circular orbit about the earth. An object of mass $m$ is ejected from the satellite such that it just escapes from the gravitational pull of the earth. At the time of its ejection, the kinetic energy of the object is
questions.solution:
Escape speed,
$v_e=\sqrt{2} \times$ orbital speed $=\sqrt{2} v$
$\therefore \quad$ Kinetic energy of the object at the time of ejection $=\frac{1}{2} m v_e^2=\frac{1}{2} m(\sqrt{2} v)^2=m v^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Orbit of a satellite is within the gravitational field of earth whereas escaping is beyond the gravitational field of earth.
Reason : Orbital velocity of a satellite is greater than its escape velocity.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
The orbital velocity, if a satellite close to earth is $v_o=\sqrt{g R_e}$, while the escape velocity for a body thrown from the earth's surface is $v_e=\sqrt{2 g R_e}$
Thus $\frac{v_o}{v_e}=\sqrt{\frac{g R_e}{2 g R_e}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ or $v_e=\sqrt{2} v_o$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Time period of pendulum, on a satellite orbiting the earth, is
questions.solution:
On an artificial satellite orbiting the earth the acceleration is given by $\frac{G M}{R^2}$ towards the centre of the earth. Now for a body of mass $m$ on the satellite the graviational force due to earth is $\frac{G M m}{R^2}$ towards the centre of the earth. Let the reaction force on the surface of the satellite be $N$, then
$\frac{G M m}{R^2}-N=m\left(\frac{G M}{R^2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad N=0$
That is on the satellite there is a state of weightlessness or $g=0$
$\therefore \quad$ The time period of the simple pendulum,
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{l}{g}}=\infty$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A satellite is in an orbit around the earth. If its kinetic energy is doubled, then
options: it will fall on the earth
it will rotate with a great speed
it will escape out of earth’s gravitational field
questions.solution:
Kinetic energy, $K=\frac{1}{2} m v_0^2$
or $v_0=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}$
When kinetic energy is doubled, then
$v_0^{\prime}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2 K}{m}}=\sqrt{2} \sqrt{\frac{2 K}{m}}=\sqrt{2} v_0$
But $\sqrt{2} v_0=v_e$ (escape velocity)
Hence satellite will escape out of earth's gravitational field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Earth orbiting satellite has solar energy collecting panel with total area $5 \mathrm{~m}^2$. If solar radiations are perpendicular and completely absorbed, the average force associated with the radiation pressure is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \text  { As, power } & =\text { solar constant } \times \text { area } \\ & =\left(1.4 \times 10^3\right) \times 5 \mathrm{~W}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \text { Force } & =\frac{\text { solar constant } \times \text { area }}{\text { speed of light }} \\ & =\frac{1.4 \times 10^3 \times 5}{3 \times 10^8}=2.33 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Average distance of the earth from the sun is $L_1$. If one year of the earth $=D$ days, one year of another planet whose average distance from the sun is $L_2$ will be
questions.solution:
According to Kepler's third law, $T^2 \propto r^3$ or $T \propto r^{3 / 2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{D^{\prime}}{D}=\left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^{3 / 2} \quad$ or $D^{\prime}=D\left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^{3 / 2}$ days

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The additional kinetic energy to be provided to a satellite of mass $m$ revolving around a planet of mass $M$ to transfer it from a circular orbit of radius $R_1$ to another of radius $R_2\left(R_2>R_1\right)$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{As}-\frac{G M m}{2 R_1}+\mathrm{KE}=\frac{-G M m}{2 R_2} \\ & \mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} G M m\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Gravitational potential of the body of mass $m$ at a height $h$ from surface of earth of radius $R$ is (Take $g=$ acceleration due to gravity at earth's surface)
questions.solution:
If a point mass m is placed at a height
h from surface of earth, the potential energy is
$U_h=-\frac{G M m}{(R+h)}=\frac{-g R^2 m}{R\left(1+\frac{h}{R}\right)}=\frac{-g R^2 m}{R}\left(1+\frac{h}{R}\right)^{-1}$$\left(\because g=\frac{G M}{R^2}\right)$
$U_h=\frac{-g R^2 m(R-h)}{R^2}$
$=-g m(R-h)$
$\therefore \quad V=\frac{U_h}{m}=\frac{-g m(R-h)}{m}=-g(R-h)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $m$ is taken from the earth's surface to the height equal to twice the $\operatorname{radius}(R)$ of the earth. The change in potential energy of body will be
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assertion : Water kept in an open vessel will quickly evaporate on the surface of the moon. Reason : The temperature at the surface of the moon is much higher than the boiling point of water.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Since the escape velocity on the surface of the moon is much less than that on earth, so the water molecules get evaporated faster.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : The difference in the value of acceleration due to gravity at pole and equator is proportional to square of angular velocity of earth.
Reason : The value of acceleration due to gravity is minimum at the equator and maximum at the pole.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Acceleration due to gravity,
$$
g^{\prime}=g-R_e \omega^2 \cos ^2 \lambda
$$
At equator, $\lambda=0^{\circ}, \quad \therefore \cos 0^{\circ}=1$
$$
\therefore \mathrm{g}_e=\mathrm{g}-R_e \omega^2
$$
At poles, $\lambda=90^{\circ}, \quad \therefore \cos 90^{\circ}=0 \quad \therefore g_p=g$
Thus, $g_p-g_e=g-g+R_e\left(\omega^2=R_e\left(\omega^2\right.\right.$
Also, the value of $g$ is maximum at poles and minimum at equator.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose the gravitational force varies inversely as the \(n^{\text {th }}\) power of distance. Then the time period of a planet in circular orbit of radius \(R\) around the sun will be proportional to
questions.solution:
Gravitational force, $F \propto \frac{1}{r^n}$ $F=\frac{k}{r^n}$ where $k$ is a constant.
For a planet, moving in a circular orbit of radius $R, F=\frac{k}{R^n}$
But, $F=m \omega^2 R$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{k}{R^n}=m R \cdot\left(\frac{2 \pi}{T}\right)^2 \Rightarrow \frac{k}{R^{n+1}}=\frac{m(2 \pi)^2}{T^2} \\ & \Rightarrow T^2 \propto R^{n+1} \\ & \therefore T \propto R^{\frac{n+1}{2}} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find out the correct relation for the dependance of change in acceleration due to gravity on the angle at the latitude, due to rotation of earth
questions.solution:
Effective acceleration due to gravity due to rotation earth is given as
$\begin{aligned} & g^{\prime}=g-R \omega^2 \cos ^2 \phi \\ \therefore & \text { Change in acceleration, } d g=R \omega^2 \cos ^2 \phi \\ \therefore & d g \propto \cos ^2 \phi\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Height of geostationary satellite is
questions.solution:
The principle of the geostationary satellite is its period of rotation is the same as that of the earth.
\(\frac{-G M m}{r^2}+m \omega^2 r=0\)
i.e. \(\frac{G M}{(R+h)^2}=\left(\frac{2 \pi}{T}\right)^2(R+h)\)
\(h\) works out to \(36000 \mathrm{~km}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: According to Hubble's law, the redshift (Z) of a receding galaxy and its distance $r$ from earth are related as
questions.solution:
Hubble's law is a statement of a direct correlation between the distance (r) to a galaxy and its recessional velocity as determined by the red shift (Z). It is stated as $Z=H_0 r$, where $Z=$ recessional velocity
$H_0=$ Hubble constant and $r=$ distance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The velocity with which a projectile must be fired so that it escapes earth's gravitation does not depend on
questions.solution:
For a projectile to leave the gravitational field of the earth, its kinetic energy would be at least equal to its potential energy. That is
$$
\frac{1}{2} m v_e^2=G \frac{M m}{R} \Rightarrow v_e=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}}
$$
where $G$ is the universal gravitational constant, $M$ is the mass of the earth and $R$ is the radius of the earth. Hence the escape velocity of a projectile is independent of its own mass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : There are very small sporadic changes in the period of rotation of the earth.
Reason : Shifting of large air masses in the earth's atmosphere produce a change in the moment of inertia of the earth causing its period of rotation to change.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Normally when a body is moving in a straight line, whatever be the internal changes, the centre of mass continuous in its trajectory with the same velocity and a system that is rotating continues to rotate with the same angular velocity and changes occur in such a way that the angular momentum is conserved. Classical physics, taking the whole of the earth as a system does not allow sporadic changes in the speed of the earth.
However, according to the information on the earth's rotation by U.S. Naval observatory sporadic changes of the speed of rotation of the earth have been observed and this is attributed to the influence of the moon on the tides. This makes it necessary to take the earth and the moon (at least) as one system.
In the reason given, though normally when taking the earth as a single system, internal changes do not affect the motion, now that one has to take the earthmoon system, the reason that the earth's angular velocity can also change due to the events on earth alone cannot be ruled out. Then the total angular momentum of the earth-moon system will be conserved. The reason is also right, though according to the rules of the game for the $12^{\text {th }}$ class examinations, one can take only the earth as a whole as a system. In that case, both the assertion and reason are wrong.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The condition for a uniform spherical mass $m$ of radius $r$ to be a black hole is $[G=$ gravitational constant and $g=$ acceleration due to gravity]
questions.solution:
The criterion for a star to be black hole is
$$
\frac{G M}{c^2 R} \geq \frac{1}{2} \text { or, } \sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}} \geq c .
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The motion of planets in the solar system is an example of the conservation of
questions.solution: One deuteron consists of one proton and one neutron. As the mass of a proton and a neutron is approximately same $\left(\approx 1.67 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}\right)$, we assume that the mass of the neutron be $m$ and the mass of the deuteron be $2 m$ as the electron mass is negligibly small compared to that of proton and neutron. Let the initial velocity of the neutron be $u$. As the deuteron is initially at rest, the final velocity of the neutron is $$ v_1=\left(\frac{m-2 m}{m+2 m}\right) u=-\frac{m u}{3 m}=-\frac{u}{3} . $$ and the velocity of the deuteron is $$ v_2=\frac{2 m}{m+2 m} u=\frac{2}{3} u \text {. } $$ Total energy before collision is $E_1=\frac{1}{2} m u^2$ After collision, the energy gained by the deuteron is $$ E_d=\frac{1}{2} \times(2 m) v_2^2=m \times\left(\frac{2}{3} u\right)^2=\frac{4}{9} m u^2 . $$ $E_d$ is the amount of energy lost by the neutron. Therefore the fractional loss of energy of the neutron is $$ \frac{E_d}{E_1}=\frac{(4 / 9) m u^2}{(1 / 2) m u^2}=\frac{8}{9} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The difference in lengths of a mean solar day and a sidereal day is about
questions.solution: The sidereal day is about 4 min shorter than our normal solar 24-hour day, to be precise, the difference is $3 \mathrm{~min} 56$ seconds.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : The length of the day is slowly increasing.
Reason : The dominant effect causing a slowdown in the rotation of the earth is the gravitational pull of other planets in the solar system.
options: if both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
if both assertion and reason are true and the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
if assertion is true but reason is false
if both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution: Because of the gravitational pull of other planets in the solar system, the earth cannot get slowed down in rotation. Because, if it happens so, it will affect the conservation of angular momentum which is not possible.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The force of gravitation is
questions.solution: Earth behaves as a huge magnet and has a tendency to attract all the objects due to gravitational force. Therefore gravitational force is a conservative force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Kepler's second law is based on
questions.solution: Kepler's second law states that the line between a planet and the sun sweeps out equal areas in equal times. Kepler's second law is based on law of conservation of angular momentum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $v_0$ be the orbital velocity of a satellite in a circular orbit close to the earth's surface and $v_e$ is the escape velocity from the earth, then relation between the two is
questions.solution:
Orbital velocity of satellite $=v_0$ and escape velocity from the earth $=v_e$.
We have orbital velocity $\left(v_0\right)=\sqrt{g R}$
and escape velocity $\left(v_e\right)=\sqrt{2 g R}$
Therefore ratio of orbital velocity to escape velocity
$$
\frac{v_o}{v_e}=\sqrt{\frac{g R}{2 g R}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \text { or } \quad v_e=\sqrt{2} v_0 \text {. }
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The change in the gravitational potential energy when a body of mass $m$ is raised to a height $n R$ above the surface of the earth is (here $R$ is the radius of the earth)
questions.solution:
Gravitational potential energ
at any point at a distance $r$ from the centre of earth is
$U=-\frac{G M m}{r}$
At the surface of earth $r=R$,
$\therefore U_s=-\frac{G M m}{R}=-m g R \quad\left(\because g=\frac{G M}{R^2}\right)$
At the height $h=n R$ from the surface of earth $r=R+h=R+n R=R(1+n)$
$\therefore \quad U_h=-\frac{G M m}{R(1+n)}=-\frac{m g R}{(1+n)}$
Change in gravitational potential energy is
$\begin{aligned} \Delta U & =U_h-U_s=-\frac{m g R}{(1+n)}-(-m g R) \\ & =-\frac{m g R}{1+n}+m g R\end{aligned}$
$=m g R\left(1-\frac{1}{1+n}\right)=m g R\left(\frac{n}{1+n}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A satellite is revolving around the earth with a kinetic energy $E$. The minimum addition of kinetic energy needed to make it escape from its orbit is
questions.solution:
(d): Orbital velocity of satellite, $v_0=\sqrt{\frac{G M}{r}}$
Kinetic energy, $E=\frac{1}{2} m v_0^2=\frac{1}{2} \frac{G M m}{r}$
Kinetic energy for escaping,
$\begin{aligned} E^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2} m v_e^2 & =\frac{1}{2} m \frac{2 G M}{r}=\frac{G M m}{r} \\ & =2 E \quad\left(\because v_e=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{r}}\right)\end{aligned}$
Additional kinetic energy needed $=E^{\prime}-E=2 E-E=E$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Taking the radius of the earth to be $6400 \mathrm{~km}$, by what percentage will the acceleration due to gravity at a height of $100 \mathrm{~km}$ from the surface of the earth differ from that on the surface of the earth?
questions.solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at a height $h$ from the surface of the earth is
$g_h=\frac{g R^2}{(R+h)^2}$
where $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth and $R$ is the radius of the earth.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What will be the effect on the weight of a body placed on the surface of earth, if earth suddenly stops rotating?
questions.solution:
The value of $g$ at latitude $\lambda$ is $g^{\prime}=g-R \omega^2 \cos ^2 \lambda$
If earth stops rotating, $\omega=0 ; g^{\prime}=g$.
It means the weight of body will increase.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Kepler's second law can be understood by conservation of angular momentum principle.
Reason : Kepler's second law is related with areal velocity which can further be proved to be based on conservation of angular momentum as $(d A / d t)=\left(r^2 \omega\right) / 2$.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
According to Kepler's law, $\frac{d A}{d t}=$ constant As, $\frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{1}{2} r^2 \frac{d \theta}{d t}=\frac{1}{2} r^2 \omega$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{m r^2 \omega}{2 m}=\frac{L}{2 m}=\text { constant } \\ & \therefore L=\text { constant }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : The earth is slowing down and as a result the moon is coming nearer to it.
Reason : The angular momentum of the earth moon system is not conserved.
options: if both assertion and reason are true and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
if both assertion and reason are true and the reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
if assertion is true but reason is false
if both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution: One of the basic requirement of the existence of all planetary motions is the conservation of angular momentum. If this law is violated, then no gravitational law will be able to explain the behaviour of the planets and the satellites as they do.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Planet is a heavenly body revolving round the sun.
Reason : Star is luminous body made of gaseous material.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: A heavenly body revolving round the sun is called a planet and there are nine planets in our solar system. A heavenly body, made of gaseous material and luminous due to its own energy, is called a star. The sun is also a star and is nearest to earth.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The orbital velocity of an artificial satellite in a circular orbit above the earth's surface at a distance equal to radius of earth is $v$. For a satellite orbiting at an altitude half of earth's radius, orbital velocity is
questions.solution:
The satellite is moving at an attitude above the surface equal to the radius of the earth $R$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad \frac{m v_0^2}{2 R}=G \frac{M m}{4 R^2} \\
& \Rightarrow v_0=\sqrt{\frac{G M}{2 R}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now if the satellite moves at an attitude equal to $R / 2$ then,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{m v_0^{\prime 2}}{3 R / 2}=G \frac{M m}{9 \times(R / 2)^2} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad v_0^{\prime 2}=\frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{G M}{R} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad v_0^{\prime}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} \sqrt{\frac{G M}{R}}=\frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} v_0 \sqrt{2} \\
& =\frac{\sqrt{4}}{\sqrt{3}} v_0=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} v_0 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Escape velocity of a rocket is $11.2 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{sec}$. It is released at an angle of $45^{\circ}$. Its escape velocity is
questions.solution: The escape velocity is independent of the angle of projection. So, the escape velocity of the rocket remains same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : The time period of revolution of a satellite around a planet is directly proportional to the radius of the orbit of the satellite.
Reason : Artificial satellite do not follow Kepler's laws of planatary motion.
options: If both $A$ and and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
If Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.solution: Kepler's laws are the general rules for all planetary motions. Hence artificial satellites showed also follow the same laws.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Generally the path of a projectile from the earth is parabolic but it is elliptical for projectiles going to a very great height.
Reason : Up to ordinary height the projectile moves under a uniform gravitational force, but for great heights, projectile moves under a variable force.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Upto ordinary heights the change in the distance of a projectile from the centre of the earth is negligible compared to the radius of the earth. Hence projectile moves under a nearly uniform gravitational force and its path is parabolic. But for projectiles going to great heights, the gravitational force decreases in inverse poroportion to the square of the distance of the projectile from the centre of the earth. Under such a variable force the path of projectile is elliptical.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Hubble's law is related with
questions.solution: From Hubble law, speed of a galaxy (v) $=H r$ (where $H=$ Hubble constant and $r$ is its distance from earth). Thus Hubble's law is related with the speed of the galaxy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body weighed $250 \mathrm{~N}$ on the surface assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, how much would it weigh half way down to the centre of the earth?
questions.solution: At a depth $d$ below the surface of the earth, $$ \begin{aligned} & m g^{\prime}=G \frac{M^{\prime} m}{(R-d)^2} \\ & M^{\prime}=\frac{4}{3} \pi(R-d)^3 \rho \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad g^{\prime} & =\frac{G M^{\prime}}{(R-d)^2} \\ & =\frac{4}{3} G \pi(R-d) \rho \end{aligned} $$ If $\quad d=\frac{R}{2}$ then $$ g^{\prime}=\frac{4}{3} G \pi \rho=\frac{2 \pi G R \rho}{3} $$ On the surface of the earth $$ \begin{aligned} g & =\frac{G m}{R V}=\frac{4}{3} \pi G R \rho \\ \therefore \quad g^{\prime} & =\frac{g}{2} \end{aligned} $$ $\therefore$ The body weighed $250 \mathrm{~N}$ on the surface of the earth would weigh $\frac{1}{2} \times 250=125 \mathrm{~N}$, half way down towards the centre of the earth.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assertion : At pole value of acceleration due to gravity $(g)$ is greater than that of equator. Reason : Earth rotates on its axis in addition to revolving round the sun.
options: If both $A$ and and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
If both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
If Assertion is true but Reason is false.
If Assertion is false but Reason is true.
questions.solution: Because of the rotation of the earth, the value of acceleration due to gravity changes. Due to this rotation, the value of ' $g$ ' becomes minimum at the equator and maximum at the poles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Block $A$ of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is placed over block $B$ of mass $8 \mathrm{~kg}$. The combination is placed over a rough horizontal surface. Coefficient of friction between $B$ and the floor is 0.5 . Coefficient of friction between $A$ and $B$ is 0.4 . A horizontal force of $10 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied on block $B$. The force of friction between $A$ and $B$ is $\left(g=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}\right)$
questions.solution: Here, $m_A=2 \mathrm{~kg}, m_B=8 \mathrm{~kg}, \mu_1=0.4, \mu_2=0.5$, $F=10 \mathrm{~N}$ The frictional force between block $B$ and surface is $f=\mu_2 N=\mu_2\left(m_A+m_B\right) g=0.5 \times(2+8) \times 10=50 \mathrm{~N}$ As applied force $F(=10 \mathrm{~N}) < f(=50 \mathrm{~N})$, the system will not move. Hence, the force of friction between $A$ and $B$ is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Frictional forces are conservative forces.
Reason : Potential energy can be associated with frictional forces.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion: On a banked curved track, vertical component of normal reaction provides the necessary centripetal force.
Reason : Centripetal force is not always required for turning.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Centripetal force is provided by the horizontal component of normal reaction. Vertical component balances the weight of the vehicle. Centripetal force is required for turning. Hence, both, assertion and reason are false.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the diagram $100 \mathrm{~kg}$ block is hanging from a pulley and force $F$ is applied on the string to keep the system in equilibrium. If the whole system is moving up with a constant velocity $v$, then tension at point $P$ in the string will be
questions.solution:
Tension in each part of the string will be F. Assume tension at point $P$ is $T$.
$\begin{aligned} & T=3 F \\ & 2 F=m g \\ & 2 F=100 \times 9.8 \mathrm{~N} \\ & F=490 \mathrm{~N} \\ & \therefore \quad T=3 \times 490=1470 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : When a cyclist doubles his speed of turning, the chance of his skidding becomes nearly four times.
Reason: When the speed of the vehicle increases, the angle of bending also increases.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: For the banking of curve, $\tan \theta=\frac{v^2}{r g}$. When velocity $v$ is doubled, $\tan \theta$ becomes four times. With increase in $\theta$, the chance of overturning also increases. When $v$ increases, $\theta$ increases and consequently chance of skidding or overturning becomes greater.

Reason : The gravitational force on insect is balanced by force due to surface tension.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A cloth covers a table. Some dishes are kept on it. The cloth can be pulled out without dislodging the dishes from the table.
Reason : For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Cloth can be pulled out without dislodging the dishes from the table because of inertia. Reason is Newton's third law and hence true. But is not a correct explanation of assertion.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Pseudo force is an imaginary force which is recognised only by a non-inertial observer to explain the physical situation according to Newton's laws.
Reason : Pseudo force has no physical origin, i.e., it is not caused by one of the basic interactions in nature. It does not exist in the action-reaction pair.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Two bodies of masses $M$ and $m$ $(M>m)$ are allowed to fall from the same height if the air resistance for each be the same then both the bodies will reach the earth simultaneously.
Reason : For same air resistance, acceleration of both the bodies will be same.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
The force acting on the body of mass $M$ are its weight $M g$ acting vertically downwards $\therefore$ and air resistance $F$ acting vertically upward. $\therefore$ Acceleration of the body
$a=\frac{M g-F}{M}=g-\frac{F}{M}$
Now, $M>m$, therefore, the body with larger mass will have greater acceleration and it will reach the ground first.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $60 \mathrm{~kg}$ is suspended by means of three strings $P, Q$ and $R$ as shown in the figure is in equilibrium. The tension in the string $P$ is
questions.solution: $R \cos 60^{\circ}=Q=60 \mathrm{~g}^{60 \mathrm{~g}}$ $\begin{aligned} & R=\frac{60 \mathrm{~g}}{\cos 60^{\circ}}=120 \mathrm{~g} \\ & P=R \sin 60^{\circ}=120 \mathrm{~g} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=103.9 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A stone of mass $0.3 \mathrm{~kg}$ attached to a $1.5 \mathrm{~m}$ long string is whirled around in a horizontal circle at a speed of $6 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The tension in the string is
questions.solution:
Here, mass of the stone $m=0.3 \mathrm{~kg}$
Length of a string $=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$
Speed $v=6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$T=\frac{m v^2}{R}=\frac{(0.3)(6)^2}{1.5}=7.2 \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the system shown in figure. The pulley and the string are light and all the surfaces are frictionless. The tension in the string is (take $g=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$ )
questions.solution:
If $a$ is acceleration of $m_2$, then from fig.

From (i), $m_2 g-T=m_2 a$ $\begin{aligned} & 1 g-m_1 a=m_2 a \\ & 1 g=\left(m_1+m_2\right) a=(1+1) a \\ & a=\frac{g}{2}=\frac{10}{2}=5 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2} \\ & \therefore \quad T=m_1 a=1 \times 5=5 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Centripetal force is always required for motion in curved path.
Reason : On a banked curved track, vertical component of normal reaction provides the necessary centripetal force.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Ifboth assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: While taking a turn on banked road, the necessary centripetal force is provided by the horizontal component of normal reaction and vertical component balances the weight of the vehicle. Also roads are usually banked for average speed of vehicles passing over them. However, if the speed of a vehicle is somewhat less or more than this, the self adjusting static friction will operate between the tyres and the road.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The rear side of a truck is open and a box of mass $20 \mathrm{~kg}$ is placed on the truck $4 \mathrm{~m}$ away from the open end. If $\mu$ is 0.15 and $g$ is $10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$ and the truck starts from rest with an acceleration of $2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$ on a straight road, then the box will fall off the truck when it is at a distance of $x$ metre from the starting point. The value of $x$ is
questions.solution: $a=2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}, N=m g$ For the box's motion w.r.t. truck, $\begin{aligned} & m a-\mu N=m a_{B T} \Rightarrow(m a-\mu m g)=m a_{B T} \\ & \Rightarrow a_{B T}=a-\mu g\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & =2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}-(0.15)\left(10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}\right) \\ & =0.5 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & s_{B T}=u_{B T} \cdot t+\frac{1}{2} a_{B T} \cdot t^2 \\ & 4=0+\frac{1}{2}(0.5) t^2 \Rightarrow t^2=16 \\ & \Rightarrow t=4 \mathrm{~s} .\end{aligned}$ After $4 \mathrm{~s}$, the box will fall off the truck. During $4 \mathrm{~s}$, the truck travels, $x=\frac{1}{2} a t^2=\frac{1}{2}(2)(4)^2=16 \mathrm{~m}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A mass $M$ is tied to the top of two identical poles of height $H$ using massless strings of equal length. The mass is at height $h$ above the ground at equilibrium. If the distance between poles is $L$ the tension in each string will be
questions.solution:
At equilibrium, $M g=2 T \sin \theta$
$T=\frac{M g}{2 \sin \theta}$ Here, $\sin \theta=\frac{(H-h)}{\sqrt{(L / 2)^2+(H-h)^2}}$ From equation (i) $$ T=\frac{M g \sqrt{(L / 2)^2+(H-h)^2}}{2(H-h)} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cart has mass 2 metric tone and sand of 1 metric tone is inside the cart. Now a force of $10 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied on cart and sand starts to leak with rate of $0.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{s}$. What is the velocity of cart when total sand has come out from the cart?
questions.solution:
Mass of the cart with sand changes then its acceleration also changes.
$a=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{F}{m_0-\mu t}$
where $m$ is mass of system at time $t$,
$\begin{aligned} & m=m_0-\mu t \\ & a=\frac{F}{m_0-\mu t} \quad \text { or } \quad \frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{F}{m_0-\mu t}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \int_0^v d v=F \int_0^t \frac{d t}{m_0-\mu t} \\ & v=\frac{F}{\mu} \ln \left(\frac{m_0}{m_0-\mu t}\right)\end{aligned}$
Here $F=10 \mathrm{~N} ; \mu=0.5 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{s}$ $m_0=2$ metric tone $m_0-\mu t=1$ metric tone
$\therefore \quad v=\frac{10}{0.5} \ln (2)=20 \times 0.693=13.86 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A rocket moves forward by pushing the surrounding air backwards.
Reason : It derives the necessary thrust to move forward according to Newton's third la
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: A rocket moves forward taking the help of reaction force. For that it has to exert a force on the surrounding air so that it receives reaction force as per Newton's third law.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Same spring is attached with $2 \mathrm{~kg}, 3 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ blocks in three different cases as shown in figure. If $x_1, x_2, x_3$ be the extensions in the spring in the three cases, then
questions.solution: If $T_1, T_2, T_3$ are the tensions in the strings in the three cases, we have $\begin{aligned} & T_1=\frac{2 m_1 m_2 g}{m_1+m_2}=\frac{2 \times 2 \times 2 g}{(2+2)}=2 g \\ & T_2=\frac{2 \times 3 \times 2 g}{(3+2)}=2.4 g \\ & T_3=\frac{2 \times 1 \times 2 g}{(1+2)}=1.33 g \\ & \text { As } x \propto T \text { and } T_2>T_1>T_3 \\ & \therefore \quad x_2>x_1>x_3\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is placed on the floor. The coefficient of static friction is 0.4 . If a force of $2.8 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied on the block parallel to the floor, the force of friction between the block and the floor is $\left(g=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}\right)$
questions.solution:
$\text { As } f_{m s}=\mu_s m g=0.4 \times 2 \times 10=8 \mathrm{~N} \text {. }$
The applied force of $2.8 \mathrm{~N}$ is less than $f_{m s}(=8 \mathrm{~N})$ and as such the block does not move. When the block is at rest, frictional force $=$ applied force $=2.8 \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The figure shows a horizontal force $\vec{F}$ acting on a block of mass $\mathrm{M}$ on an inclined plane (angle $\theta$ ). What is the normal reaction $N$ on the block?
questions.solution: From the figure, the normal reaction on the block is $N=m g \cos \theta+F \sin \theta$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cyclist is moving in a circular track of radius $80 \mathrm{~m}$ with a velocity of $36 \mathrm{~km} \mathrm{~h}^{-1}$. He has to lean from the vertical approximately through an angle (Take $g=10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-2}$ )
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{r}\text { Here, } r=80 \mathrm{~m}, v=36 \mathrm{kmh}^{-1}=36 \times(5 / 18) \mathrm{ms}^{-1} \\ =10 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad \tan \theta=\frac{v^2}{r g}=\frac{(10)^2}{80 \times 10}=\frac{1}{8}$
or $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A body subject to three concurrent forces may be in equilibrium.
Reason: For equilibrium, the sum of all the concurrent forces acting at a point should be zero.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: A body subjected to three concurrent forces is in equilibrium if their resultant is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following graph depicts spring constant $k$ versus length $l$ of the spring correctly?
questions.solution: Since $k \propto \frac{1}{l}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $5 \mathrm{~kg}$ moving with a speed of $1.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ on a horizontal smooth surface collides with a nearly weightless spring of force constant $k=5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. The maximum compression of the spring would be
questions.solution:
At maximum compression the whole kinetic energy of ball $=$ potential energy of spring.
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{1}{2} k x^2 \Rightarrow x=v \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}} \\ & x=1.5 \times \sqrt{\frac{5}{5}}=1.5 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
