questions.q: The direction of the angular velocity vector is along
options: the tangent to the circular path
questions.solution:
v \(=r \times \omega\).
\(v\) is the tangential. If \(r\) is in the \(X Y\) plane and \(v\) is also in this plane, \(\omega\) is in the \(Z\) direction. + or - is decided by whether the particle is rotating in the anticlockwise direction or clockwise direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : When the velocity of projection of a body is made \(n\) times, its time of flight becomes \(n\) times.
Reason : Range of projectile does not depend on the initial velocity of a body.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
As time of flight,
$T=\frac{2 u \sin \theta}{g} \quad \therefore T^{\prime}=\frac{2(n u) \sin \theta}{g}=n T$
But range
$R=\frac{u^2 \sin 2 \theta}{g} \therefore R^{\prime}=\frac{n^2 u^2 \sin 2 \theta}{g}=n^2 R$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In the figure, a ray of light is perpendicular to the face $A B$ of a glass prism $(\mu=1.52)$.
Find the value of $\theta$ so that the ray is totally reflected at face $A C$, if the prism is immersed in water $(\mu=1.33)$. Given $\sin 61^{\circ}=0.875$
questions.solution:
Given, $\mu_g=1.52, \mu_w=1.33, \theta=$ ? Apply Snell's law at point $D$,
$\begin{aligned} & \mu_g \sin i_c=\mu_w \sin 90^{\circ} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin i_c=\frac{\mu_w}{\mu_g}=\frac{1.33}{1.52}\end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow \quad i_c=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1.33}{1.52}\right)=61^{\circ}$ From figure, $\theta+i_c=90^{\circ}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \theta=90^{\circ}-61^{\circ}=29^{\circ} \approx 30^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The refractive index of the material of an equilateral prism is 1.6. The angle of minimum deviation due to the prism would be
questions.solution:
Here, $\mu=1.6, A=60^{\circ}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { As, } \mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_m}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)} \\ & \therefore \quad \sin \left(\frac{60^{\circ}+\delta_m}{2}\right)=1.6 \sin \left(\frac{60^{\circ}}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \left(\frac{60^{\circ}+\delta_m}{2}\right)=0.8 \Rightarrow \delta_m=46.3^{\circ}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spherical mirror forms an image of magnification 3. The object distance, if focal length of mirror is $24 \mathrm{~cm}$, may be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Focal length of convex lens is $100 \mathrm{~cm}$ and focal length of concave lens is $8 \mathrm{~cm}$. Find the magnification of the given combination of lens if object is placed at point $O$.
questions.solution:
For first lens
$m_1=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{f}{u+f}=\frac{100}{-50+100}=2$
Image distance $v=2 u=2 \times(-50)=-100 \mathrm{~cm}$
For second lens
Now $u=-190 \mathrm{~cm}, f=-8 \mathrm{~cm}, m_2=$ ?
$m_2=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{f}{u+f}=\frac{-8}{-190-8}=0.04$
Net magnification $=m_1 m_2=0.08$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bi-convex lens is formed with two thin plano-convex lenses as shown in the figure. Refractive index $n$ of the first lens is 1.5 and that of the second lens is 1.2. Both the curved surfaces are of the same radius of curvature $R=14 \mathrm{~cm}$. For this bi-convex lens, for an object distance of $40 \mathrm{~cm}$, the image distance will be
questions.solution:
According to lens maker's formula
$\frac{1}{f}=(n-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)$
For the first lens : $n=1.5, R_1=+14 \mathrm{~cm}, R_2=\infty$
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{f_1}=(1.5-1)\left(\frac{1}{14}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right)=\frac{0.5}{14}$
For the second lens : $n=1.2, R_1=\infty, R_2=-14 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{f_2}=(1.2-1)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-14}\right)=\frac{0.2}{14}$
The focal length of the bi-convex lens is
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}=\frac{0.5}{14}+\frac{0.2}{14}=\frac{0.7}{14}=\frac{1}{20}$
According to thin lens formula; $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-40}=\frac{1}{20}$ or $v=40 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A double convex lens $(\mu=1.5)$ has focal length $10 \mathrm{~cm}$. When the lens is immersed in water $(\mu=4 / 3)$, its focal length becomes $40 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Reason : $\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{\mu_g-\mu_a}{\mu_a}\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)$
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
As $\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{\mu_g}{\mu_a}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)$
$\frac{1}{10}=\left(\frac{3}{2}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) \Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)=\frac{1}{5}$
If $f_w$ is the focal length of the lens when immersed in water,
$\frac{1}{f_w}=\left(\frac{\mu_g}{\mu_w}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)=\left(\frac{3 / 2}{4 / 3}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)=\frac{1}{40}$
Hence, $f_w=40 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The least distance of vision of a longsighted person is $60 \mathrm{~cm}$. By using a spectacle lens, this distance is reduced to $12 \mathrm{~cm}$. The power of the lens is
questions.solution:
Using, $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}$
Here, $v=-60 \mathrm{~cm}, u=-12 \mathrm{~cm}$.
$\begin{gathered}\therefore \quad \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{(-60)}-\frac{1}{(-12)}=\frac{-1}{60}+\frac{1}{12}=\frac{1}{15} \\ \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{15} \Rightarrow f=15 \mathrm{~cm}\end{gathered}$
Power, $P=\frac{100}{15}=\frac{20}{3} \mathrm{D}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light wave enters from medium 1 to medium 2. Its velocity in $2^{\text {nd }}$ medium is double from $1^{\text {st }}$. For total internal reflection the angle of incidence must be greater than
questions.solution:
Total internal reflection occurs when the angle of incidence is greater than critical angle (C) in the denser medium.
${ }^1 \mu_2=\frac{\text { Velocity of light in medium '1' }}{\text { Velocity of light in medium ' } 2}$ ' ${ }^1 \mu_2=\frac{v}{2 v}=\frac{1}{2}$. Medium ' 1 ' is denser. or $\sin C=\frac{1}{2}=\sin 30^{\circ}$ or $C=30^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a situation shown in figure, find the refrective index of glass so that it will suffer total internal reflection at the vertical surface.
questions.solution:
Clearly $r+i_c=90^{\circ}$ $\begin{aligned} & r=90-i_c \\ & \mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin \left(90-i_c\right)}\end{aligned}$
From eqns (i) and (ii) $\begin{aligned} & \sin i_c=\frac{2 \cos i_c}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow \tan i_c=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \\ \text { or } & i_c \approx 49^{\circ} 11^{\prime} \approx 50^{\circ} \\ \Rightarrow & \mu=\frac{1}{\sin i_c}=\frac{1}{\sin 50^{\circ}}=1.31\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $2.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ tall object is placed $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ in front of a concave mirror of focal length $10 \mathrm{~cm}$. What is the size and nature of the image?
questions.solution: According to new cartesian sign convention, Object distance, $u=-15 \mathrm{~cm}$ Focal length of the concave mirror, $f=-10 \mathrm{~cm}$ Height of the object, $h_O=2 \mathrm{~cm}$ According to mirror formula, $\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{-10}-\frac{1}{-15}=\frac{1}{-10}+\frac{1}{15}$ or $\quad v=-30 \mathrm{~cm}$. The image is formed at a distance of $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the mirror on the same side of the object. It is a real image. Magnification of the mirror, $m=\frac{-v}{u}=\frac{h_I}{h_O}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{-(-30)}{-15}=\frac{h_I}{2} \Rightarrow h_I=-4 \mathrm{~cm}$. Negative sign shows that image is inverted. The image is real, inverted, of size $4 \mathrm{~cm}$ at a distance of $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ in front of the mirror.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Electron microscope has more resolving power than optical microscope.
Reason : We can control the energy of electron.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Resolving Power, R.P. $\propto \frac{1}{\lambda}$. An electron microscope has extremely high resolving power because the electronic beams have wavelength $10^5$ times shorter than visible light.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transparent cube of $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ edge contains a small air bubble. Its apparent depth when viewed through one face is $6 \mathrm{~cm}$ and when viewed through opposite face is $4 \mathrm{~cm}$. The refractive index of material of cube is
questions.solution:
Here, real depth, $x=15 \mathrm{~cm}$ apparent depth, $y=6+4=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\mu=\frac{x}{y}=\frac{15}{10}=1.5$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : When an object is placed between two plane parallel mirrors, then all the images found are of equal intensity.
Reason : In case of plane parallel mirrors, only two images are possible.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: When an object is placed between two plane parallel mirrors, then infinite number of images are formed. Images are formed due to multiple reflections. At each reflection, a part of light energy is absorbed. Therefore, distant images get fainter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person uses $+1.5 \mathrm{D}$ glasses to have normal vision from $25 \mathrm{~cm}$ onwards. He uses a $+20 \mathrm{D}$ lens as a simple microscope to see an object. What is the maximum magnifying power if he uses the microscope without glasses.
questions.solution:
Let $d$ be the distance of near point of the person without glasses. Focal length of glasses,
$$
f_1=\frac{100}{P_1}=\frac{100}{1.5}=\frac{200}{3} \mathrm{~cm}
$$
Now $u=-25 \mathrm{~cm} ; v=$ ?
From lens formula, $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f_1}$
or $\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{3}{200}-\frac{1}{25}=-\frac{5}{200}$
$$
\therefore v=-\frac{200}{5}=-40 \mathrm{~cm}
$$
Therefore, the near point of the person is $40 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Focal length of microscope,
$$
\begin{gathered}
f_2=\frac{100}{P_2}=\frac{100}{20}=5 \mathrm{~cm} \\
\therefore M=1+\left|\frac{d}{f_2}\right|=1+\frac{40}{5}=9
\end{gathered}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : In optical fibre, the diameter of the core is kept small.
Reason : The smaller diameter of the core ensures that the fibres should have incident angle more than the critical angle required for total internal reflection.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Ifboth assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Diameter of a single mode optical fibre lies between 8 to $10.5 \mu \mathrm{m}$
Critical angle for total internal reflection does not depend on width of media.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : The resolving power of a telescope is more if the diameter of the objective lens is more.
Reason : Objective lens of large diameter collects more light.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Ifboth assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Resolving power of telescope
$$
R=\frac{1}{\Delta \theta}=\frac{a}{1.22 \lambda}
$$
Here $a$ is the diameter of the objective of the telescope.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : The frequencies of incident, reflected and refracted beam of monochromatic light passing from one medium to another are the same.
Reason : The incident, the reflected and refracted rays are coplanar.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Ifboth assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Frequency of light depends on the source of its generation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The length of deviation for a glass prism is equal to its refracting angle. The refractive index of glass is 1.5. Then the angle of prism is
questions.solution:
Here, $\delta_m=A, \mu=1.5$
$\therefore \mu=\frac{\sin \frac{\left(A+\delta_m\right)}{2}}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}=\frac{\sin \frac{(A+A)}{2}}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}$
$=\frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{A}{2}\right)}$
$\frac{3}{2}=2 \cos \frac{A}{2} \quad\left(\because \mu=1.5=\frac{3}{2}\right)$
$\cos \frac{A}{2}=\frac{3}{4}$
$\frac{A}{2}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)$ or $A=2 \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion: The edges of the images of white object formed by a concave mirror on the screen appear white.
Reason: Concave mirror does not suffer from chromatic aberration.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The rays of all colours incident on a concave mirror at the same angle are reflected at the same angle. As there is no chromatic aberration, the reflected image is white.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion: A secondary rainbow have inverted colours than the primary rainbow.
Reason : The secondary rainbow is formed by single total internal reflection.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Ifboth assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
In the formation of secondary rainbow, light suffers two total internal reflections instead of one. In this, ray undergoes two internal partial reflections having a maximum deviation of about $50^{\circ}$ for red and $54^{\circ}$ for violet. Also secondary rainbow will sometimes be formed with inverted colours. Secondary rainbow is fainter than the primary for two reason 
(i) the light has undergone two internal reflections and has therebybecomes weakened,
(ii) there is greater angular dispersion in this rainbow than in the primary.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A telescope, whose objective lens has an aperture of $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ for the wavelength of light $500 Å$, then limiting resolving power of the telescope is
questions.solution:
Given:
Aperture of objective lens $(a)=1 \mathrm{~mm}=1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$ Wavelength of light $(\lambda)=500 Å=500 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$ Limiting resolving power of telescope,
$d \theta=\frac{1.22 \lambda}{a}=\frac{1.22 \times 500 \times 10^{-10}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}=6.1 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{rad}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The frequency of a light wave in a material is $2 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}$ and wavelength is $5000 Å$. The refractive index of material will be
questions.solution:
Using, $\mu=\frac{\mathcal{C}}{v}$
Here, $c=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
$v=v \lambda=2 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz} \times 5000 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$
$=1 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
$\therefore \quad \mu=\frac{3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{1 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}=3$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : The sun rises some time before the actual sun-rise.
Reason : Because of the refraction through the different layers of atmosphere.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
This is because refraction through the
different layers of atmosphere.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Resolving power of a telescope depends only on wavelength.
Reason : This is proportional to square of wavelength.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assertion: A thick lens shows more chromatic aberration.Reason : Thick lens behave as many thin lenses.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Lenses have different refractive index for different wavelengths of light. The refractive index decreases with increasing wavelength. So red light will bend least and violet the most. This will result is slight dispersion which will be seen as chromatic aberration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a convex lens of focal length $F$, the minimum distance between an object and its real image must be
questions.solution:
Let $L$ is the distance between a real object and its real image formed by a convex lens, then as $L=(|u|+|v|)$ $=(\sqrt{u}-\sqrt{v})^2+2 \sqrt{u v}$
L will be minimum, when $(\sqrt{u}-\sqrt{v})^2=0$ $\text { i.e., } u=v$ Putting, $u=-u$ and $v=+u$ in lens formula, $\frac{(0}{v}-\frac{@}{u}=\frac{\text { @) }}{F}$ $\frac{(0}{u}-\frac{®}{-u}=\frac{\text { ๑) }}{F}$ $u=2 F$ $\therefore \quad(L)_{\min }=2 \sqrt{2-\times 2}=4$ $(U \operatorname{sing}(\mathrm{i}))$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Resolving power of reflecting microscope increases with
options: decrease in wavelength of incident light
increase in wavelength of incident light
increase in diameter of objective lens
questions.solution: $\mathrm{RP}$ of a reflecting microscope $=\frac{2 \mu \sin \theta}{\lambda}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A ray of light is incident from outside on a glass sphere surrounded by air. This ray may suffer total internal reflection at second interface.
Reason : If a ray of light goes from denser to rarer medium, it bends away from the normal.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: When a ray of light enters a spherical glass sphere, it is first refracted at first interface and then it strike the inner surface of sphere (second interface)and get totally internally reflected if the angle between the refracted ray and normal to the drop surface is greater than the critical angle ( $42^{\circ}$, in this case).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two lens of focal lengths $-20 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $+10 \mathrm{~cm}$ are put in combination, find the power of the combination.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A fish in water (refractive index $n$ ) looks at a bird vertically above in the air. If $y$ is the height of the bird and $x$ is the depth of the fish from the surface, then the distance of the bird as estimated by the fish is
questions.solution:
The distance of the surface of water for the fish $=x$.
From the surface, apparent distance of the bird is $n y$.
$\therefore \quad$ The total apparent distance $=x+n y$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person wear normal spectacles in which the distance between glasses and eyes is approximately $2 \mathrm{~cm}$, then power required is -5 D. If he wears contact lens, then the required power is
questions.solution:
Contact lens is more effective, so its required power is less.
For glasses: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{-x}-\frac{1}{\infty}=\frac{1}{f} \\ & f=-x \mathrm{~cm}=-\frac{x}{100} \mathrm{~m} \\ & \text { Power, } P=\frac{1}{f}=-\frac{100}{x}=-5 \Rightarrow x=20 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$ If he used contact lens then
$\begin{aligned} & u=-\infty, v=-22 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{-22}=\frac{1}{f} \\ & f=-22 \mathrm{~cm}, P=-\frac{100}{22} \mathrm{D}=-4.54 \mathrm{D}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If focal length of human eye is $2 \mathrm{~cm}$, then find the focal length of contact lens, such that a combined focus system of focal length $2.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ is obtained after using contact lens.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & (\mathbf{}): P_{\text {net }}=P_{\text {eye }}+P_{\text {lens }} \\ & \frac{1}{f_{\text {net }}}=\frac{1}{f_{\text {eye }}}+\frac{1}{f_{\text {lens }}} \\ & \frac{1}{2.5}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{f_{\text {lens }}} \text { or } f_{\text {lens }}=-10 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A far sighted person has his near point $50 \mathrm{~cm}$, find the power of lens he should use to see at $25 \mathrm{~cm}$, clearly.
questions.solution:
Here $u=-25 \mathrm{~cm}, v=-50 \mathrm{~cm}$
We have $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{-u}+\frac{1}{v}$
i.e, $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{25}-\frac{1}{50} \quad$ or $f=50 \mathrm{~cm}$
Power of lens he should use,
$$
P=\frac{100}{f}=\frac{100}{50}=+2 \mathrm{D}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Focal length of objective and eye piece of telescope are $200 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $4 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively. What is the length of telescope for normal adjustment?
questions.solution:
In normal adjustment,
$L=f_o+f_e=200 \mathrm{~cm}+4 \mathrm{~cm}=204 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion: When white light fall on the compact disc, multicolours are seen after reflection.
Reason: CD disc behaves like a prism.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The CD acts like a prism because the narrow grooves are so close to each other that you cannot see the separation. But, white light fall on the CD will be separated by multicolours by grooves.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The near point of distinct vision is at $1 \mathrm{~m}$ for an elderly person. What power of lens is to be used to bring the near point to the minimum distance of distinct vision $(25 \mathrm{~cm})$ ?
questions.solution:
Here, $u=-25 \mathrm{~cm} v=-1 \mathrm{~m}=-100 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{-100}+\frac{1}{25}=\frac{3}{100}$
or $f=\frac{100}{3} \mathrm{~cm}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~m}$
Therefore, required power,
$P=\frac{1}{f(\mathrm{~m})}=\frac{1}{1 / 3}$
$P=3 \mathrm{D}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assertion : The focal length of objective lens in telescope is much more than that of eye piece.Reason : Telescope has high resolving power due to large focal length.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
In telescope to have a large magnifying 
power, focal length of objective lens is kept large. To increase the magnifying power and resolving power, aperture of the objective lens is made large.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion: When a white light is passed through a lens, violet light is more refracted than red light.
Reason : Focal length for red light is greater than violet.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: When white light is passed through a lens, violet light is more refracted than red light because wavelength of violet is less than red light and therefore focal length for red light is greater than violet.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Microscope magnifies the image. 
Reason : Angular magnification for image is more than object in microscope.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Microscope is an optical instrument which forms a magnified image of a small nearby object and thus, increases the visual angle subtended by the image at the eye so that the object is seen to be bigger and distinct. Therefore angular magnification for image is more than object.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Magnification of a convex mirror is always positive, but that of a concave mirror may be both positive or negative.
Reason : It depends on the sign convention chosen.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The sign of magnification depend on the sign convention chosen. According to the new Cartesian sign conventions, magnification for a convex mirror is positive because image formed by a convex mirror is always virtual and erect. A concave mirror can form virtual or erect images and also real, inverted images. In the latter case, its magnification becomes negative. The reason though correct does not explain the assertion properly.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a person near point of vision is $100 \mathrm{~cm}$. Then the power of lens he must wear so as to have normal vision, should be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A total reflecting prism is used to erect the inverted image without deviation.
Reason : Rays of light incident parallel to base of prism emerge out as parallel rays.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Beam of light incident on face $A B$, suffers refraction from air to glass and strikes the face $A C$ suffering total internal reflection. The beam now strikes the face $B C$ of the prism at an angle of incidence less than the critical angle hence it suffers refraction from glass to air and bends away from the normal. The beam emerges out parallel to the base. As a result of refraction, on emergence the rays are inverted.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : The edges of the images of white object formed by a concave mirror on the screen appear white.
Reason : Concave mirror does not suffer chromatic aberration.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The rays or all colours incident on a concave mirror at the same angle are reflected at the same angle. Hence image formed by reflection does not suffer chromatic aberration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A convex lens of refractive index $\frac{3}{2}$ has a power of $2.5 \mathrm{D}$ in air. If it is placed in a liquid of refractive index 2 , then the new power of the lens is
questions.solution:
Focal length of a convex lens having power $2.5 \mathrm{D},=\frac{1}{2.5} \mathrm{~m}$ Also focal length of a lens in a medium of refractive index $\mu$ is given by $\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)$
Dividing the two, $2.5 \frac{0.5}{-0.25}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{f^{\prime}}=\frac{-5}{25 \times 0.25}=-1.25 \mathrm{D}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When white light passes through a prism, the deviation is maximum for
questions.solution: Since the wavelength of violet light is the smallest, therefore maximum deviation will occur for violet light.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A converging lens forms a real image \(I\) of an object on its principal axis. A rectangular slab of refractive index \(\mu\) and thickness \(x\) is introduced between \(I\) and the lens, \(I\) will move
options: away from the lens by \((\mu-1) x\)
away from the lens by \(\left(1-\frac{1}{\mu}\right) x\).
questions.solution:
Due to insertion of slab, the optical path increases by $x / \mu$, where $x$ is thickness of slab. Therefore the converging point will shift away by
$\left[x-\frac{x}{\mu}\right]=x\left(1-\frac{1}{\mu}\right)$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The camera lens has an aperture of \(f\) and the exposure time is \((1 / 60) \mathrm{s}\). What will be the new exposure time if the aperture become \(1.4 f\) ?
questions.solution:
Time of exposure \(\propto f^2\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{60} \times \frac{1}{1}=\frac{t}{1.4^2} \Rightarrow t=0.03266 \mathrm{~s} \\
& t=\frac{1}{31} \mathrm{~s} .
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : If objective and eye lenses of a microscope are interchanged then it can work as teles cope.
Reason : The objective lens of telescope has small focal length.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: We cannot interchange the objective and eye lenses of a microscope to make a telescope. The reason is that the focal length of lenses in microscope are very small, of the order of $\mathrm{mm}$ or a few $\mathrm{cm}$ and the difference $\left(f_0-f_e\right)$ is very small, while the telescope objective have a very large focal length as compared to eye lens of microscope.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The focal length of the objective and eye lenses of a microscope are \(1.6 \mathrm{~cm}\) and \(2.5 \mathrm{~cm}\) respectively. The distance between the two lenses is \(21.7 \mathrm{~cm}\). If the final image is formed at infinity. What is the linear magnification?
questions.solution: Here \(v_O=L-f_e=21.7 \mathrm{~cm}-2.5 \mathrm{~cm}=+19.2 \mathrm{~cm}\) \(+v e\) sign shows that image is real. For a objective lens \(\frac{1}{u_O}+\frac{1}{v_O}=\frac{1}{f_O}\) [using real-virtual sign convention] \(\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{1}{u_O}=\frac{1}{f_O}-\frac{1}{v_O} \quad \therefore \quad \frac{1}{u_O}=\frac{1}{(+1.6)}-\frac{1}{(+19.2)} \\ & \frac{1}{u_O}=\frac{19.2-1.6}{(1.6)(19.2)} \quad \text { or } \quad u_O=+1.745 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}\) +ve sign shows that object is real. When the image is formed at infinity, the magnification is given by \(m=\left(\frac{v_O}{u_O}\right)\left(\frac{D}{f_e}\right)\) where \(D\) is the distance of distinct vision. \(\therefore \quad m=\left(\frac{19.2 \mathrm{~cm}}{1.745 \mathrm{~cm}}\right)\left(\frac{25 \mathrm{~cm}}{2.5 \mathrm{~cm}}\right)=110 .\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion: A double convex lens \((\mu=1.5)\) has focal length \(10 \mathrm{~cm}\). When the lens is immersed in water \((\mu=4 / 3)\) its focal length becomes \(77 \mathrm{~cm}\).
Reason : \(\frac{1}{f}=\frac{\mu_g-\mu_w}{\mu_w}\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)\)
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } \frac{1}{f}=\left({ }^a \mu_g-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right] \\ & =\left(\frac{\mu_g}{\mu_w}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) \\ & \frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{\mu_g-\mu_w}{\mu_w}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right] \\ & =\left(\frac{1.5-1.33}{1.33}\right) \cdot\left[\frac{1}{20}+\frac{1}{20}\right] \\ & \frac{1}{f}=\frac{0.13 \times 2}{20} \Rightarrow f=\frac{20}{0.26} \\ & \therefore f=76.92 \mathrm{~cm} \text {. } \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : The colour of the green flower seen through red glass appears to be dark.
Reason : Red glass transmits only red light.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Green flower emit all colour except green and the emitted colours are dominated by red colour. So flower appear black.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Goggles have zero power.
Reason : Radius of curvature of both sides of lens is same
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The focal length is given by \(\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)\). For goggle lenses, both sides are curved the same way. \(R_1\) and \(R_2\) are positive. If they are the same, \(\frac{1}{f}=0\) i.e. power is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Optical fibres are used for telecommunication
Reason: Optical fibres are based on the phenomenon of total internal reflection.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Optical fibers are used in communication and it is because the phenomenon of total internal reflection, taking place inside the fiber for the signal incident. Both the statements are correct and the reason given is the right explanation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In refraction, light waves are bent on passing from one medium to the second medium, because in the second medium
options: the coefficient of elasticity is different
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire mesh consisting of very small squares is viewed at a distance of $8 \mathrm{~cm}$ through a magnifying converging lens of focal length $10 \mathrm{~cm}$, kept close to the eye. The magnification produced by the lens is
questions.solution:
$u=-8 \mathrm{~cm}, f=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
As, $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$,
$$
\therefore \quad \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{-8}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{8}=\frac{4-5}{40}=\frac{-1}{40}
$$
or, $v=-40 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Magnification produced by the lens, $m=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-40}{-8}=5$.
This is a virtual image, erect and on the same side as the object.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What should be the maximum acceptance angle at the air-core interface of an optical fibre if $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the refractive indices of the core and the cladding, respectively?
questions.solution: Core of acceptance angle $\theta=\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{{n_1}^2-n_2^2}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A lens is made of flint glass (refractive index $=1.5$ ). When the lens is immersed in a liquid of refractive index 1.25 , the focal length
questions.solution:
Let $f$ be focal length of lens.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{f}=\left({ }^a \mu_g-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)=(1.5-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) \\
& \text { or, } \frac{1}{f}=0.5\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) \text { or, } \frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}=\frac{1}{0.5 f} . .
\end{aligned}
$$
Let $f^{\prime}$ be focal length of the lens when immersed in a liquid.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{f^{\prime}}=\left({ }^L \mu_g-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)=\left(\frac{1.5}{1.25}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right) \\
& =\left(\frac{0.25}{1.25}\right)\left(\frac{1}{R_1}-\frac{1}{R_2}\right)=\frac{0.25}{1.25} \times \frac{1}{0.5 f} \text { (using (i)) } \\
& \frac{1}{f^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{2.5 f} \text { or, } f^{\prime}=2.5 f .
\end{aligned}
$$
i.e. focal length of lens when immersed in a liquid is increased by a factor of 2.5 .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A person standing on a rotating platform suddenly stretched his arms. The platform slows down.
Reason : This happens as angular momentum is conserved.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
On stretching arms, his moment of inertia (I) increases. As no torque has been applied, therefore $I \omega=$ constant.
As $I$ increases, $\omega$ decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A solid sphere is rolling on a rough horizontal surface. Acceleration of contact point is zero.
Reason : A solid sphere can roll on the smooth surface.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
During rolling motion, there is radial acceleration towards the centre. Hence, the contact point moves vertically upward.
On smooth surface, sphere will slide without rolling.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An elevator starts going up with an acceleration $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. If radius of the wheel attached to the elevator is $0.1 \mathrm{~m}$, then find out number of revolutions in $10 \mathrm{~s}$.
questions.solution:
$\theta=2 \pi n=\frac{1}{2}(\alpha) t^2$
$n=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{a}{r}\right) \frac{t^2}{2 \pi}$
Here, $a=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, r=0.1 \mathrm{~m}, t=10 \mathrm{~s}, n=$ ?
$\therefore n=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{2}{0.1}\right) \frac{10^2}{6.28} \approx 160$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Value of radius of gyration of a body depends on axis of rotation.
Reason : Radius of gyration is root mean square distance of particle of the body from the axis of rotation.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Radius of gyration of a body about a given axis is equal to $k=\frac{\sqrt{r_1^2+r_2^2+\ldots \ldots r_n^2}}{n}$. It thus depends upon shape and size of the body, position and configuration of the axis of rotation and also on distribution of mass of body w.r.t. the axis of rotation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Given $v_{C M}=2 \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, $m=2 \mathrm{~kg}, R=4 \mathrm{~m}$ Find the angular momentum of ring about its origin if it is in pure rolling.
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } \vec{L}=I_{C M} \cdot \omega+m v_{C M} R \\ & \omega=\frac{v}{R}=\frac{2}{4}=0.5 \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1} \\ & \vec{L}=m R^2 \cdot \omega+m v_{C M} \cdot R \\ & =2 \times 16 \times 0.5+2 \times 2 \times 4=16+16 \\ & \vec{L}=32 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : For a system of particles under central force field, the total angular momentum is conserved.
Reason : The torque acting on such a system is zero.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
When external torque acting on the system is zero then only the total angular momentum of system is considered to be conserved.
$\vec{\tau}=\frac{d \vec{L}}{d t}$
If $\vec{\tau}=0, \frac{d \vec{L}}{d t}=0 \Rightarrow \vec{L}=I \omega=$ constant

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A disc of radius $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ and mass $1 / 2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is rolling on an inclined plane. Find out the frictional force such that disc performs pure rolling.
questions.solution: Let $m$ be the mass and $R$ be the radius of the disc. $m g \sin \theta-f=m a_{c m}$ $\begin{aligned} & \tau_{c m}=I_{c m^\alpha} \\ & f R=\frac{m R^2}{2}\left(\frac{a_{c m}}{R}\right)\end{aligned}$ $f=\frac{m a_{c m}}{2}$ or $a_{c m}=\frac{2 f}{m}$ So, $m g \sin \theta-f=m\left(\frac{2 f}{m}\right)$ $f=\frac{m g \sin \theta}{3}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{10}{3} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{5}{3 \sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An air compressor is powered by a $200 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ electric motor using a V-belt drive. The motor pulley is $8 \mathrm{~cm}$ in radius, and the tension in the $\mathrm{V}$-belt is $135 \mathrm{~N}$ on one side and $45 \mathrm{~N}$ on the other. The power of the motor will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A judo fighter in order to throw his opponent on to the mat tries to initially bend his opponent and then rotate him around his hip.
Reason : As the mass of opponent is brought closer to the fighter's hip, the force required to throw the opponent is reduced.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
Ifboth assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Torque on the opponent by a judo fighter $\vec{\tau}=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}=r F \sin \theta \hat{n}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A child is standing with folded hands at the centre of a platform rotating about its central axis. The kinetic energy of the system is $K$. The child now stretches his arms so that the moment of inertia of the system doubled. The kinetic energy of the system now is
questions.solution: According to the principle of conservation of angular momentum, $I \omega=$ constant As $I$ is doubled, $\omega$ becomes half Now K.E. of rotation, $K=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ Since $I$ is doubled and $\omega$ is halved, K.E. will become half i.e. $\frac{K}{2}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle moves in a circular path with decreasing speed. Choose the correct statement.
options: Particle moves in a spiral path with decreasing radius
The direction of angular momentum remains constant
questions.solution: Angular momentum is an axial vector, so its direction is along the axis, perpendicular to the plane of motion which is not changing because of change of speed. Therefore, the direction of angular momentum remains the same and its magnitude may vary.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion: The velocity of a body at the bottom of an inclined plane of given height is more when it slides down the plane compared to when it is rolling down the same plane.
Reason: In rolling down, a body acquires both kinetic energy of translation and rotation.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: When a body slides, its entire PE is converted into KE of translation. But when it rolls down, some part of its PE is converted into its $\mathrm{KE}$ of rotation, thereby reducing its $\mathrm{KE}$ of translation and hence its linear velocity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Moment of inertia is always constant.
Reason : Angular moment is conserved that is why moment of inertia is constant.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: The moment of inertia is not a fixed quantity but depends on the orientation and position of the axis of rotation with respect to the body as a whole.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Given that force $(5 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}) \mathrm{N}$ acts on a particle at position $(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}) \mathrm{m}$. Find torque of this force on the particle about origin.
questions.solution:
Torque, $\vec{\tau}=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$
$=(\hat{i}+\hat{j}-\hat{k}) \times(5 \hat{i}+7 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k})$
$=\hat{i}(-3+7)-\hat{j}(-3+5)+\hat{k}(7-5)$
$\vec{\tau}=4 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $4 \mathrm{~kg}$ roller is attached to a massless spring of spring constant $k=100 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. It rolls without slipping along a frictionless horizontal road. The roller is displaced from its equilibrium position by $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ and then released. Its maximum speed will be
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid cylinder, a circular disc, a solid sphere and a hollow cylinder of the same radius are placed on an inclined plane. Which of the following will have maximum acceleration at the bottom of the plane?
questions.solution:
Let the inclination of the inclined plane be $\theta$. Acceleration of a rolling body is given as
$a=\frac{g \sin \theta}{\left(1+K^2 / R^2\right)}$ Here, $K$ is radius of gyration. For circular disc $I=\frac{1}{2} M R^2 \quad \therefore \frac{K^2}{R^2}=\frac{1}{2}$ $\therefore \quad a=\frac{2}{3} g \sin \theta$
For solid cylinder $I=\frac{1}{2} M R^2 \therefore \frac{K^2}{R^2}=\frac{1}{2}$ $\therefore \quad a=\frac{2}{3} g \sin \theta$
For solid sphere $I=\frac{2}{5} M R^2 \therefore \frac{K^2}{R^2}=\frac{2}{5}$ $\therefore \quad a=\frac{5}{7} g \sin \theta$
For hollow cylinder $I=M R^2 \quad \therefore \frac{K^2}{R^2}=1$ $\therefore a=\frac{1}{2} g \sin \theta$
Hence, from (i), (ii), (iii) and (iv) we conclude that the solid sphere will reach the bottom with maximum acceleration.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the moment of inertia for a solid sphere w.r.t. a tangent touching to its surface?
questions.solution: The moment of inertia for a solid sphere along its diameter is $$ I_{\text {diameter }}=\frac{2}{5} M R^2 $$ Moment of inertia about a tangent touching to its surface, $$ I_{\text {tangent }}=I_{\text {diameter }}+M R^2 $$ (using theorem of parallel axes) $$ =\frac{2}{5} M R^2+M R^2=\frac{7}{5} M R^2 $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A wheel moving down a perfectly frictionless inclined plane will undergo slipping (not rolling motion)
Reason : For perfect rolling motion, work done against friction is zero.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: Rolling occurs only on account of friction which is a tangential force capable of providing torque when the inclined plane is perfectly smooth, it will simply slip under the effect of its own weight. Once the perfect rolling begins, force of friction becomes zero. Hence, the work done against friction is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four holes of radius $R$ are cut from a thin square plate of side $4 R$ and mass $M$. The moment of inertia of the remaining portion about $z$-axis is
questions.solution:
If $M$ is mass of the square plate before cutting the holes, then mass of portion of each hole, $m=\frac{M}{16 R^2} \times \pi R^2=\frac{M \pi}{16}$
$\therefore \quad$ Moment of inertia of remaining portion about $Z$ axis
$$
\begin{aligned}
& I=I_{\text {square }}-4 I_{\text {hole }} \\
& =\frac{M}{12}\left(16 R^2+16 R^2\right)-4\left[\frac{m R^2}{2}+m(\sqrt{2} R)^2\right] \\
& =\frac{M}{12} \times 32 R^2-10 m R^2 \\
& =\frac{8}{3} M R^2-\frac{10}{16} M R^2 \pi \\
& I=\left(\frac{8}{3}-\frac{10 \pi}{16}\right) M R^2 .
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : A hollow shaft is found to be stronger than a solid shaft made of same material.
Reason : The torque required to produce a given twist in hollow cylinder is greater than that required to twist a solid cylinder of same size and material.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Side of an equilateral triangle is \(l\). Three point masses, each of magnitude \(m\), are placed at the three vertices of the triangle. Moment of inertia of this system about one side of the triangle as axis is given by
questions.solution:
Distance of corner mass from opposite side $=r$
$\begin{aligned} & r^2=l^2-\left(\frac{l}{2}\right)^2=\frac{3 l^2}{4} \\ & I=m r^2=\frac{3}{4} m l^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin wire of mass $M$ and length $L$ is bent to form a circular ring. The moment of inertia of this ring about its axis is
questions.solution:
A wire of length $L$ is bent to form a circular ring of radius $R$,
$\therefore \quad 2 \pi R=L$ or $R=\frac{L}{2 \pi}$
Moment of inertia of the circular ring about its axis is
$I=M R^2=\frac{1}{4 \pi^2} M L^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Torque on a body can be zero event if there is a net forces on it.
Reason : Torque and force on a body are always perpendicular.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A communication satellite of $500 \mathrm{~kg}$ revolves around the earth in a circular orbit of radius $4.0 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m}$ in the equatorial plane of the earth from west to east. The magnitude of angular momentum of the satellite is
questions.solution:
As the satellite is moving in equatorial plane with orbital radius $4 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m}$.
$\therefore \quad$ Satellite is geostationary satellite.
Hence, the time taken by satellite to complete its one revolution, $T=24 \mathrm{~h}=86400 \mathrm{~s}$
Velocity of satellite, $v=\frac{2 \pi r}{T}$
Angular momentum, $L=m v r$
$\begin{aligned} & L=m\left(\frac{2 \pi r}{T}\right) r=\frac{2 \pi m}{T} r^2 \\ & \therefore \quad L=\frac{2 \times 3.14 \times 500}{86400} \times\left(4 \times 10^7\right)^2 \\ & =0.58 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1} \\ & \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a solid sphere of mass \(1 \mathrm{~kg}\) and radius \(0.1 \mathrm{~m}\) rolls without slipping at a uniform velocity of \(1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\) along a straight line on a horizontal floor, the kinetic energy is
questions.solution:
\(\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E} \text { of rolling }=\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^2+\frac{1}{2} I \cdot \frac{v^2}{R^2}\right) \\
\Rightarrow \mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} m v^2+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{5} \cdot \frac{m R^2 \cdot v^2}{R^2} \\
\mathrm{~K} . \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \times \frac{7}{5} \\
m=1 \mathrm{~kg}, \quad v = 1 ~m/s \\
\mathrm{~K} \cdot \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 1 \times \frac{7}{5}=\frac{7}{10} \mathrm{~J}
\end{gathered}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The moment of inertia of a rod about an axis through its centre and perpendicular to it is $\frac{1}{12} M L^2$ (where $M$ is the mass and $L$, the length of the rod). The rod is bent in the middle so that the two halves make an angle of $60^{\circ}$. The moment of inertia of the bent rod about the same axis would be
questions.solution: Moment of inertia of uniform rod about one end $=\frac{M L^2}{3}$. $\therefore$ Moment of inertia of a rod about an axis passing through $O$ and perpendicular to the plane of the rod will be $$ =2\left(\frac{M}{2}\right) \frac{(L / 2)^2}{3}=\frac{M L^2}{4 \times 3}=\frac{M L^2}{12} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sphere of mass $10 \mathrm{~kg}$ and radius $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ rotates about a tangent. The moment of inertia of the sphere is
questions.solution:
M.I. about tangent $=I+M R^2$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{2}{5} M R^2+M R^2=\frac{7}{5} M R^2 \\ & =\frac{7}{5} \times(10) \times(0.5)^2=3.5 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The radius of gyration of a thin uniform circular disc (of radius $R$ ) about an axis passing through its centre and lying in its plane is
questions.solution:
Moment of inertia of the thin uniform circular disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$ about an axis passing through its centre and lying in its plane (i.e., about its diameter) is
From (i) and (ii), we get $k^2=\frac{R^2}{4} \quad$ or $\quad k=\frac{R}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assertion : A judo fighter in order to throw his opponent on to the mat tries to initially bend his opponent and then rotate him around his hip. Reason : As the mass of the opponent is brought closer to the fighter's hip, the force required to throw the opponent is reduced.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of the assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of the assertion
If assertion is true, but reason is false
Both assertion and reason are false statements.
questions.solution: Rotation of the opponent makes it difficult for the person rotated to resist. Any torque given by a force by pushing the person about an axis will be resisted but not the torque by rotation. By bringing him close to the hip, the opponents moment of inertia is reduced.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solid sphere is rolling on a frictionless surface, shown in figure with a translational velocity $v \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If it is to climb the inclined surface then $v$ should be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assertion : For a system of particles under central force field, the total angular momentum is conserved. Reason : The torque acting on such a system is zero.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
When external torque acting on the system is zero then only the total angular mom entum of system is considered to be conserved.
If $L=I \omega=$ constant , $\tau=I \alpha=0$.
$\therefore \quad \alpha=\frac{d \omega}{d t}=0$ for constant $\omega$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an orbital motion, the angular momentum vector is
questions.solution: In orbital motion, the angular momentum vector is perpendicular to the plane in which the rotational motion take place and its sense is given by the right hand fist rule. When the fingers of right hand fist point in the direction of motion, the thumb is in the direction of $\vec{L}$ (angular momentum).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The angular momentum of a moving body remains constant if
options: net external torque is not applied.
questions.solution: When the net external torque acting on a system is zero, the total angular momentum of the system remains constant. Therefore the angular momentum of a moving body remains constant if net external torque is not applied.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two masses are attached to a rod end to end. If torque is applied they rotate with angular acceleration $\alpha$. If their distances are doubled and same torque is applied, then they move with angular acceleration
questions.solution: Suppose the masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ are attached to the ends of a massless rod. $C_1$ is the axis of rotation which is at a distance $R$ from each of the masses. $\therefore$ the moment of inertia of the system, $$ \mathrm{I}=m_1 R^2+m_2 R^2=\left(m_1+m_2\right) R^2 $$ Now if the distance between the masses is doubled, then the moment of inertia $$ \Gamma=m_1(2 R)^2+m_2(2 R)^2=\left(m_1+m_2\right) 4 R^2 $$ Since the same torque is applied in both the cases, $$ \begin{aligned} & \tau=I \alpha=I^{\prime} \alpha^{\prime} \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha^{\prime}=\frac{I \alpha}{I^{\prime}}=\frac{\left(m_1+m_2\right) R^2 \alpha}{\left(m_1+m_2\right) 4 R^2}=\frac{\alpha}{4} \end{aligned} $$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the earth is treated as a sphere of radius $R$ and mass $M$, its angular momentum about the axis of its rotation with period $T$, is
questions.solution:
The moment of inertia $I$ of a solid sphere about the axis of rotation through the centre is $I=\frac{2}{5} M R^2$
$\therefore$ Angular momentum $L=I \omega=\frac{2}{5} M R^2 \times \frac{2 \pi}{T}$
$=\frac{4 \pi M R^2}{5 T}$ where $T=$ time period of rotation

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ mass is rotating on a circular path of radius $0.8 \mathrm{~m}$ with angular velocity of $44 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$. If radius of the path becomes $1 \mathrm{~m}$, then what will be the value of angular velocity?
questions.solution:
Mass $(m)=2 \mathrm{~kg}$; initial radius of the path
$\left(r_1\right)=0.8 \mathrm{~m}$; initial angular velocity
$\left(\omega_1\right)=44 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$ and final radius of the path $\left(r_2\right)=1 \mathrm{~m}$
Moment of inertia, $I_1=m r_1^2=2 \times(0.8)^2$ $=1.28 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^2$ and $I_2=m r_2^2=2 \times(1)^2=2 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{\textrm {m } ^ { 2 }}$
Therefore from the law of conservation of angular momentum $I_1 \omega_1=I_2 \omega_2$
or $\omega_2=\frac{I_1 \times \omega_1}{I_2}=\frac{1.28 \times 44}{2}$
$\omega_2=28.16 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is moment of inertia of a cylinder of radius $r$, along its height ?
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Angular speed of a planet around the sun increases, when it is closer to the sun.
Reason : Total angular momentum of the system remains constant.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
According to law of conservation of angular momentum, when sum of external torques acting on a system is zero, then the total angular momentum of the system remains constant. $\tau=0, L=I \omega=$ Constant
If $I$ increases, $\omega$ decreases and vice versa.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the diagram shown below all three rods are of equal length \(L\) and equal mass \(M\). The system is rotated such that \(\operatorname{rod} B\) is the axis. What is the moment of inertia of the system?
questions.solution: Since the width is negligible, the moment of inertia of \(B\) is zero along its length. \(I_A+I_C=\frac{m L^2}{12}+\frac{m L^2}{12}=\frac{m L^2}{6}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A horizontal platform is rotating with uniform angular velocity around the vertical axis passing through its centre. At some instant of time a viscous fluid of mass $m$ is dropped at the centre and is allowed to spread out and finally fall. The angular velocity during this period
questions.solution:
According to law of conservation of momentum,
$$
I \omega=\text { constant }
$$
When viscous fluid of mass $m$ is dropped and start spreading out then its moment of inertia increases and angular velocity decreases. But when it start falling then its moment of inertia again starts decreasing and angular velocity increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the dimension of luminous flux?
questions.solution: Dimension of Luminous flux is $\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-3}\right]$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the units of length, mass and force are chosen as fundamental units, the dimensions of time would be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { As } F=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right] \text { or } & {\left[\mathrm{T}^2\right]=\frac{[\mathrm{ML}]}{[\mathrm{F}]} } \\ \text { or }[\mathrm{T}] & =\left[\mathrm{M}^{1 / 2} \mathrm{~L}^{1 / 2} \mathrm{~F}^{-1 / 2}\right]\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Dimensional formula of angular momentum is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \text { Angular momentum }= & \text { moment of inertia } \\ & \times \text { angular velocity }\end{aligned}$
$=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2\right] \times\left[\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right]=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimensions of specific resistance are
questions.solution:
Specific resistance $\rho=\frac{R A}{l}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-3} \mathrm{~A}^{-2}\right][\mathrm{L}] \\ & =\left[\mathrm{ML}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-3} \mathrm{~A}^{-2}\right]\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : Power of a engine depends on mass, angular speed, torque and angular momentum, then the formula of power is not derived with the help of dimensional method. 
Reason : In mechanics, if a particular quantity depends on more than three quantities, then we can not derive the formula of the quantity by the help of dimensional method.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.
If assertion is true but reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the energy, $E=G^p h^q c^r$, where $G$ is the universal gravitational constant, $h$ is the Planck's constant and $c$ is the velocity of light, then the values of $p, q$ and $r$ are, respectively
questions.solution:
$E=G^p h^q c^r$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\mathrm{M}^1 \mathrm{~L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]^p\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]^q\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-1}\right]^r} \\
& =\mathrm{M}^{-p+q} \mathrm{~L}^{3 p+2 q+r} \mathrm{~T}^{-2 p-q-r}
\end{aligned}
$$
Applying principle of homogeneity of dimensions, we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
& -p+q=1 \\
& 3 p+2 q+r=2 \\
& -2 p-q-r=-2
\end{aligned}
$$
Add (iii) and (iv), $p+q=0$
Add (ii) and (v), we get $q=\frac{1}{2}$
From (ii), we get $p=q-1=\frac{1}{2}-1=-\frac{1}{2}$.
Put in (iii), we get $-\frac{3}{2}+1+r=2, \quad r=5 / 2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The only mechanical quantity which has negative dimension of mass is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  } F=\frac{G m_1 m_2}{r^2} \text { or } G=\frac{F r^2}{m_1 m_2} \\ & {[G]=\left[\frac{\mathrm{MLT}^{-2} \mathrm{~L}^2}{\mathrm{M}^2}\right] \text { or }[G]=\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimensional formula of Planck's constant is
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : The dimensional formula for product of resistance and conductance is same as for dielectric constant.
Reason: Both have dimensions of time constant.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
Both the quantities are dimensionless.
Resistance $\times$ conductance $=R \times 1 / R=\left[\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{~L}^0 \mathrm{~T}^0\right]=1$ and Dielectric constant $K$ is dimensionless.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Lumen is the unit of
questions.solution: Lumen is the S.I. unit of luminous flux.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an electrical circuit containing \(L, C\) and \(R\) which of the following does not denote the dimensions of frequency?
questions.solution: No Solution Available

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the dimensions of impedance ?
questions.solution:
Impedance has the same dimensions as that of a resistance
\(\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad \text { Resistance } R=\frac{\text { Voltage } V}{\text { current } I} \\
& \quad=\frac{\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-3} \mathrm{I}^{-1}\right]}{[\mathrm{I}]}=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-3} \mathrm{I}^{-2}\right]
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following physical quantities do not have same dimensions?
questions.solution:
Pressure and stress both have the dimensions of force/area. Strain and angle are both dimensionsless. Energy and work have the same dimensions force \(\times\) distance.
Tension and surface tension refer to two different physical quantities and their dimensions are different. Tension is a force and surface tension is force per unit length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnetic moment has dimensions of
questions.solution: The S.I. unit of magnetic moment is $\mathrm{Am}^2$. Therefore its dimensions is $\left[\mathrm{L}^2 \mathrm{~A}\right]$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: "Parsec" is the unit of
questions.solution: Parsec (pc) is an astronomical unit of length equal to the distance at which a baseline of one astronomical unit subtends an angle of one second of arc. 1 parsec $=3.085677 \times 10^{16} \mathrm{~m}=3.26$ light years.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Using mass $(M)$, length $(L)$, time $(T)$ and current $(A)$ as fundamental quantities, the dimension of permittivity is
questions.solution:
Force of attraction between two charges is given by
$$
\begin{aligned}
F & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_0 & =\frac{1}{4 \pi} \frac{1}{F} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}=\frac{\mathrm{A}^2 \mathrm{~T}^2}{\mathrm{MLT}^{-2} \mathrm{~L}^2} \\
& =\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^{-3} \mathrm{~T}^4 \mathrm{~A}^2\right] .
\end{aligned}
$$
(where $4 \pi$ is dimensionless)
$=\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^{-3} \mathrm{~T}^4 \mathrm{~A}^2\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Dimension of electrical resistance is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Using mass $(M)$, length $(L)$, time $(T)$ and current $(A)$ as fundamental quantities, the dimension of permeability is
questions.solution:
We know that the force per unit length of a wire carrying current due to another parallel wire carrying current is given by
$$
\begin{gathered}
\frac{d F}{d l}=\frac{\mu_0 i_1 i_2}{2 \pi d} \Rightarrow \mu_0=\frac{2 \pi d}{i_1 i_2} \frac{d F}{d l} \\
\therefore \quad\left[\mu_0\right]=\frac{[\mathrm{L}]}{\left[\mathrm{A}^2\right]} \cdot \frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]}{[\mathrm{L}]} \\
\text { or }\left[\mu_0\right]=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]\left[\mathrm{A}^{-2}\right]=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2} \mathrm{~A}^{-2}\right] .
\end{gathered}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Length cannot be measure by
questions.solution: Debye is the unit of electric dipole moment. Therefore length cannot be measured by debye.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The dimension of torque is
questions.solution:
Torque, $\tau=$ force $\times$ distance.
Therefore dimension of torque $=$ dimensions of force $\times$ dimensions of distance $=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right][\mathrm{L}]=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion : S.I. units are logical and coherent.
Reason : S.I. system of units is a rationalised system.
options: If both the assertion and reason are true and reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but assertion is not a correct explanation of the assertion.
If the assertion is true but the reason is false.
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution: A coherent system means a system based on a certain set of basic units from which all derived units are obtained by multiplication or division without introducing numerical factors. In S.I. system of units, heat energy, electric energy and mechanical energy are measured in joule (J). But in C.G.S. system, they have different units.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Dimension of resistivity is
questions.solution:
By definition
$$
\begin{aligned}
R & =\rho \frac{l}{A} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \rho & =\frac{R A}{l}=\frac{\frac{V}{I} A}{l}=\frac{V A}{I l} \\
\therefore \quad[\rho] & =\frac{[V][A]}{[I][l]} \\
& =\frac{\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2} \mathrm{~L}^2}{\mathrm{AL}^2}=\left[\mathrm{ML}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2} \mathrm{~A}^{-1}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What are the dimensions of $\frac{A}{B}$ in the relation $F=A \sqrt{x}+B t^2$, where $F$ is the force, $x$ is distance and $t$ is time ?
questions.solution:
Given, $F=A \sqrt{x}+B t^2$
Here, $F=$ force, $x=$ distance and $t=$ time
Dimensionally, $F=A \sqrt{x}=B t^2$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{A}{B}=\frac{t^2}{\sqrt{x}} \Rightarrow \frac{A}{B}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{T}^2\right]}{\left[\mathrm{L}^{1 / 2}\right]}=\left[\mathrm{L}^{-1 / 2} \mathrm{~T}^2\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose a quantity $y$ can be dimensionally represented in terms of $M, L$ and T, that is $[y]=\left[\mathrm{M}^a \mathrm{~L}^b \mathrm{~T}^c\right]$ then $\mathrm{M}$
options: may be represented in terms of $\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{T}$ and $y$ if $a=0$
may be represented in terms of $\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{T}$ and $y$ if $a \neq 0$
can always be dimensionally represented in terms of L, T and $y$
can never be dimensionally represented in terms of L, T and $y$.
questions.solution: $\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{L}^{-\frac{b}{a}} \mathrm{~T}^{-\frac{c}{a}} y^{\frac{1}{a}}$ if $a \neq 0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following pairs does not have same dimensions ?
options: moment of inertia and moment of force
questions.solution:
Impulse $=F \times t$
$$
=\frac{m\left(v_2-v_1\right)}{t} \times t=m\left(v_2-v_1\right)
$$
$=$ change in momentum
$\therefore \quad$ [Impulse $]=[$ Momentum $]$
Angular momentum, $\mathrm{L}=m v r$
Planck's constant, $[h]=[$ energy $] \times[$ time $]$
$$
\Rightarrow[F \times r \times \text { time })=\frac{m\left(v_2-v_1\right)}{t} \times r \times t
$$
$\Rightarrow m\left(v_2-v_1\right) \times r=($ change of momentum $) \times r$
$\therefore \quad[h]=[L]$.
Work, $W=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}:$ Torque, $\tau=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$
$$
\therefore \quad[W]=[\tau]
$$
Moment of inertia, $I=m r^2=\operatorname{mass} \times(\text { distance })^2$

Moment of force, $\tau=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}=$ distance $\times$ force
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \quad=\text { distance } \times \frac{\text { change of momentum }}{\text { time }} \\
& \therefore \quad[\mathrm{I}] \neq[\tau] .
\end{aligned}
$$
Therefore, moment of inertia and moment of force have different dimensions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following physical quantities do not have same dimensions?
questions.solution:
Pressure and stress both have the dimensions of Force/area. Strain and angle are both dimensionsless. Energy and work have the same dimensions force $\times$ distance.
Tension and surface tension refer to two different physical quantities and their dimensions are different. Tension is a force and surface tension is force per unit length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the dimensions of magnetic field $B$ in terms of $\mathrm{C}$ (= coulomb), M, L, T?
questions.solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { }: B=\frac{F}{q v}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]}{[\mathrm{AT}]\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-1}\right]}=\left[\mathrm{ML}^0 \mathrm{~T}^{-2} \mathrm{~A}^{-1}\right] \\
& \text { But }[A]=\left[C \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right] \\
& \therefore \quad[B]=\left[\mathrm{ML}^0 \mathrm{~T}^{-1} \mathrm{C}^{-1}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Assertion: In the relation \(f=\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}\), where symbols have standard meaning, \(\mu\) represent linear mass density.
Reason : The frequency has the dimensions of inverse of time.
options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion
If assertion is true but reason is false
If both assertion and reason are false.
questions.solution:
From, $f=\frac{1}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}, f^2=\frac{T}{4 l^2 \mu}$
or, $\begin{aligned} \mu & =\frac{T}{4 l^2 f^2}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]}{\mathrm{L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}}=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{L}} \\ & =\frac{\text { Mass }}{\text { length }}=\text { linear mass density. }\end{aligned}$

Specific gravity is former name of relative density and relative density is the density of a substance divided by the density of water.
$\therefore \quad$ Specific gravity $=$ relative density
$$
=\frac{\text { density of substance }}{\text { density of water }}=\text { dimensionless. }
$$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If potential energy is given by $U=\frac{a}{r^2}-\frac{b}{r}$. Then find out maximum force. (Given $a=2$, $b=4)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  } F=-\frac{d u}{d r}=-\left[-\frac{2 a}{r^3}+\frac{b}{r^2}\right]=\frac{2 a}{r^3}-\frac{b}{r^2} \\ & \frac{d F}{d r}=\frac{-6 a}{r^4}+\frac{2 b}{r^3}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{6 a}{r}=2 b \Rightarrow r=\frac{3 a}{b}=\frac{6}{4}=\frac{3}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow F_{\max }=\frac{2 \times 2}{(3 / 2)^3}-\frac{4}{(3 / 2)^2} \\ & =\frac{4 \times 8}{27}-\frac{4 \times 4}{9}=\frac{32-16 \times 3}{27}=-\frac{16}{27}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics

options: If both assertion and reason are true and reason is the correct explanation of assertion.
If both assertion and reason are true but reason is not the correct explanation of assertion.

questions.solution: Work done in the motion of a body over a closed loop is zero only when the body is moving under the action of conservative forces (like gravitational or electrostatic forces). It is not zero when the forces are non-conservative e.g. frictional forces etc.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: With what minimum speed $v$ must a small ball should be pushed inside a smooth vertical tube from a height $h$, so that it may reach the top of the tube? Radius of the tube is $R$. (Assume radius of cross-section of tube is negligible in comparison to $R$.)
questions.solution:
For minimum $v$, velocity of ball at the topmost point will be zero.
Byconservationof energy, $\frac{1}{2} m v^2=m g(2 R-h)$ $v=\sqrt{2 g(2 R-h)}$
